pages.text: Home | Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare | MoA & FW | Government of India, India कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE & FARMERS WELFARE भारत सरकार GOVERNMENT OF INDIA कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण मंत्रालय MINISTRY OF AGRICULTURE & FARMERS WELFARE Digital Agriculture Division Overview Digital Agriculture Mission Overview Digital Agriculture Mission aims to establish a comprehensive farmer-centric digital and space-tech ecosystem to enhance transparency and efficiency in the execution of government programs while providing crucial support for evidence-based policy making for farmers’ welfare. Key objectives of the Digital Agriculture Mission are highlighted as under: (a) To create a robust and holistic farmer-centric digital ecosystem in the country. (b) To improve transparency and efficiency in the implementation of government initiatives. (c) To support the government in making informed policy decisions. (d) To promote public and private innovation and partnerships, encouraging Agri techs in India’s digital economy. Key Components of Digital Agriculture Mission (a) Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) for Agriculture, i.e.Agristack, Krishi Decision Support System, Comprehensive Soil Fertility, and Profile Maps. (b) Digital General Crop Estimation Survey (DGCES) (c) Support to Mahalanobis National Crop Forecasting Centre (MNCFC) (d) Activities undertaken in erstwhile NeGP- A scheme (subsumed in the Mission) which includes Development & maintenance of IT Infrastructure/Application of DA&FW and Support to States, Academic / Research Institutions for IT initiatives. Program & Schemes 1. Agristack Agri Stack is envisaged as a digital public good like “Aadhar” for efficient and effective scheme/service delivery to the farmers. It will be built in a ‘federated architecture’ as a collaborative project between the various agencies of the Central and State Governments and the Union Territories. It consists of three foundational registries or databases in the agriculture sector, i.e.: o Farmers’ Registry: Farmer’s Registry is a dynamic, accurate, and verified database of farmers created and managed by the States/ Union Territories. It will provide comprehensive and useful data on farmers comprising authenticated demographic details, land holdings, family details, crops sown, soil health, livestock owned, fisheries assets, and other vocations. o Geo-referenced village maps: Geo-referenced Village Maps are required for conducting digital crop surveys. Geo-referenced village maps will enable trustful ground truth data collection and mapping of the data points at the land parcel level during the Digital Crop Survey exercise, geography-based crop advisory system, etc. Survey-level geo-referenced village cadastral maps will serve as a base for meeting the objectives of Agristack. o Crop Sown Registry through Digital Crop Survey: A Digital Crop Survey(DCS) System will be established to collect crop - sown details through a mobile interface, ensuring data is captured directly from the field.This database will provide accurate, real-time crop area information for every agricultural plot, aiming to replace traditional survey methods like Girdawari.The DCS system will also enhance remote sensing capabilities for seamless crop area mapping, reducing the need to conduct surveys in the future. 2. Krishi Decision Support System Krishi-DSS, launched on 16th August 2024, integrates and standardizes geospatial and non-geospatial data, including satellite, weather, soil, crop signatures, reservoir, groundwater data, and government scheme information. Krishi-DSS offers crop maps, soil maps, automated yield estimation models, drought/flood monitoring systems, etc., which support the government's evidence-based decision-making and facilitate innovative solutions by research institutions and the agritech industry. This Krishi-DSS platform is aligned with the government's National Geo-Spatial policy. The information available on Krishi-DSS would support crop map generation for identifying crop sown patterns and crop diversification, drought/flood monitoring, and technology/model-based yield assessment for settling crop insurance claims by farmers. 3. Nationwide Soil Resource Mapping project A Nationwide Soil Resource Mapping project has been initiated by the Soil and Land Use Survey of India (SLUSI), which is inventorying soils at a village level on a finer and more detailed 1:10,000 scale using high-resolution satellite and ground data. Under this project, a detailed soil profile study will be carried out to create standardized soil maps for rational land use and crop planning, thus promoting sustainable agriculture. 4. DGCES DGCES is designed to streamline and automate the entire GCES process, encompassing state-level planning, recording the results of CCEs conducted on the ﬁeld, and report generation. By digitizing and enhancing the eﬃciency of CCEs, DGCES seeks to provide States/ UTs with crop yield data that is not only accurate but also accessible in a timely manner. 5. IT Initiatives Support This includes following two components: (a) IT Initiative support to States and Other Organizations under the Mission (b) Support for IT-related activities of DA&FW, Government of India 6. Support to MNCFC The key activities of MNCFC to be covered under the Digital Agriculture Mission are enlisted as follows: (a) Crop acreage and production estimation of major crops under the scheme “Forecasting Agricultural output using Space, Agro-meteorology and Land based observations” (FASAL). It also undertakes Horticulture Crop assessments. (b) Support to Crop Weather Watch Group, drought monitoring and support to the States for their capacity building on geospatial technologies for agriculture. (c) MNCFC is also the technical partner for the effective implementation of PMFBY for area discrepancy analysis, smart sampling, and yield dispute resolution. IT Initiatives for States, Academic & Research Institutions Revised Guidelines (Consolidated) Revision of erstwhile National e-Governance Plan in Agriculture (NeGP-A) implementation approach. अपने संबंधित क्षेत्रों में केन्द्रीय क्षेत्र की योजनाओं के कार्यान्वयन के साथ समन्वय के लिए देश भर में फैले हुए हैं। और देखें नई पहल और देखें कृषि निवेश कृषि डीएसएस डीजीसीईएस कृषि मैपर उपज साथी मित्रवत पुस्तिका और देखें >> सोशल मीडिया Tweets by Agriculture INDIA Tweets by AgriGoI नया क्या है और देखें खरीफ अभियान 2026 के लिए कृषि पर राष्ट्रीय सम्मेलन खरीफ विपणन सीजन (केएमएस) 2026-27 के लिए खरीफ फसलों के न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एमएसपी)। वार्षिक रिपोर्ट 2025-2026 2026-27 मौसम के लिए कच्चे पटसन हेतु न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य चेतावनी: आधिकारिक वेबसाइट अस्वीकरण एवं जन परामर्श विपणन एवं निरीक्षण निदेशालय में तीन पदों की भर्ती – स्टेनोग्राफर ग्रेड-I, ग्रुप बी गैर-गजेटेड उप कार्यालय कैडर, जिन्हें डिप्यूटेशन पर भरा जाएगा (संक्षिप्त अवधि के अनुबंध सहित) – वेतन मैट्रिक्स के स्तर-6 में (सातवें CPC के अनुसार) {पूर्व-संशोधित वेतन बैंड-2 (₹9,300-34,800) के साथ ग्रेड पे ₹4,200}। -(अंग्रेजी) कीटनाशक प्रबंधन विधेयक, 2025 के मसौदे पर टिप्पणियाँ आमंत्रित की जाती हैं। -(अंग्रेजी) 2026 मौसम के लिए कोपरा के न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एमएसपी) गैर-रासायनिक उर्वरक अनुभाग, INM डिवीजन में दो युवा पेशेवरों (Young Professionals) की एक वर्ष की अनुबंध (contract) अवधि के लिए नियुक्ति/एंगेजमेंट की जा रही है। -(अंग्रेजी) स्टेनोग्राफर ग्रेड-I ग्रुप 'बी' अराजपत्रित उप कार्यालय संवर्ग के तीन पदों की भर्ती, वेतन मैट्रिक्स में लेवल-6 (7वें सीपीसी के अनुसार) {पूर्व-संशोधित वेतन बैंड-2 (रु. 9300-34800/-) में प्रतिनियुक्ति (अल्पकालिक अनुबंध सहित) पर भरी जाएगी। 2026-27 विपणन मौसम हेतु तोरिया के लिए न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एमएसपी) एफसीओ में नए रासायनिक उर्वरकों पर विचार के लिए चेकलिस्ट बीज विधेयक 2025 पर प्रारंभिक सलाह (पूर्व विधायी परामर्श) रबी विपणन मौसम (आरएमएस) 2026-27 के लिए रबी फसलों हेतु न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में "मार्केट इंटेलिजेंस निरीक्षक (अर्थशास्त्र)" के मसौदा भर्ती नियमों पर सभी हितधारकों से सुझाव/टिप्पणियाँ आमंत्रित की जाती हैं। -(अंग्रेजी) कैबिनेट ने प्रधानमंत्री धन-धान्य कृषि योजना को मंज़ूरी दी "2025-26 में चयनित फसलों के लिए प्रौद्योगिकी का उपयोग करके ग्राम पंचायत (जीपी) स्तर पर फसल उपज अनुमान" के लिए रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति हेतु निमंत्रण -(अंग्रेजी) राष्ट्रीय किसान कल्याण कार्यक्रम कार्यान्वयन सोसाइटी (NFWPIS) में प्रतिनियुक्ति (ISTC) के आधार पर तेरह (13) पदों को भरना खरीफ विपणन मौसम (केएमएस) 2025-26 के लिए खरीफ फसलों हेतु न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य कैबिनेट ने मौजूदा 1.5% ब्याज अनुदान (आईएस) के साथ वित्त वर्ष 2025-26 के लिए संशोधित ब्याज अनुदान योजना (एमआईएसएस) को जारी रखने की मंजूरी दी. स्टेनोग्राफर ग्रेड सी और डी परीक्षा, 2025 के लिए रिक्तियां फसलों के वास्तविक समय अवलोकन और फोटो के संग्रह के लिए रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति हेतु आमंत्रण (CROPIC) कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में (i) सहायक पुस्तकालय एवं सूचना अधिकारी तथा (ii) पुस्तकालय एवं सूचना सहायक के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियां/सुझाव आमंत्रित किए जाते हैं। एनएमएनएफ पर प्रतिस्पर्धी अनुसंधान अनुदान अधिसूचना वार्षिक रिपोर्ट 2024-25 रिक्ति वर्ष 2018, 2019 और 2020 के विरुद्ध अनुकंपा के आधार पर नियुक्ति के मामलों पर विचार करने के लिए 06-09-2024 और 14-11-2024 को आयोजित विभागीय स्थायी समिति की बैठक का कार्यवृत्त। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में स्टाफ कार ड्राइवर (साधारण ग्रेड) के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियां/सुझाव आमंत्रित किए जाते हैं। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में डिस्पैच राइडर के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियां/सुझाव आमंत्रित किए जाते हैं। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में डिस्पैच राइडर के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियां/सुझाव आमंत्रित किए जाते हैं। 2025-26 मौसम के लिए कच्चे पटसन हेतु न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य प्राकृतिक खेती पर राष्ट्रीय मिशन के संशोधित दिशानिर्देश प्राकृतिक खेती पर राष्ट्रीय मिशन के लिए अंतिम अनुमोदित दिशानिर्देश 2025-26 विपणन मौसम हेतु तोरिया के लिए न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एमएसपी) (शुद्धिपत्र ) (ईओआई) राष्ट्रीय किसान कल्याण कार्यक्रम कार्यान्वयन सोसायटी (एनएफडब्ल्यूपीआईएस) सोसायटी पंजीकरण अधिनियम, 1860 के तहत पंजीकृत है, जो कृषि और किसान कल्याण मंत्रालय के प्रशासन नियंत्रण में है। एनएफडब्ल्यूपीआईएस चौथी और छठी मंजिल, प्लांट अथॉरिटी भवन (पीएबी), पूसा, नई दिल्ली-12 पर चालू है, जो सिविल और इलेक्ट्रिकल कार्य, ए.सी की स्थापना, कला कार्य, फर्श/मैट आदि के लिए योग्य फर्मों/ठेकेदारों से रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति (ईओआई) आमंत्रित करता है। (ईओआई) राष्ट्रीय किसान कल्याण कार्यक्रम कार्यान्वयन सोसायटी (एनएफडब्ल्यूपीआईएस) सोसायटी पंजीकरण अधिनियम, 1860 के तहत पंजीकृत है, जो कृषि और किसान कल्याण मंत्रालय के प्रशासन नियंत्रण में है। एनएफडब्ल्यूपीआईएस चौथी और छठी मंजिल, प्लांट अथॉरिटी भवन (पीएबी), पूसा, नई दिल्ली-12 पर चालू है, जो सिविल और इलेक्ट्रिकल कार्य, ए.सी की स्थापना, कला कार्य, फर्श/मैट आदि के लिए योग्य फर्मों/ठेकेदारों से रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति (ईओआई) आमंत्रित करता है। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में आर्थिक अधिकारी के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियाँ/सुझाव आमंत्रित हैं कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में तकनीकी क्लर्क (अर्थशास्त्र) के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियाँ/सुझाव आमंत्रित हैं। कृषि विपणन पर राष्ट्रीय नीति ढांचे का मसौदा - सार्वजनिक टिप्पणियाँ/सुझाव के संबंध में। क्लाउड-आधारित एपीएम टूल के लिए रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति (ईओआई) हेतु अनुरोध हेतु शुद्धिपत्र 1 2024-25 (केवल खरीफ) के लिए खाद्यान्न, तिलहन और अन्य वाणिज्यिक फसलों के उत्पादन का पहला अग्रिम अनुमान रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति (ईओआई) के लिए अनुरोध कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में तकनीकी सहायक (अर्थशास्त्र) के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियाँ/सुझाव आमंत्रित आर्टिफिशियल इंटेलिजेंस (एआई) और डिजिटल कृषि पर सार-संग्रह डीए एंड एफडब्ल्यू के तहत कृषि लागत और मूल्य आयोग (सीएसीपी) में सदस्य (आधिकारिक) के एक (01) पद को भरना -(अंग्रेजी और हिंदी) कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में योजना अधिकारी के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों से टिप्पणी/सुझाव आमंत्रित हैं। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग के तहत कृषि लागत और मूल्य आयोग (सीएसीपी) में सदस्य (आधिकारिक) के एक (01) पद को भरना -(अंग्रेजी) सीएसीपी में सदस्य सचिव के 01 पद को भरने की तिथि में विस्तार -(अंग्रेजी) डिजिटल कृषि पहल पर सार-संग्रह -(अंग्रेजी) वार्षिक रिपोर्ट 2023-2024 -(अंग्रेजी) खरीफ विपणन मौसम (केएमएस) 2024-25 के लिए खरीफ फसलों हेतु न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य। किसान उत्पादक संगठनों पर राष्ट्रीय नीति का मसौदा -(अंग्रेजी) महिलाओं के यौन उत्पीड़न की रोकथाम के लिए आंतरिक शिकायत समिति (आईसीसी) की संशोधित संरचना। -(अंग्रेजी) क्रेडिट प्रभाग, कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग (उचित) के तकनीकी पदों के संबंध में भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियाँ/सुझाव आमंत्रित हैं। -(अंग्रेजी) G20 परिणाम दस्तावेज़ -(अंग्रेजी) कृषि क्षेत्र में स्वैच्छिक कार्बन बाजार के लिए रूपरेखा -(अंग्रेजी) कृषि वानिकी नर्सरी के लिए प्रत्यायन प्रोटोकॉल -(अंग्रेजी) ईएनएएम और अन्य ई-ट्रेडिंग प्लेटफार्मों के माध्यम से भंडारण और ईएनडब्ल्यूआर व्यापार के लिए किसानों को बढ़ावा देने के लिए संकल्पना नोट - पीएम किसान भाई योजना- टिप्पणियाँ / इनपुट - सादर। -(अंग्रेजी) 2024-25 विपणन मौसम की रबी फसलों के न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एम्एसपी) राष्ट्रीय बीज निगम लिमिटेड के अध्यक्ष सह प्रबंध निदेशक के पद के लिए चयन - एक अनुसूची बी स्पष्टीकरण ड्रोन के लिए एस.ओ.पी परियोजना निगरानी इकाई (पीएमयू) की प्रशासनिक लागत के लिए राज्य सरकारों को सहायता अनुदान जारी करने और उपयोग करने के लिए मानक संचालन प्रक्रिया (एसओपी) के संबंध में एग्रीकल्चर इन्फ्रास्ट्रक्चर फंड की हिंदी में दिशा-निर्देश कृषि अवसंरचना कोष के तहत वित्त पोषण के लिए केंद्रीय क्षेत्र योजना में ब्याज सबवेंशन के संबंध में स्पष्टीकरण कृषि समझौता और देखें मॉडल कृषि करार केला क्षेत्रीय भाषाओं में केले पर मॉडल खेती समझौता किसान कल्याण किसानों के डेटाबेस पर अवधारणाओं के प्रमाण विकसित करने पर समझौता ज्ञापन क्षेत्रीय भाषाओं में केले पर मॉडल कृषि करार सोशल मीडिया pages.title: G20 - Agriculture Working Group
pages.text: G20 - Agriculture Working Group About G20 G20 or a Group of Twenty is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union (EU). It works to address major issues related to the global economy, such as international financial stability, climate change mitigation, and sustainable development. The G20 is composed of most of the world's largest economies, including both industrialised and developing nations; it accounts for around 80% of gross world product (GWP), 75% of international trade, two-thirds of the global population, and 60% of the world's land area. Hence, authors call the forum as “The G20 and the Future of International Economic Governance” book by Mike Callaghan & Tristram Sainsbury and “G20 Governance for a Globalized World” book by John J. Kirton. G20 works with two tracks and one group namely: Finance Track, Sherpa Track and Outreach/ Engagement Group Agriculture Working Group Agriculture Working Group (AWG) was established in the year 2011, sixth meeting of G20 during Cannes Summit, France. Formation of AWG was the outcome of this summit, most notably in the area of agriculture and food security with a special launch of Agricultural Market Information System (AMIS) and the endorsement of an Action Plan on Food Price Volatility and Agriculture as tangible steps to address the world agriculture and food challenges. AWG – India’s Presidency Aligning with India’s G20 Presidency theme of ‘One Earth, One Family, One Future’, the Agriculture Working Group (AWG) aspires to enhance cooperation among G20 nations. The summit provides the member countries with an opportunity to coordinate at the policy level to work towards achieving global economic stability and sustainable growth. Concerning agriculture, the current challenges that member nations face are food insecurity, gaps in the global value chain, crop productivity due to climate change, and employment. Through the Issue note, the aim is to address these challenges Officers in Charge Shri Manoj Ahuja, Session Chair, AWG-G20 Secretary, DoA&FW, MoA&FW Shri Rakesh Ranjan, Additional Secretary Dr. Pramod Kumar Meherda, Additional Secretary Dr. Smita Sirohi, Session Co-chair, AWG-G20 Joint Secretary Shri Ritesh Chauhan, Joint Secretary Shri Rabindra Prasad, AWG-G20, Director Shri Muktanand Agrawal, Director, Logistics Shri Pankaj Singh, Under Secretary Shri Shashank Sahu, Under Secretary Ms. Parul Jain, Assistant Director (IC) AWG - Events and Host Cities | Event | Department | Dates | Cities | | 1st Agriculture Deputies Meeting (ADM), Indore | DA&FW | 13-15 February 2023 | Indore | | 2nd Agriculture Deputies Meeting (ADM) | DA&FW | 29-31 March 2023 | Chandigarh | | Meeting of Agricultural Chief Scientists (MACS) | DARE | 17-19 April 2023 | Varanasi | | 3rd ADM* & G-20 Agriculture Ministers Meeting (AMM) | DA&FW | 15-17 June 2023 | Hyderabad | Indore is a district of Madhya Pradesh state in central India. The city is named after its Indreshwar Mahadev temple. Indore was also known as Holkar state, a kingdom in India that belonged to the Holkar dynasty of Maratha Empire. Majestic palace, scenic beauty of patalpani falls and tincha falls adds to the warmth of the city. Varanasi, a city on the banks of river Ganges is the oldest city of India. With around 2000 temples, the “City of Temples” is famous as Spiritual Capital of the country. It is sought after by people from all walks of life, who are on the path of ‘sadhna’ spiritual discipline and penance. Located at the foothills of Shivaliks, Chandigarh, derives its name from the temple of “Chandi Mandir.” It is one of the best experiments of urban planning and modern architecture in India. It has been included in the list of world’s renowned cities for its quality of life and is the capital of two states Punjab and Haryana. Hyderabad is the capital and largest city of the Indian state of Telangana and the de jure capital of Andhra Pradesh. It is also known as “City of Pearls” and a Biryani paradise. It is known to have the largest film city in the world i.e. Ramoji Film City. With around 2000 temples, the “City of Temples” is famous as Spiritual Capital of the country. It is sought after by people from all walks of life, who are on the path of ‘sadhna’ spiritual discipline and penance. Located at the foothills of Shivaliks, Chandigarh, derives its name from the temple of “Chandi Mandir.” It is one of the best experiments of urban planning and modern architecture in India. It has been included in the list of world’s renowned cities for its quality of life and is the capital of two states Punjab and Haryana. Hyderabad is the capital and largest city of the Indian state of Telangana and the de jure capital of Andhra Pradesh. It is also known as “City of Pearls” and a Biryani paradise. It is known to have the largest film city in the world i.e. Ramoji Film City. pages.text: Home | Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare | MoA & FW | Government of India, India कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE & FARMERS WELFARE भारत सरकार GOVERNMENT OF INDIA कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण मंत्रालय MINISTRY OF AGRICULTURE & FARMERS WELFARE Department At A Glance Agriculture plays a vital role in India's economy. 54.6% of the total workforce is engaged in agriculture and allied sector activities (Census2011). Agriculture and Allied sector accounts for18.4 % of India's GVA at current prices during 2022-23. Given the importance of the agriculture sector, Government of India has taken several steps for its development in a sustainable manner. The Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare (DA&FW) is one of the two constituent Departments of the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare. This Department is headed by Agriculture & Farmers Welfare Minister and is assisted by two Ministers of State. The Secretary (A&FW)is the administrative head of the Department. The Secretary is assisted by five Additional Secretaries including one Financial Adviser, 10 Joint Secretaries, one Agriculture Commissioner, Horticulture Commissioner, Sr. Economic Advisor, Horticulture Statistics Advisor, Advisor cost and Deputy Director General. In addition, Chairman of Commission for Agriculture Costs and Prices (CACP) advises Department on pricing policies for selected agricultural crops. The DA&FW is organized into 28 divisions and has five attached offices and twenty-one subordinate offices which are spread across the country for coordination with state level agencies and implementation of Central Sector Schemes in their respective fields. Further, one Public Sector Undertaking, seven autonomous bodies, and two authorities are functioning under the administrative control of Department. Land Use Statistics As per land use statistics for 2021-22, the geographical area of our country is 328.7 million hectares, of which around 54.8% is agricultural land, 141.00 million hectares is the reported net sown area and 219.158million hectares is the gross cropped area with a cropping intensity of 155.4%. Net area sown comprises of 42.8% of the total geographical area. At all India level, total cropped area has increased by 66.1 percent in 2021-22 as compared to 1950-51 (131.89 million hectares in 1950-51 to 219.15 million hectares in 2021-22). The net irrigated area during 2021-22was 77.9millionhectare, out of which 24.7% was accounted by canal source ofirrigation, 2.8 % by tanks, 47.3 % by Tube wells, 13.2 % by other wells and remaining 12.0 % is irrigated by other source of irrigation. The gross irrigated area in 2021-22 was estimated at 120.3 million hectare, which was 54.9 % of the gross cropped area. Gross Value Added of Agriculture & Allied Sector As per the Provisional Estimates of National Income, 2022-23, released by National Statistical Office (NSO), Ministry of Statistics & Programme Implementation, the agriculture and allied sectors contributed approximately 18.4 % of India's Gross Value Added, (GVA) at current prices during 2022-23.The absolute Gross Value Added of agriculture and allied sectors and its share in total GVA of the country at current prices during the last6years is as follows: Gross Value Added of Agriculture & Allied Sector (Rs. documents.extracted_text:
F.No.Z-11018/8/2020-IT FTS No. 81917
Government of India
Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare
 Department of Agriculture, Cooperation and Farmers Welfare
Digital Agriculture Division
***
Krishi Bhawan, New Delhi
Dated the:    July, 2023
To,
 
The Principal Secretaries/Secretaries/ Commissioner/Director (Agriculture) of all
States/UTs
 
Subject:
 Revision of guidelines of Digital Agriculture scheme
erstwhile National e-Governance Plan in Agriculture (NeGP-A) to
include Digital Crop Survey
 
and need for building applications
on advanced technologies.
Madam/Sir,
            The undersigned is directed to refer to this Department’s letter of even no.
dated 02.06.2020 regarding revision of National e-Governance Plan in Agriculture
(NeGP-A) implementation approach and subsequent letter dated 15.11.2022
wherein, directives issued to the State(s) for implementation of the scheme.
2.      The revised guidelines for consideration of projects received from
States/UTs are as under:
i
. 
Funds under NeGPA will be released to States only for the project involving
use of modern Information Technologies such as Artiﬁcial Intelligence &
Machine Learning, Block Chain Technology, Internet of Things, Robotics etc.,
and for customization / shifting of web & mobile applications already
developed by the States, to the platform to be developed using digital
technologies mentioned above.
ii
. 
Digital Public Infrastructures/Digital Public Goods: Applications/scheme
relates to the digital public infrastructure for agriculture that will be built as an
open source, open standard and interoperable public good, which will enable
inclusive, farmer-centric solutions through relevant information services for
crop planning and health, improved access to farm inputs, credit and
insurance, help for crop estimation, market intelligence, and support for the
growth of the Agri Techs industry and start-ups will be given priority. One of
the Digital Public Infrastructures will be built with three core registries
(Farmer, Farmland Plot, and the Crop Sown data captured through a digital
crop survey) and will have a Uniﬁed Farmers' Service Interface (UFSI) to
develop farmer-centric digital service delivery solutions across the agriculture
value chain.
iii
. 
Evaluation of the projects submitted by the States will be done by the
Assessment Committee headed by JS (Digital) with representative of the
concerned Division, NIC & DS/Director (Digital). Further, Director (MNCFC)
has also been invited as special invitee during the Assessment Committee
Z-11018/8/2020-IT
I/28366/2023
I/28366/2023
File No. Z-11018/8/2020-IT (Computer No. 81917)
Generated from eOffice by RAHUL SWAMI, ASO(Digital)RS, ASO, DAC on 17/07/2023 01:08 PM
1
meeting.
iv
. 
The States/ UTs should submit the proposal under NeGP-A preferably with in
First quarter but not later than Second quarter of the Financial Year.
v
. 
The proposal should contain DPR, Financial implication, clear timelines and
technology involved.
vi
. 
Proposal would be examined on the use of latest technology, innovation,
scalability study of success and viability of the proposed solution before
proposing for pilot. Accordingly, necessary information may be submitted by
the States in the proposal.
vii
. 
The States/ UTs are advised to come up with proposals whose life-cycle from
conception to implementation is preferably for one year with the clear
timelines of implementation.   
viii
. 
As per latest guidelines of the Department of Expenditure (DoE), funds to the
States would be released in 4 equal tranches. Accordingly, States are
requested to update the details of expenditure on PFMS before requesting
the next installments.   
ix
. 
Next installment to the States/ UTs will be released after the receipt of the
satisfactory status report from the States/ UTs.     
x
. 
Pilot project submitted by State(s) wherein, State(s) are requesting for their
requirement of basic infrastructure viz. Hardware & manpower for support
and execution of the project, the same shall be borne by the concerned state.
xi
. 
Centre would have the co-ownership on the intellectual property created
during the pilot and accordingly, States/ UTs have to share the details with
Centre alongwith the outcomes of the pilot.
xii
. 
As a part of feedback project monitoring, the States/UTs are requested to
share the Cost Beneﬁt analysis with the Central Government. This will help
identify best practices and projects that have had tangibly beneﬁcial results
for farmers and eventually extrapolate such projects at pan-lndia level.
xiii
. 
The project sanctioned should be completed within the stipulated time.
However, this may be extended for a further period of 1 year at a time with
the prior approval of the Department, upto maximum of two years. Further,
even if the project is not completed in the extended year, State will require
surrendering the unspent amount. Any new proposal would be considered,
only after confirmation from PAO on receiving the refund amount.
Digital Crop Survey:-
3.      Also, it is requested to refer to this Department’s letter of even no. dated
30.06.2023 regarding release of funds for Digital Crop Survey for FY 2023-24
wherein, State(s) were informed that the ﬁnancial assistance for the Digital Crop
Survey pilots during the FY 2023-24 will be provided under the existing scheme
viz. NeGP-A. Also, separate guidelines were issued to the States for the same vide
letter dated 30.05.2023 (copy enclosed).
4.      The Department has initiated the Pilot on Digital Crop Survey (DCS) from
Kharif - 2023, as a component of Digital Agriculture Mission (DAM). Given that the
Digital Crop Survey is part of DAM, it is proposed to make provisions for DCS
under NeGP-A guidelines:
i
. 
Since, DCS has to be carried out by State(s) and for this purpose the state
will require large no. of human resources, that will be required to carry out
physically ﬁeld/plot-based crop survey, and for PMU and helpdesk, hence
funds may also be released to States for manpower deployment to conduct
DCS/setting up of Help Desk/ State PMU etc.

2
ii
. 
States may procure infrastructure related to DCS only. 
iii
. 
Funds already available with the State, which were released during last FYs
for different projects may be utilized for carrying out DCS.
iv
. 
States may be requested to submit the proposal under NeGP-A for DCS
during current FY i.e. 2023-24. All the proposals received from the States
w.r.t Digital Crop Survey may be approved at the level of Secretary (A&FW).  
 
Building of Digital Public Infrastructure:-
5.    The Department has taken note of the need of agriculture academic and
research institutes, such as ICAR, regarding their data requirements for the
purpose of Agri Stack/Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) in Agriculture.
6.    In this regard, 100% funds release may be considered to such academic and
research institutes for building scalable digital solutions using advanced and
emerging technologies which synchronizes with Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI)
in Agriculture requirement(s).
7.    Funds will be released to them only after examination of the need for the pilot
project and recommendation of a High-level Evaluation Committee constituted
under the chairmanship of AS (Digital) and DDG(NIC), Director (MNCFC),
DDG/ADG (ICT) DARE/ICAR, Agriculture Commissioner and subject matter expert
as nominated by the Chair based on the requirement(s).
 
8.    This issues with the approval of Hon'ble Agriculture Minister.
Encl: As above.
Yours faithfully,
 
 
Chandan Kumar
Under Secretary to the Govt. of India

.

equivalent level officers, DA&FW

1 Introduction 
Agriculture is rightfully considered to be one of the most resource and labor intensive industries. 
The challenges farmers face today include, but aren’t limited to the following: Regular equipment 
maintenance, Correct water estimates , Eliminating water waste and overhead expenses , 
Estimating correct planting times, Measuring soil temperature and moisture levels, Pest control 
Regular satellite monitoring of crops, allows farmers to easily follow the growth status of plants 
and carry out crop yield estimation using remote sensing. The evolution of plant health status 
provides key information to decide on possible interventions to the needs of crops. 
Local governments are tasked with  significant responsibilities with budgetary constraints. This 
results in ownership and other administrative issues where, under such circumstances, farmers 
find it difficult to concentrate on improving their yield. In order to meet these critical challenges 
and remain competitive with other countries, local governments should turn towards innovative 
analytics that are able to economically, efficiently  and accurately meet their analytical needs -
primarily satellite and location intelligence – and systematically monitor resources and yields as 
a way to further positive economic, social and environmental outcomes. 
2 Scope of Work 
The proposed project is designed with four broad objectives.  
• The first component is generation of Geo-referenced village map with digital basemap 
Farm Plot/ Crop land extracted using high resolution satellite images. 
• The second component is Digital Farmer’s Database creation. 
• A dashboard will be developed  to present the results and analytics  along with mobile 
application. 
3 Area of Interest 
The proposed project shall be implement at 6 (Six) Districts namely Tawang, Namsai, East Siang, 
Lower Subansiri, Lower Dibang Valley and Pakke kessang. The area have been selected on the 
basis of various factors like topography, climate, soil type, etc. so that better approach can be 
made for project implementation for rest of the part. 
4 Approach & Methodology 
4.1 Rationale 
Remote sensing data is increasingly used in agriculture and water resources management. In the 
last two decades, improvements in spatial, spectral , and temporal resolution of the earth 
observation systems (EOS) data encouraged the adoption of this technology for managing 
agriculture to meet the demand of the increasing population. The most important application in 
this regard is the use of medium and high -resolution data to map agriculture crops, monitor the 
crop condition, and estimate crop productivity. The improvements in remote sensing technology 
Creation of Farmar Database & Supporting Farmar Through Real Time Geospatial Analytics to Increase Farmar 
Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 5 of 21 
 
 
and the progressive development in computer software and hardware resulted in the 
development, calibration, and adoption of remote sensing models for managing agriculture.  
India’s agricultural production is mainly comprised of basic grains, such as rice, maize, and beans; 
fruits, including bananas, pineapples, and oranges; and vegetables such as onions and potatoes. 
The ag riculture sector experiences high fragmentation of land among farmers, preventing 
efficiency increases, which is further exacerbated by the slow adoption of technology. These 
challenges translate into the stagnation of agro-export, due to the decrease in local production, 
and high increases in agro import. This leads to variability in crop type, rotation, and their sowing 
dates, etc. in turn posing challenges in their delineation.  
In recent years, the integration of  machine learning algorithms to remote sensing  data 
with Geographic Information Systems  (GIS) has been used in automatic or semiautomatic crop 
extraction and mapping.  To determine the spatially accurate crop acreages, two methods have 
been explored: AI & ML-based image classification and spectral indexing.  
5 Proposed Data to be used in the study 
5.1 Satellite Images 
• Temporal High to medium spatial resolution satellite images  
• High-resolution satellite images viz., Mosaics (provided by consulting team) 
5.2 Secondary Data 
• Field information on the sample areas of crop and their coordinates.   
• Weather data include for crop monitoring. 
• Administrative boundary shapefile with attributes for the study area 
5.2.1 Support required from consulting team on secondary data 
• We request support from consulting team on the procurement and provision of high -
resolution satellite images.  
• We assume that consulting team will provide ground tr uth data. In the case of ground 
truth data collection and digital database creation , we propose for use of the FarmVigil 
app developed by the EOfactory  team. Using this field  survey and surveyors  can be 
monitored very effectively.    
6 Brief Methodology 
6.1 Project Initiation, Data Collection, and processing 
• Project Initiation 
o The department and the consulting team will finalize the study area. The process 
for procurement of satellite imagery will be initiated in the case of high-resolution 
images.  
• Satellite Images  
Creation of Farmar Database & Supporting Farmar Through Real Time Geospatial Analytics to Increase Farmar 
Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 6 of 21 
 
 
o The satellite images resolution with sub-meter spatial resolution images will be 
selected. Since the country of India is mostly clouded during all the crop seasons, 
it is proposed to use HRSI images for mapping and analytics.   
o Temporal satellite images will be selected and procured for the current year. The 
different period of images will help in extracting paddy crops of different seasons 
as well as in monitoring crop conditions during the crop grown period.  
• Ground Truth Data 
o It is assumed that the Ground truth Information on identified crops (Paddy, Corn, 
and Sugarcane) along with sample coordinates of other land use will be collected 
from consulting team.  
o In case the consulting team would like to undertake a field survey using a mobile 
application then provided it for the data collection. 
o A brief training will be provided on the application function s to the field survey 
team.  
o The data collected using the app can be monitored during the field survey. 
o The data can be downloaded and processed into GIS-compatible format.  
o In the situation w here ground truth data is not available then the accuracy of 
results will get affected.  
6.2 Crop Mapping  
• Crop identification, classification, and estimation using Machine Learning and 
remote sensing technique. 
o This component of the project will use the Sub-meter resolution or High 
resolution (AxelGlobe) satellite image to locate, identify and map identified crops 
of Paddy, Corn, and Sugarcane in the study area.  
o The Application proposed will be used for crop signature creation and crop 
classification. The application uses machine learning algorithms for the 
processing of images.  
o An index-based extraction approach will be followed for completing the task in a 
short time.  
• GIS analysis for acreage estimation 
o Administrative boundary shapefiles for the sub-region and provinces will be used 
for computing the area statistics.  
6.3 All season paddy crop mapping using time-series satellite images 
• Time-series image classification for Paddy crop mapping 
Creation of Farmar Database & Supporting Farmar Through Real Time Geospatial Analytics to Increase Farmar 
Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 7 of 21 
 
 
o Basically Paddy crop is planted three times in the year. To map and estimate the 
all-season paddy crop grown areas, time -series satellite images will be selected 
and classified.  
• GIS analysis for administrative unit wise / study area block-wise area computation 
o Administrative boundary shapefiles for the sub-region and provinces will be used 
for computing the area statistics. 
6.4 Dashboard development for data viewing and analytics  
• Develop a user -friendly web  dashboard to display maps and data for effective 
decision-making.  
o This component of the project will be Web-Dashboard Development. This activity 
will include the development and customization of a decision-making application 
as a web dashboard. The objective of this application will be to view and analyze 
crops of different periods and their conditions using satellite image -derived 
indices.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Creation of Farmar Database & Supporting Farmar Through Real Time Geospatial Analytics to Increase Farmar 
Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 8 of 21 
 
 
7 Process flow diagram of the proposed project 
 
7.1 The proposed project approach diagram:

Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

7.2 Proces flow of SAR Image Analysis of crop mapping:

Creation of Farmar Database & Supporting Farmar Through Real Time Geospatial Analytics to Increase Farmar 
Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 10 of 21 
 
 
8 Project Deliverables: 
 
Followingare the deliverables  
8.1 Deliverable 1: Farm/Plot/Village boundary and Satellite images 
• Digital layer (.pdf / .jpg / .ppt or GIS compatible format) of Farm layers for the project 
study area overlaid administrative boundaries.  
• Seamless high Resolution images 
8.2 Deliverable 2: Farmer Digital Database 
• Digital layer (.pdf / .jpg / .ppt or GIS compatible format) of farmer database layers for the 
project study area overlaid administrative boundaries.  
• The statistics and report (.xls / .ppt) will be submitted in dashboard.  
8.3 Deliverable 3: Web Dashboard for viewing, monitoring, planning, and 
effective decision making. 
• A decision -making application with analytical functionalities as a web  dashboard for 
displaying maps and results.  
9 Our solution                                          
9.1 Farm/Plot Boundary map 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
      
 
  
Creation of Farmar Database & Supporting Farmar Through Real Time Geospatial Analytics to Increase Farmar 
Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 11 of 21 
 
 
9.2 Time Series Analytics 
 
 
Using time-series analytics, you can visualize historic vegetation health conditions in your area of 
interest. Filter Dates and understand specific crop information on any given day. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
EOF monitor can identify up to 7 zones with different productivity rates and create differential 
fertilizing, sowing, and irrigation maps, thus saving you a lot of time and resources. 
 
9.3 Soil Moisture Condition 
Soil condition is an important indicator helping farmers decide on the optimal planting, and crop 
gathering time. With soil condition monitoring, farmers get instantly alerted of soil moisture and 
salinity. Other metrics include soil temperature and air te mperature: estimating them correctly 
enables farmers to plan watering times and know when to expect pests.  
 
9.4 Input Weather Data 
    
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Creation of Farmar Database & Supporting Farmar Through Real Time Geospatial Analytics to Increase Farmar 
Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 12 of 21 
 
 
 
Input weather data for additional information helps users to get updated with the latest 
weather information and react to changes in a timely manner. Monitor Wind Speed, Wind 
Direction, Humidity, Cloud Cover Percentage, and Precipitation Information. 
9.5.1 Tools for Field Analytics  
• Monitoring Indices 
Using various indices derived using sentinel imagery, such as NDVI, SAVI, GCI, RECI, EVI2, SIPI, 
NDRE, and moisture index. 
• Time-Series Panel  
Use Time-Series Panel to Visualize historic vegetation health conditions in your area of 
interest 
• Filter and Change the Date Range for the analysis 
• Weather Forecast 
This helps users to get updated with the latest weather information and react to changes in a 
timely manner. Monitor Wind Speed, Wind Direction, Humidity, Cloud Cover Percentage, and 
Precipitation Information 
9.5.2 Description of the Monitoring Indices 
Vegetation indices are an important parameter to develop crop analytics. 
Monitor the state of the crops using multiple vegetation indices and moisture index.  
1. Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI): NDVI compares the reflectance values of 
the red and near-infrared regions of the electromagnetic spectrum. 
o The NDVI value, which ranges from -1.0 to 1.0 for each pixel in an image, helps to  
identify areas of varying levels of plant biomass/vigor. Higher values indicate high 
biomass/high vigor.   
o NDVI values give accurate analytics in the middle of the season at the stage of 
active crop growth. 
1. Soil-Adjusted Vegetation Index (SAVI): SAVI accounts for the differential red and near-
infrared extinction through the vegetation  canopy. The index is a transformation 
technique that minimizes soil brightness influences from spectral vegetation indices 
involving red and near-infrared (NIR) wavelengths. 
o Empirically derived NDVI products have been shown to be unstable, varying with 
soil color, soil moisture, and saturation effects from high-density vegetation. 
1. Green Chlorophyll Vegetation Index (GCI): GCI is used to estimate the content of leaf 
chlorophyll in various species of plants. The health of vegetation is directly correlated 
with the chlorophyll content of the leaves. 
Creation of Farmar Database & Supporting Farmar Through Real Time Geospatial Analytics to Increase Farmar 
Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 13 of 21 
 
 
o GCI reflects the physiological state of vegetation; a lower value of GCI represents 
stressed conditions of plants and a higher value shows the healthy condition of 
the crop, hence GCI is very useful to measure vegetation health. 
1. Red-Edge Chlorophyll Vegetation Index (RECl): The ReCI vegetation index is 
responsive to chlorophyll content in leaves that is nourished by nitrogen. ReCI shows the 
photosynthetic activity of the canopy cover. 
2. Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI): The EVI is an 'optimized' vegetation index designed 
to enhance the vegetation signal with improved sensitivity in high biomass regions and 
improved vegetation monitoring through a de-coupling of the canopy background signal 
and a reduction in atmosphere influences. 
3. Structure Intensive Pigment Vegetation Index (SIPI): SIPI index is a reflectance 
measurement designed to maximize the sensitivity of the index to the ratio of bulk 
carotenoids (for example, alpha -carotene and beta -carotene) to chlorophyll while 
decreasing the sensitivity to variation in canopy structure (for example, leaf area index).  
o Increases in SIPI are thought to indicate increased canopy stress (carotenoid 
pigment). Applications include vegetation health monitoring, plant physiological 
stress detection, and crop production and yield analysis 
1.  Normalized difference red edge index  (NDRE): NDRE is an index that can only be 
formulated when the Red edge band is available in a sensor. It is sensitive to chlorophyll 
content in leaves (how green a leaf appears), variability in leaf area, and soil background 
effects. High values of NDRE represent higher levels of leaf chlorophyll content than lower 
values.  
o Soil typically has the lowest values, unhealthy plants have intermediate values, 
and healthy plants have the highest values. Consider using NDRE if you are 
interested in mapping variability in fertilizer requirements or foliar Nitrogen, not 
necessarily Nitrogen availability in the soil. 
1. Normalized Difference Moisture Index (NDMI): The Normalized Difference Moisture 
Index (NDMI) is sensitive to the moisture levels in vegetation. It is used to monitor 
droughts as well as monitor fuel levels in fire-prone areas. It uses NIR and SWIR bands to 
create a ratio designed to mitigate illumination and atmospheric effects.

10 The Benefits of the proposal  
10.1 Maximized productivity 
Agriculture crop monitoring timely measures to eliminate the usual threats increases crop yields.  
10.2 Improved quality 
It helps to maintain optimal conditions to ensure better crop quality. For example, weather 
monitoring in agriculture helps estimate the exact supply of water, chemicals, and nutrients 
needed to grow high-quality crop yields. 
10.3 Predictability and control 
Driven by real time agriculture monitoring, data analytics predict the optimal harvest dates and 
ensure the security of supply contracts. The control farmers gain over time-to-market helps make 
farm processes more manageable.

10.4 Future casting 
By collecting and processing data retrieved using smart agriculture monitoring, farmers can 
predict the future state of soils and environments, and plan for next year’s crops. Thus, predictive 
analytics enable them to make calculated farm -management decisi ons and plan for the years 
ahead. 
11 Development of system for data storage and processing. 
Arunachal Pradesh is unique and its issues are different in nature than other states. The proposal 
concentrate on developing the department’s infrastructure for supporting the data generated 
during the project. Various data like satellite image, farm plot map, GIS layers, etc shall be 
developed for which a centralised support system is must required.  
 The system shall have various server, storage, security and threat system, power back, 
connectivity, etc,. The entire system shall be centralised and shall caters the support 
infrastructure to the department. 
12 Training 
Once the mapping of the crop plot/village boundary survey is completed and platform developed 
along with mobile application, training shall be provided to all the farmers and departments staff 
on usage of the application. 
13 Web dashboard portal for viewing, monitoring, planning, 
and effective decision making 
Farm boundary extraction and updating using data from a dashboard is a critical task for 
agriculture management and land -use planning. This process involves extracting the 
geographical boundaries of farms from satellite imagery or other geospatial data sources and 
then updating these boundaries based on real-time or periodically collected information available 
in a dashboard. Here's a detailed write-up on how to accomplish this task: 
1. Data Sources and Dashboard: 
● Satellite Imagery: Obtain high -resolution satellite imagery of the area where farms are 
located. Services like Google Earth, Sentinel Hub, or custom providers can be used. 
● GIS Data: Access geographic information system (GIS) data, including parcel maps, land 
cover data, and other relevant geospatial information. 
● Dashboard: Utilize a dashboard or data management system that provides up -to-date 
information about farms, including ownership, land-use changes, and other relevant data. 
This dashboard can be a custom -built application or a commercial solution like Tableau 
or Power BI. 
 
Creation of Farmar Database & Supporting Farmar Through Real Time Geospatial Analytics to Increase Farmar 
Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 17 of 21 
 
 
2. Preprocessing: 
● Data Integration: Combine the satellite imagery, GIS data, and dashboard information into 
a unified database or GIS platform for analysis. 
● Data Cleaning: Ensure that all data is accurate, consistent, and in a suitable format for 
analysis. 
3. Farm Boundary Extraction: 
● Image Processing: Use remote sensing and image processing techniques to analyze the 
satellite imagery. This includes applying filters, segmentation, and classification 
algorithms to identify potential farm boundaries. 
● Object Detection: Employ object detection models like YOLO or Faster R -CNN to identify 
and outline farm boundaries in the imagery. 
● Boundary Refinement: Refine the initial boundary detections using GIS data and 
additional information from the dashboard. Correct any inaccuracies or false positives. 
4. Data Fusion: 
● Dashboard Information: Overlay the extracted farm boundaries on the dashboard data. 
This allows you to link the geographical boundaries with farm -specific information such 
as ownership, crop type, and land-use history. 
5. Boundary Updating: 
● Dashboard Integration: Develop a mechanism for updating farm boundaries based on 
changes observed in the dashboard. This can be done manually by authorized users or 
automatically using algorithms that compare historical data with the current dashboard 
information. 
● Change Detection: Implement change detection algorithms to identify areas where farm 
boundaries have changed. This could include land expansion, subdivision, or changes in 
land use. 
● Validation and Approval: Establish a process for validating and approving boundary 
updates. Ensure that authorized individuals review and confirm the proposed changes 
before they are applied. 
6. Visualization and Reporting: 
● Dashboard Display: Visualize the updated farm boundaries and associated information on 
the dashboard. Users should be able to view farms, their boundaries, and relevant details 
in an easy-to-understand format. 
 
Creation of Farmar Database & Supporting Farmar Through Real Time Geospatial Analytics to Increase Farmar 
Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 18 of 21 
 
 
● Reports: Generate reports summarizing farm boundary updates, changes in land use, and 
other relevant insights. These reports can be used for decision -making and compliance 
monitoring. 
 
● Charts and Graphs: Create various charts and graphs to represent farm data, such as line 
charts for temperature trends, bar graphs for crop yields, and scatter plots for soil 
analysis. 
 
● Maps: Utilize maps to display the farm layout, field boundaries, and real -time data like 
weather conditions or equipment locations. 
 
● Tables: Include tables to show detailed data records, such as crop planting and harvest 
schedules. 
 
● Key Performance Indicators (KPIs): Highlight important metrics and KPIs, such as crop 
yield per acre, cost per unit produced, and weather alerts. 
 
● Set up alerting mechanisms to notify farmers or farm managers of critical events, such as 
adverse weather conditions, equipment malfunctions, or deviations from planned 
schedules. 
 
● Ensure that the dashboard is responsive and can be accessed on mobile devices, as 
farmers often need to make decisions while in the field. 
 
● Implement data analysis algorithms and machine learning models to provide predictive 
insights. For example, you can predict crop yields based on historical data and current 
weather conditions. 
7. Data Security and Access Control: 
● Data Security: Implement robust data security measures to protect sensitive information 
about farms and landowners. 
 
● Access Control: Define user roles and permissions to ensure that only authorized 
personnel can update farm boundaries and access sensitive data. 
8. Continuous Monitoring and Feedback: 
● Regular Updates: Set up a schedule for periodic updates of farm boundaries based on 
dashboard information. This ensures that the data remains current and accurate. 
 
● Feedback Loop: Establish a feedback mechanism for users to report discrepancies or 
suggest boundary updates based on ground observations.

9. Integration with Farm Management: 
● APIs and Integration: Integrate the farm boundary data with other farm management 
systems to streamline operations and improve decision-making for farmers. 
10. Compliance and Regulations: 
● Compliance: Ensure that the farm boundary extraction and updating process complies 
with local regulations, privacy laws, and data-sharing agreements. 
Farm boundary extraction and updating based on dashboard information is a dynamic and 
ongoing process that contributes to efficient land management, precision agriculture, and 
informed decision -making for both farm owners and agricultural authorities. It enhances the 
accuracy of land records and supports sustainable land use practices.

Problem Statement 
 
NIC, Gujarat  is entrusted for the development, implementation and maintenance of the iKhedut 
portal for the Agriculture , Farmer Welfare  and Cooperation department, Govt. of Gujarat.T he 
iKhedut portal is developed  and  implemented in 2014 and since then it is running successfully.  
The iKhedut portal along with its related modules are hosted on the dedicated servers co-located 
at Gujarat State Data Centre and owned by Agriculture Department. 
 
Approximate 250+ various schemes of the Agriculture department which are implemented by 
respective HoDs of the department being made online every year on the iKhedut portal. Apart 
from this various other applications software modules are developed and made operational. 
 
The existing software is developed using the proprietary software tools. The current hardware 
and software infrastructure on which iKhedut portal is hosted at Gujarat State Data Centre will 
become out of support in near future. The infrastructure deployed since 2014 has not 
undergone any major up-gradation. 
 
A joint meeting was organized in the chairmanship of Director of Agriculture in his office at Krishi Bhavan 
on Date 19-4-2023 to discuss the revamping of iKhedut portal and enhancing the current scope of the 
portal.The Director of Agriculture has requested NIC to prepare a Application Architecture Diagram 
based on the discussion held during the meeting  to submit it along with  the consolidated proposal 
which will be prepared by Directorate and will be submitted to NeGP-A. 
 
The objective is to revamp the existing iKhedut to iKhedut 2.0: 
 
1) The existing system was developed using the proprietary software which will in near future 
becoming out of the support. Hence it is highly required to upgrade the technology as per the 
current need. 
2) The existing hardware’s infrastructure is became out dated and need to be upgraded. The new 
infrastructure can be deployed which minimize the overall license cost to the department. 
3) The system user interfaces in existing system are in  old fashion. Proposed system will have 
better ,attractive and user friendly user interfaces. 
4) To bring Transparency in the system 
5) Providing information on fingertips at the managerial level. 
6) Reports /Queries and dashboard for top level management. 
7) Providing functionalities on mobile devices which are feasible for  mobile device

1 Introduction 
India, the world 's second most populous nation is seeking to ensure food security 
not just for its population but also would want to play a pivotal role i n securing food 
security for the world. In the overall scheme of things , Punjab is extremely important 
given the progres s it has made in the agriculture sector over the past many years. 
Climate change is a reality; we have already started to see its impact of it with 
increasingly volatile weather conditions. In an increasingly volatile world access to 
information will hold the key to a proactive response to ensure effective risk 
mitigation and control. Climate change makes agriculture the most vulnerable sector 
hence a serious threat to food security. 
Technology adoption is still at a nascent stage in India. “Limited availab ility of 
technology infrastructure and recurring natural phenomena like floods, and droughts 
have also worked against the deployment of digital solutions,” in India. However, 
there is one data source that is free from such natural hazard s which is satellit e 
data. If a harnessed way can potentially change the way we look at agriculture as 
the sector with recent advancement in satellite imagery, coupled with advancement 
in the field of computation has made technologies like Artificial intelligence /machine 
learning leveraging big data, a reality. 
Punjab is one of the most fertile regions on Earth. The region is ideal for growing 
wheat, rice, sugarcane, fruits , and vegetables. Indian Punjab is called the “Granary 
of India” or India’s bread -basket. It produces 20% of India’s wheat and 9% of India’s 
rice. On a global scale, this represents 3% of the world’s production of these crops, 
so the Indian Punjab produces 2% of the world’s cotton, 2% of its wheat , and 1% of 
the world’s rice. The largest grown crop is wheat; however, other important crops are 
rice, cotton, sugarcane, pearl millet, maize, barley, and fruits.  The principal crops of 
Punjab are barley, wheat, rice, maize , and sugarcane. Among the fodder crops are 
bajra and jowar. The main sources of irrigation  are canals and tube wells. The 
economy of the state primarily depends upon the agriculture sector. The total 
geographical area of  Punjab is 5.036 million hectare s and the area under forest is 
2, 93,000 hectare s. The cultivable area is 4.20 million hectare s (83.4%of total 
5 | P a g e  
geographical area) and the net area sown is 4.023 million hectare s (95.7% of the 
cultivable area).   The gross cropped area is 7.739 million hectare s and the area 
sown more than once is 3.704 million hectare s with a cropping intensity of 186  %. 
The net irrigated area is 4.019 million hectare s (By canals- 26.2%, By Tube wells - 
72.5%, and by others – 1.3%). The gross irrigated area is 7.442 million hectare s and 
the percentage of net irrigated sown area is 96.17%.    The total number of land 
holdings is 10.93 lakh out of which 2.04  lakh (18.7%) are marginal farmers, 
1.83 lacks (16.7%) are small farmers , and 7.06  lakh (64.6%) farmers hold land 
above 2 hectare s.  As the farmer navigates through the crop cycle, they come 
across multiple challenges in the state across different crop phases such as field 
preparation, sowing, crop monitoring & management, harvesting, post-harvest 
& marketing/ selling of crops . These cha llenges are due to climate change and 
uncertainty, which impacts the crop cycle; and information asymmetry, which limits 
farmers’ ability to take timely decisions. For Punjab Agri Export Corporation Limited 
in association with other stake -holding departments there is a need to create an 
efficient structure for collecting data and disseminating information to farmers 
on various new technologies and to bring-in efficiencies by transforming the services 
by leveraging digital technologies, integration of best practices , and systematic 
management of interdependencies. 
1.1 The Vision of the Project 
Digital transformation technology can be a big enabler to bring transparency in the 
value chain of agriculture commodities. Digital technology including the intern et, 
mobile technologies, sensors, remote -sensing, drones, data analytics, artificial 
intelligence, and block -chain are changing agriculture and its supply chain. It can 
provide better market linkage between producers and suppliers and new markets 
and enable new ways to monitor and ensure compliance with standards and provide 
a faster and more efficient procedure that is essential, especially for perishable 
products. This is achieved by digitizing the farmer data base giving access to 
technical know-how on c rop planning and management, inputs, credit, post -harvest 
management, value addition, and better market linkages for the farmers. This 
6 | P a g e  
integrated block -chain platform would digitize all the farmers engaged with them 
across different districts in the state and makes the field -level data collection user -
friendly. The centralized platform would help capture all relevant information through 
a simple mobile application and give dashboard -based insights on the data 
captured. Having field-level monitoring capability, all the farm activities are captured 
in the system ensuring traceability of the entire value chain.  
Some of the problems made easy by Digital transformation are as under: 
1. Supply chain efficiency & traceability through Block Chain platform.  
2. Monitor and ensure compliance with standards operating procedures or Package 
of Practices to provide faster and more efficient procedures for advisory service.  
3. Monitoring production, harvest & market linkage of Kinnow , Basmati Rice & 
Organic /Millets products.  
4. Adhering to export compliance & building trust between consumers & suppliers.  
5. Implementing a Package of Practices.  
6. Value addition through enhancing the supply chain of an agricultural commodity 
through technology. 
1.2 Primary Objectives:  
Punjab Agri Export Corporation Limited (PAGREXCO) is a government enterprise 
established by the Government of Punjab in the year 1997 as the nodal agency for 
agricultural export from the state of Punjab, India. The company works for the export 
of fresh and processed agricult ural produce, infrastructural development , and policy 
implementation in the state. It is a subsidiary of Punjab Agro Industries Corporation. 
The company has been awarded Sustainable Development Goals Action Award by 
the Punjab Planning Department for Susta inable Organic Production and Innovative 
Marketing System. It has also received a Leadership award in Agriculture Marketing. 
The list of functions of PAGREXCO is as follows:  
 Facilitating export of fresh & seasonal agricultural produce  and value added 
products like; Chilli Paste, Rice, Wheat Flour etc.

2 About DPR 
2.1 Agro-Climatic Zones of Punjab 
According to Punjab Agriculture University (PAU), Ludhiana, there are 6 agro -climatic 
zones in Punjab which include: 
1. Sub-Mountain Undulating Region 
2. Undulating Plain Region (UPR)  
3. Central Plain Region (CPR)  
4. Western Plain Region (WPR) 
5. Western Region (WR) 
6. Flood Plain Region (FPR)  
11 | P a g e  
These regions have rainfall variations from 165 mm to 2000 mm annually and climate 
from humid to cold -arid to arid and extreme arid. The variations in soil range from hill 
soils, tarai, brown hill, alluvial to desert. 
 
Following are few major crops grown in this state based on the majorAgro -Climatic 
Zones: 

12 | P a g e  
 
The State has a Flood Plain region which is locally known as Bet area.  Patiala, Ropar, 
Sangrur, Bathinda, Ferozepur districts fall under Bet area.  This region has four 
components, Satluj, Beas, Ravi &  Gagghar with an area of 3500 sq. km which is 7 
percent area of the state. The flood plains transversally cut across the state in the east -
west cross section, so the region is not homogenous in climatic conditions. The climate 
of different segments of Flood Plain region corresponds to the surrounding agro -climatic 
zone. 
At present, 82 percent of the geographical area of the state is under cultivation. The 
cropping intensity is 191 percent with over 95 percent of the cultivable area being under 
assured irrigation. The gross area sown in the state was 7823 thousand hectares & Net 
area sown was 4119 thousand hectares during 2019-20. As per Agriculture Statistics At 
Glance of 2021, Issued by Government of India, , The State has highest yi eld of Rice 
(4366 kg per hectare) and Wheat (4862 kgs per hectare).  
The food grain production in the state has increased from 3.16 million tons in 1960 -61 
to 31.53 million tons in 2018 -19 with a minor decrease of 29.77 million tons in 2020 -21. 
as a result  of introduction of High Yielding  Varieties & new technologies to the hard 
working and highly receptive farmers with the support of the government policies. The 

13 | P a g e  
Punjab state known as “ Granary of India ” has played key role in transforming India 
from a famine affected & grain importing nation  to self-reliant& export surplus nation by 
contributing to the central pool over the years. Punjab contributed the production of 
wheat with 17.14 million hectare and 12.8 million hectares of rice to central pool during 
the year 2020-21. 
The gains resulting from the success of ever-increasing food grain production during the 
past five decades, have not been without their consequent environmental & socio -
economic costs. The major environmental issues assailing the agricult ure in the state 
includes alarming decline in water table, deterioration soil health, non -judicious use of 
farm chemicals, reduced genetic diversity, crop residue management & over all 
degradation of fragile agro -ecosystem. Thus, it has resulted in greater  impacts on 
environment & climate and health of people in the state. The stagnated yields and high 
cost of inputs are also leading to diminishing economic returns to farming community in 
the state. 
Imperative initiatives are required to keep the agricultur al economy of the state in stable 
condition like increase in Research and Development, crop diversification and to restore 
rural non-farm activities to put the agriculture sector of the state on fast track. 
2.2 AI Powered Geo Spatial Block Chain Platform: 
AI-powered Geo -spatial block chain platform  is envisaged to create a geo – spatial 
intelligence web for agriculture to ensure a data-driven approach to policy making and 
interventions. It is expected to revolutionize the sector with information and bring 
symmetry in such information across all stakeholders to bridge the information 
asymmetry which exit today. It will have both direct and indirect effect s on agriculture as 
a sector and the rural economy as a whole. It ’s going to cut across the value chain o f 
agriculture and make all transactions more efficient. The state of Punjab has been at the 
forefront of adopting digital technologies to bring positive change in the agriculture 
sector. This is another step in making the sector truly digital with data lak es which will 
be benefited all stakeholders with backward or forward linkages to the sector. 
Information asymmetry across stake holders is the most fundamental challenge for the 
14 | P a g e  
sector. Such information asymmetry makes every transaction costly and hence 
inefficient. To address the huge information asymmetry, we need to have a fully 
integrated decision support system with a 360-degree view o f the sectors where all 
stakeholders with the backward or forward linkages to the sector including govt are fully 
connected in a true sense. 
Access to real -time information powered by state-of-the-art intelligence shall make 
every transaction with the sector more efficient and cost -effective. The proposed 
dynamic decision support system aims at bridging information asymmet ry across all 
stake holders including farmers to ensure that all can make more informed decisions. 
This project has three components: 
The first component, Promoting Climate ‐  resilient Agricultural Systems, aims to 
strengthen the adaptive capacity of smallh older farmers to adjust and modify their 
production systems to moderate potential future impacts from climate events. 
The second component, Post‐ harvest Management, and Value Chain Promotion aim to 
support the participation of smallholder farmers in Farmer Producer Organizations 
(FPOs) and the integration of these FPOs in value chains for crops relevant to the 
climate agenda and to strengthen the supply chain for cli mate-resilient crop varieties in 
the project area. 
The third component, Institutional Development, Knowledge and Policies for a Climate 
Resilient Agriculture, aims to enhance the transformative capacity of institutions and 
stakeholders to promote and pursu e a more climate resilient agriculture, with sector 
strategies and policies based on strong analytical underpinnings and cutting ‐  edge 
climate, water, and crop modelling.

2.3 Challenges and Problems to be Addressed: Agriculture Site 
 Presently, agriculture has become a relatively unrewarding profession mainly due 
to low productivity, unfavourable prices, and practically little value addition. 
 Scanty rainfall, scarcity of irrigation water , and inefficient water management 
practices constitute some of the major challenges in the State. 
 Besides increasing the overall system productivity, the challenge also is 
appropriately reducing the cost of cultivation. 
 The i ntegrated farming approach is another area that needs preferential 
attention. 
 Huge yield gaps exist between the experimental yield and those obtained at the 
farm level. Up -scaling of farm -validated modern technologies and agriculture 
innovation is often lacking. 
 Farm mechanization, which is important for increasing production and qua lity of 
produce, is presently lacking in the State, especially in tribal areas. 
2.4 Challenges and Problems to be Addressed: Technology Site 
 The land size, cost, and benefits of technology are some of the economic factors 
that determine the rate of agricultural technology adoption. 

16 | P a g e  
 Farmers’ education level, age, social groupings, and gender are some of the 
social factors that influence the probability of a farmer adopting modern 
agricultural technologies. 
 Small-scale farmers face both internal and external challenges as far as the 
adoption of modern agricultural technologies is concerned. This aspect accounts 
for the slow rate at which such technologies are adopted. 
The challenge also is to provide proper institutional mechanism s and coordination 
between various agencies for development activities and investment for overcoming the 
felt constraints coming in the way of farm prosperity in the State.  
Regardless of the  challenges, what matters is whether modern technology has any 
value in the agricultural sector especially Karnataka state -specific requirements in the 
current scenario and futuristic changes. One of the challenges that farmers face 
nowadays is the need to  satisfy labour. There is an increasing cost of labour, which 
calls for better approaches to ensure less cost o f labour. The use of modern technology 
in agriculture ensures that farmers grow vast food within the shortest time possible. It is 
therefore important, to plant ; early harvest, in time, as well as ensure that the yield is 
stored within the right time. 
2.5 National e-Governance Plan in Agriculture (NeGP-A) 
National e -Governance Plan in Agriculture (NeGP -A), is a central government 
sponsored scheme where  in use of Information & Communication Technology (ICT) is 
used in agriculture information distribution to the Indian farmer. All the relevant 
information pertaining to agriculture is provided to farming community, government 
agencies, private sectors, and  any stakeholder involved in agriculture. This scheme will 
improve access of farmers to on -time and relevant information & services throughout 
crop-cycle. This can be achieved through a uniform platform and hence the time 
between the generation and dissemi nation of information is reduced. It is important to 
notice that this plan is farmer centric and all relevant information is provided in a real -
time manned, with utmost transparencies and with advisory services.

With recent significant advancement in smart  agriculture, more focus is given to remote 
sensing &GIS system, data analytics, cloud computing, Artificial Intelligence and 
Machine Learning ( AIML) tool, Drone implementation and Blockchain in the supply 
chain for traceability. To infuse these advancemen ts in agriculture to make it more 
accurate in assessing and determining more developed solution s to make better 
implementations; the government has made amendments to NeGP-A.

soil moisture, humidity, etc. can be recorded using IoT devices

Using state of art remote sensing, drone, artificial intelligence , and machine learning all 
critical information associated with the crop life cycle. The fundamental benefits are: 
3.1 Crop Area Estimate, Yield Estimate, and Performance Monitoring to 
Manage Supply-Side Risk: 
While for the government the real benefit lies in ability to assess acreage under different 
crops in quick time and the ability to assess the crop growth more frequently to be able 
to assess the situa tion in advance as it has impact on various critical decision that the 
government undertakes. Traditional approach which is predominantly based on surveys 
/ crop cutting experiments are time consuming and costly. Moreover, the coverage is 
always a challeng e given the geographical area to be covered. Given the scale, the 
sample size for these surveys / crop cutting exercise is always a challenge. More 
importantly by the time the yield is assessed it is already a reality. There is literally no 
response time. This leaves all stakeholders including government and famers exposed 
to supply side risk on either side (deficits / surplus).  
One of the main objective is to leverages such a technology to able to monitor crop 
across the life cycle of the crop growth to b e able to forecast likely yield with acceptable 
confidence to ensure lead time to respond to ground realities instead, of treating it as 
supply side shock which has been the reality so far.  
3.2 Run the Business of Agriculture More Business-Like 
Until advancement in remote sensing, there was no primary source of high frequency 
data available to be able to monitor crops growth. The alternate technologies were 
prohibitively expensive. With advancement in remote sensing, coupled with 
19 | P a g e  
advancement in the area of computation, machine learning and AI has made it possible 
monitor every single land parcel with high frequency in near real time. These advances 
create a significant opportunity to make all interventions and interaction with the sector 
data driven and run the  whole sector more like business to make it viable and 
sustainable. At the same time make the business of farming more lucrative. 
3.3 Acreage Allocation to Crop (Profitable &Sustainable Farming) 
Any yield improvement measures must also be linked with crop dive rsity as it would 
otherwise lead to oversupply situation which will not help price realization of end 
produce. We must ensure that the areas under cultivation across all Agriculture 
commodities are linked with market. Unless collective intelligence is made  available to 
farmers to ensure they can take more informed decision on how much areas they 
should allocate to which crop given the likely yield. Unless the collective intelligence is 
made available and consumable, we cannot protect farmers from falling on  the wrong 
side of the price curve. Area allocation is critical for price realization. One of the 
medium-term objectives is hence to drive crop diversity leveraging a digital platform to 
not only monitor the farm land but also aggregate farmers on such pla tform to ensure 
that the desired end objective is achieved. The ultimate success parameter will be 
impact it creates own farmer’s income. Monitoring alone won’t suffice unless some of 
the key objectives are met. The ultimate objective remains making farmin g sustainable 
and profitable as a result of which farmers income shall improve. 
3.4 Climate Change 
Climate change is another concern which is already reality. Weather risk management 
becomes extremely critical to manage otherwise it potentially wipe out all the gains of 
past we have made. Agriculture is one of the most vulnerable sectors as it depends 
heavily on weather conditions. Such platform which also aggregate farmers in due 
course would also help in weathering the weather risk.
