Project wise detailed description  
1. Crop Health Monitoring  
 
 
 
 
Sub-Project 
title 
Crop Health Monitoring 
Input 
technologies 
Remote sensing, GIS, Image recognition, Data Analytics, Artificial  
Intelligence, Machine Learning etc enabled crop health monitoring 
and production system. 
Problem 
statement 
Agriculture is a complex and multi -disciplinary system and a data  
driven approach can increase farmers’ incomes while ensuring the  
sustainability goals of water, soil, and climate. Crop health  issues 
are much prevalent in the country, leading farmers to use 
excessive fertilizers and pesticides, incurring much input cost and  
resource wastage. Crop health issues include diseases, pests, and  
micro- nutrient deficiencies, for which timely detection and 
monitoring would be required to reduce its impact on yield and  
productivity of the crops. 
Solutions 
components 
Timely detection of crop stress issues (caused by deficiencies, 
pests, diseases etc) and adopting appropriate mitigation strategies 
would help farmers to address the root cause and maintain yield.  
This would be helpful in reducing costs of cultivation. 
The pilot will involve use of combination of technologies such as  
geospatial application for weather and crop health monitoring, 
geo- fencing of target plots, use of an integrated application to  
capture ground data on plant growth, weeds, pest and diseases  
from the field. The app will be used to deliver custom advisory to  
the farmer; or it can be deployed using VLEs to cover a larger  
group of farmers The pilot will also involve data collection and  
annotation of weeds, pests and diseases common to the selected  
crops to enable the farmers to spot them in the field, and plan  
mitigation strategies that can reduce input costs. The AI-based app 
should be able to predict 
 
P a g e  | 15 
 
 
 Estimated yield based on the cropping conditions. 
Scope Pilot in 20-30 acres of land maize and cotton crop (The majorly 
cultivated crops in the state other that rice) 
Impact • Integrated crop health monitoring using emerging technology  
will be standardized and piloted, which could be scaled up to  
other parts of the state and beyond 
• Reduce input costs using data-driven decision-making 
approach for early detection of crop health issues and timely  
remediation. Reduces dependence on chemical -based solutions 
and improve the crop production and productivity.

2. Post-harvest Moisture Prediction  
 
 
 
 
Sub-Projecttitle Farm-based Crop Quality Assessment using Machine Learning to 
de-risk Farmers 
Input 
technologies 
Using nonparametric statistical machine learning models for the 
identification of moisture inside maize and groundnut grains. 
Problem 
statement 
The focus grain for this project is maize and Groundnut, which are  
one of the largest and climate-resilient agricultural products in 
India. The maize market  relies on the grade and shelf life of the  
grain for its pricing and processing. The grade is determined by  
identifying the size, color, percentage of inert materials, and 
fungus infection. The shelf-life is determined by measuring the 
moisture content inside the maize grain. Currently, the assessment  
of these parameters is done subjectively, and the only non- 
subjective process is the rare usage of the moisture meter in the  
buyer’s warehouse. In this entire cycle, neither the farmer nor the  
buyer is  sure about these quality parameters. There is close to  
22% loss after harvest of maize. 7-8% at farm level, 2-3% at 
logistics process level and 11-12% at warehouse of the buyers 
cumulatively 22-23% of loss. Agri tech startups are developing and  
validating innovative product using machine learning models to  
effectively predict these quality parameters and provide the 
information to farmers and buyers directly from the farm level. By  
using this product – based on a specially developed mobile 
application, farmers get a fair price, and the buyers can rely on 
the quality of the maize using this information. 
The project plan of this proposal is to validate the developed 
moisture estimation  model to ensure high statistical effectiveness 
to estimate the quality of the maize/groundnut objectively. 
 
P a g e  | 17 
 
 
  
Solutions 
components 
Moisture inside the grain is related with the dryness and wetness  
of the  environment. Therefore, location-based temperature and 
rainfall data are collected and compare these data with the 
collected crop moisture data in seeds that exact location and time  
from the farms. For example, 30 years of daily rainfall and 
temperature data of each location are collected in a 20 m2 grid. 
To compare with these weather variables, collection of the 
maize/groundnut moisture data manually using the moisture 
meter. Preliminary Data analysis vise moisture prediction using  
weather variables is reported to have 80% correlation with the  
manually collected ground data and nearly 15% growth in farm  
income per acre estimated on preliminary basis. Larger data sets  
may be needed to improve the model for a better estimation. 
Scope A focus on farm-based (post-harvest) quality assessment o 
maize/groundnut helps to de -risk the challenges of the farmers by  
providing them better opportunity to improve their livelihood 
through a valid opportunity to price their produce. It helps a 
objective knowledge of quality of their produce and brings better  
base for negotiation process with buyers. 
Impact Useful as the quality information of maize/groundnut available 
from the farm level for both farmers and wholesale buyers. The  
exact quality information is tagged for each lot, which becomes one 
of the most useful information for the buyers and processing units  
who need to strategically sort their lots for either further sales  
storage, or immediate processing. The quality parameters are 
useful with the inclusion of moisture and when the amount of 
moisture is known directly at the farm level, quality estimation for  
buyers and valid pricing for the farmers may be more objectively  
based. The entire process can de -risk both farmers and buyers and  
at the same time can reduce the loss of  crop due  to unreliable  
knowledge of grade, moisture, and fungus levels. Since higher 
moisture leads to higher fungal infection and lower shelf life, 
 
P a g e  | 18 
 
 
 Traceability of the produce quality from farm to processing units  
provides unique and important value for the entire product chain  
inthe market. 
Overall, the challenges faced by both farmers and buyers are 
reduced and financial growth of the farmers is ensured with a valid 
pricing based on objective quality assessment.

3. Traceability for Tandoor Dal (GI tagged)  
 
 
Sub-Projecttitle Block chain technology enabled traceability system for Tandur GI 
Tur Dal 
Input 
technologies 
The technology framework solution comprises of a combination of, 
(1) Online cloud connected app to be used on mobiles and tablet 
for data collection, and (2) Block chain integration to secure the  
transactions for trust & transparency. This also includes data 
collection and block chain process of the collected data and 
transactions along the process also helping reaching the export 
markets. 
Problem 
statement 
The red gram from Tandur region is a superior variety gram 
among red grams due to its higher protein content and the 
superior soil conditions of Tandur region. However, the superior  
value of the produce does not monetarily materialize for the 
farmers of Tandur region. Using block chain technology for red  
gram seed traceability in Tandur region will ensure that Tandur  
farmers obtain attractive procurement price as well as properly  
labeled and traceable high quality produce availability of healthy  
food in the market at affordable prices. 
In recent years, it has been observed that some farmers have lost  
their crops, even their lives, because of low-quality seeds. The 
source of these seeds and their trustworthiness is highly uncertain.  
Healthier crops start from healthy seeds and lead to high quality  
crop yield. When farmers have access to high -quality, strong, and  
disease and pest -resistant seeds, health of the crop yield and  also 
its value is positively impacted. Low and spurious quality seeds,  
which cannot be traced back to their source directly, impact 
 
 
P a g e  | 20 
 
 
 farmers’ income negatively and it happens primarily because Agri- 
companies are unable to trace the seed to its source. 
Solutions 
components 
Block chain technology provides an innovative solution for end -to- 
end traceability. It provides transparency among all involved 
parties and facilitates the collection of reliable data. The proposed  
block chain solution can facilitate end-to-end visibility and 
traceability for farmers, businesses and the end-consumer 
Traceability of Tadnur Dal using Block chain technology does 
provide a more transparent and more secure process to trace the  
quality of produce to their source i.e., GI Tag. 
With digitization and traceability in the industry, there will be 
authentication to the geo -location of GI tagged Tandur Dal, which  
will lead to better market value of the produce which will translate  
to better earnings for the farmers and more importantly Export  
Earnings. 
Scope Block chain enables the Traceability of products, which helps the  
consumer be informed about the complete information about 
source, Geo location from where it is produced, farmer details, a  
package of practices followed to raise the crop, processing, 
monitoring by scientific teams, and finally packaging. This is 
anend- to-end traceability solution which traces the food from the  
crop to agricultural produce going up to the end-consumer 
andcreating an export linkage. 
Impact Block chain traceability solutions can help to improve the overall  
traceability of Tandur dal, by providing a secure, immutable record  
of all production, processing, and distribution steps taken. This 
will help to ensure that the dal is produced according to the 
standards of the GI tag and that any irregularities, such as mixing 
of other varieties or adulteration, can be easily identified and 
prevented. Block chain technology ensures traceability and 
transparency inseed production.

5. Agri Sandbox  
 
 
 
Sub-Projecttitle Digital Agri Sandbox 
Input 
technologies 
AI and ML, IoT, Mobile Application, Block chain, Drones 
Problem 
statement 
Telangana has been progressively promoting use of digital 
agriculture technologies such as AI and ML, IoT or block chain for  
smart farming, pest management or quality assessment use -cases. 
It is evident that these solutions hold the potential of transforming  
the agriculture sector and enhance farmer’s access to scientific 
advisories. However, while deploying these solutions on the 
ground, the question of “what if something goes wrong” is ever - 
present as the implications of a failed solution can be massive for  
farmers. Unlike many traditional technologies in agriculture, the  
Agri tech solutions are still in developing stage and no or limited  
empirical evidence is available on the accuracy of their output. 
Solutions 
components 
To address this complex situation where farmers’ interest is to be  
safeguarded while enabling the Agri tech ecosystem and 
encouraging innovations, need of a Digital Agri Sandbox is felt to  
incubate and validate Agri tech solutions. The standard definition  
of Sandbox is an environment for testing, validation, and 
certification of a solution from multiple dimensions. In case of 
agriculture, the isolated environment will be at three levels 
including technology lab, agriculture research facilities and test 
farms where agri  tech solutions can be deployed and tested for  
accuracy of outcomes based on set of test data sets. Considering  
start-ups are at different levels of business and technology 
maturity, role of sandbox will be: 
i. Product development support, customized scientific/  
business mentoring, advisory on regulatory issues, go to 
 
P a g e  | 23 
 
 
 market assistance, fund raising assistance for POC or 
early-stage start-ups. 
ii. Early/ growth stage agri  tech start-ups to pilot/ validate  
the start-up technology through a soft-landing platform 
at real farm situation including client interaction and  
user acceptance testing alongside other areas of 
validation. This would include bottom-up approach to 
building business models at the local level. 
iii. For early/ growth stage start-ups to collaborate, co- 
create and co-develop technology offering (products/ 
services) jointly between a start-up team and the 
agricultural scientists in the respective domains 
iv. Acceleration support to growth stage start-ups. 
Objectives 
i. To act as a validating platform for Agri tech solutions to  
go-on ground and engage with farmers or be part of the  
Public Private Partnership with governments. 
ii. To foster innovations in agri tech sector while 
minimizingrisks to the consumers 
iii. To provide a regulated access to test/validation data to  
startups, regulate with certain boundary  conditions and  
in a controlled environment. 
Scope Government of Telangana shall establish nation’s first Agritech 
Sandbox at AgHub, Professor Jayashankar Telangana State 
Agriculture University. In the initial pilot year, 10 solutions wil 
be scouted through wide publicity and pitch announcements on 
specific set of problem statements as identified by Sandbox’s 
Steering Board. The support of ecosystem partners in State o 
Telangana (RICH, Department of Emerging Technologies, 
TSIC/T-Hub, NRSA, State Department of 
Agriculture/Horticulture/Commodity Boards)  will be an 
essential part of the pitch platform. The technologies from fina 
selected startups will be designed for validation in accurate 
interface of farmer-agri-scientists –startup in real-farm situation 
 
 
P a g e  | 24 
 
 
 for at least one season 
Impact Sandbox will foster innovations and create an opportunity for  all 
the stakeholders to gain firsthand empirical evidence of outcomes  
and possible risks of a particular solution. This will allow 
considerable data for State Governments to enable different 
solutions while containing the risks. At the same time for start -ups, 
sandbox will allow innovators to test their solutions with limited  
costs, at real-farm level with technical expertise from agri- 
domain. Such a pilot will also help manage reputation risks 
associated with pilots before large-scale adoption of the 
technology. Most importantly, it will safeguard interest and build  
trust, competencies of farmers and all other stakeholders in agri - 
food value chain while ensuring they have access to innovations.  
Such a sandbox will also drive innovations to the State, as 
innovators would get domain knowledge and test environment 
which is most often challenging for agri entrepreneurs or agtech  
startups with limited funds.

6. Agri Photo voltaics  
 
 
 
Sub-Project 
title 
Solar Energy as 3rd Crop for Farmer with AI analytics for solar panel 
monitoring 
Input 
technologies 
AI based solar panel monitoring. 
Problem 
statement 
Solar panels in the market are completely opaque and hence the  
shadow underneath make the land underneath the solar panel 
unsuitable for agricultural cultivation. And  it’s due to  this reason  
that solar farms are primarily installed in large arid lands. 
The problem with this approach is there  will be expected shortage  
in arid lands if the country were to achieve the carbon neutral goals 
of 450 GW of renewable during the next decade. It is expected that  
as much as land needed for this is the size of the state of Sikkim and  
for achieving 5000 GW by 2070 the  land needed is the size of the  
state of West Bengal 
Solutions 
components 
Innovative transparent panel for Agri Photovoltaics that can 
enable a farmer to use his 100% land for cultivation and at the same  
time have 100% of the same land for solar energy generation 
Solutions to promote dual usage of land with innovative AI based  
solar panel technology to enable more sunlight to penetrate the  
ground underneath and allow production of crops underneath solar  
panels. It is well known that the plastic sheet on on the backside of  
the panel in currently available models of solar panels hinders ligh t 
and no crops can be grown in the shadow region. 
Through the proposed solution of specially designed panel with AI  
monitored Panel, it is proposed to develop a model wherein the  
farmer may lease his land to the solar developers and get a steady  
income for renting this land from the developers and also be able to 
 
P a g e  | 26 
 
 
 grow suitable crops with economic returns 
Scope Currently in place panels in Telangana have an energy density 
(based on number of panels were laid out in the Agri PV plot) 
andpotentially give Rs 14,000/acre/annum. Now with that is 
proposedto enhance to a denser layout with increased number of 
panels andboost this income to Rs 28,000/acre/annum. The AI 
analytics wil ensure that the panels are regularly maintained 
and achieveconsistent performance through the panel lifetime. 
The scope is to increase the area and establish the value 
propositionfor farmers. 
Impact Current results of potentially increase in the farmer income by 20%  
only from the solar energy generated in that land boosted to 40%  
farmer income through the panel schematics. Also, the AI  analytics 
on the panel will constantly monitor the energy generation and 
alert the maintenance official of any poor performing panel, 
cleaning schedule alerts etc . Timely monitoring of the panels and  
isolating faulty systems can boost the overall energy generation in  
the panels by 5-10% more. 
Thus, it can be mentioned that this technology seeks to significantly  
impact the life of farmers in India by augmenting their income with  
this technology while going about their regular agricultural 
activitiesin Telangana.

7. Smart Farming  
 
Sub-Project 
title 
Smart Farming - Robotics 
Input 
technologies 
Robotics, AI, IoT 
Problem 
statement 
Most of the agricultural operations involve human labor, which is  
currently in short supply, time -consuming and expensive. Also, some  
of the operations such as pesticide spraying pose a serious  health 
hazard to the laborer. These all contribute to an increased  cost of  
cultivation that reduces the farm income for the grower Some of the  
tasks can be automated to free up time for the farmer and offers off - 
farm employment opportunities for rural labor force. 
Solutions 
components 
Implementation of autonomous ag-robotic models for sowing 
localized spraying of pesticides, fertilizer placement, and imaging- 
based weeding. The bots would relay data back to the cloud fo 
visualization of crop calendar, crop health status, job monitoringvia 
a smart phone application. 
Scope Pilot in one district in Telangana; 100 ac each for cotton and chilli 
Impact ● Address problem of farm labor unavailability 
● GPS controlled robots ensures easy and precise application of 
pesticides and herbicides 
● Placement of fertilizers at the root zone, reduces wastage 
● Overall reduction in cost of cultivation 
• Scaling up model across the state. 
Impact ● Address problem of farm labor unavailability 
● GPS controlled robots ensures easy and precise application of 
pesticides and herbicides  
● Placement of fertilizers at the root zone, reduces wastage

P a g e  | 28 
 
 
8.Spot Spraying with Drones 
Sub-Project 
title 
Spot Spraying with Drones 
Input 
technologies 
Drones, AI, ML, Remote sensing and GIS technology, and app 
integration. 
Problem 
statement 
Crop weed management is a global challenge. Weed pressure in the field  
decreases crop yield as a result of resource competition. To combat 
weeds and protect their crops, farmers are increasingly adopting 
herbicides in farm management. However, overuse of herbicides not 
only increases farmers’ operational costs but also undermines the health  
of the ecosystem. Many countries have set a goal to reduce agricultural  
chemical usage. 
Solutions 
components 
The pilot focus on deploying Multispectral drone over the field to collect  
multispectral images for processing it based  on the database of weeds  
in chilli and cotton along with  ground truthing to generate an indices - 
based map from these images. Based on the indices map, a prescription  
map is generated for the spot application of weedicides depending up on  
the infection density. The pilot also focuses on monitoring the drone - 
based spraying over the manual operations. This will help in effectively  
controlling the weed population and reducing the cost incurred in 
chemical spraying. 
Scope Pilot in one district in Telangana; 25 ac each for cotton and chilli 
Impact ● Address problem of farm labor unavailability for weeding 
● Spot application of herbicide to control weeds; determiningthreshold 
and reducing input costs 
• Scaling up model across the state.

INTRODUCTION
Agriculture is one of the most
important occupations for the people of
Union Territory of Puducherry. About 45%
of the total population of the UT depends
directly or indirectly on farming. The main
crop of this territory is paddy. The four
isolated territories of the Union Territory
of Puducherry are scattered over
different agro climatic zones in the States
of Tamilnadu, Andhra Pradesh and Kerala
and hence have varying cropping
patterns.
While multiple cropping and a high cropping intensity is possible in Pondicherry
region due to the favorable soil and climatic conditions, a homogenous cropping pattern
with paddy being the predominant crop followed by rice fallow pulses prevails in
Karaikal region. Mahe and Yanam regions are tiny territories together accounting for
less than 6% of the net area sown.Agriculture sector contributes less than 2% of the Gross
State Domestic Product (GSDP) of the Union Territory of Puducherry.
During the last five decades the Union Territory of Puducherry has made
significant strides in the field of Agriculture by increasing the productivity of agricultural
land, achieving crop diversification, exploiting irrigation potential, building
infrastructure, creating a well united input and marketing network etc.

1971 Establishment of t he Farmers' Training Centre and the Government
Agricultural Engineering Workshop
1972 Establishment of the Quality Control Laboratories for Seeds and Fertilizers

EXTENT & GEOGRAPHICAL LOCATION
The Union Territory of Puducherry consists of four
small unconnected regions: Puducherry, Karaikal and
Yanam on the Bay of Bengal and Mahé on the Arabian
Sea. Puducherry and Karaikal have the largest areas and
population, and are both enclaves of Tamil Nadu.
Yanam and Mahé are enclaves of Andhra Pradesh and
Kerala respectively.
The territory has an area of 492 square kilometres (190 sq miles): Puducherry
293 km² and more than a 200 km² of Tamil Nadu area in and around surrounded by
puducherry region, Karaikal 160 km², Mahé 9 km² and Yanam 30 km². Its 2011
population was 1,244,464.

CLIMATE
The Pondicherry and Karaikal regions have a temperature range of 24 0 to 33.2 0
Celsius with an average annual rainfall of 1336 mm in Puducherry and 1386 mm in Karaikal,
most of which is received during the north -east monsoon. The Mahe receives an average

IRRIGATION
During the sixties, surface water
bodies like tanks, wells and ponds played a
significant role in augmenting irrigation in
Puducherry region. Besides the two major
tanks viz., Oosuteri and Bahour, there were 84
surface water storage irrigation tanks with
total command area of 6765 hectares and with
a water holding capacity of 46.4 mcm. There
were also 140 small tanks for harvesting
surface water.
Due to appropriation of the tank areasfor agricultural activities, their water holding
capacity had been gradually reduced. Consequently, dependence on ground water has
grown at a fast pace over the last 20 years increasing the number of bore wells to about
7000 from around 1000 in the early sixt ies and the irrigation by surface water source has
become almost nil since 1985. Nearly 90% of the net cropped area in Pondicherry is now
under irrigation through ground water.
Being located in the Cauvery River ayacut system, canal irrigation through th e
Mettur Dam in the main source of irrigation in Karaikal region. The irrigated area in both
Puducherry and Karaikal regions amount to about 90% of net cropped area. Yanam region
is irrigated by river Godavari and a copious rainfall covers Mahe region.
IMPORTANT CROPS
The principal food crops are paddy, bajra (cumbu), ragi, and pulses
(Greengram, Blackgram and mothbean, cowpea). The cash crops include cotton,
sugarcane, oilseeds, coconut, gingelly and chillies. The important h orticultural products
are bananas, mangoes and vegetables. The plantation wealth of the UT lies in Mahe region
mainly. Crops like coconuts, arcanuts, condiments and spices are grown in less quantity.
Paddy, Pulses, groundnut, Chillies and Coconut are other crops grown in Yanam.

VISION STATEMENT
To leverage ICT for improving productivity, sustain the growth rate of agriculture
in the U.T. of Puducherry, enhance the economic status of the farming community by
overcoming constraints with increased technological intervention and establishment of a
more efficient system for delivery of extension services.
MILESTONES IN THE INTEGRATION OF ICT FOR AGRICULTURAL DEVELOPMENT
FARM HELP CENTERS
In the wake of competition due to the liberalization of economic policies, the
Department of Agriculture, Puducherry revamped the extension system to help the
farming community to shift to a system that suits the market.  This led to the introduction
of the scheme Farm Help Centers (“Uzhavar Udhaviyagams” in vernacular parlance) for
providing information on all facets of Agriculture to enable the farmers to take informed
decisions.  The main aim of these Farm Help Centres is to function as information hubsand
also to deliver effective extension services at a single point. The aspect of timely and
relevant information, especially with the role of information communication and
technology to connect farmers with the information they need and management
information systems to monitor and implement schemes was attached importance . In
short, to keep pace with the developments, seamless blending of Information and
communication technology with agriculture was felt imperative. All the farm help centers
were provided with connectivity and all information services were extended. Instant Soil
Testing kits were also provided to the farmers.
LAUNCH OF PORTAL
The Department forayed into the IT initiatives during the year 2002 with the
launching of the portal for Agricul ture, in the vernacular language .  At that point of time,
even most of the Universities and Departments didn’t had any exclusive portal on
Agriculture.  This Department collected and collated as much as information as possible on
Agriculture, particularly, the publications of Tamil Nadu Agricultural University,
Coimbatore, whose recommendations are followed for Puducherry, translated all the
content and the information spanning around 1500 HTML pages was launched on July 4,
2003. Downloadable forms were provided for easy access to the resources. All the
schemes were published in the portal for social audit and still continued.

WIDE AREA NETWORK
On the same day, the technological intervention was taken further with the
introduction of an exclusive network with the dedicated leased lines connecting two farm
help centres each in Puducherry and Karaikal regions with the headquarters and it was also
expanded in the subsequent years. With the introduction of the concept of broadband in
2005, the network was disbanded and now, all the Offices of this Department are under
the optical fibre connectivity with 100 MBPS bandwidth through BSNL, the State Service
Provider.
DEVELOPMENT OF WEB APPLICATIONS AND MOBILE APP UNDER NeGP-A
The Department of Agriculture proposed to convert the existing Farmers Help
Centers into e - farm centers and where ICT concepts that could supplement and leverage
the extension machinery. As a corollary, various applications are proposed covering G2C,
G2B and G2G services were proposed to take the technology further for easy and swift
delivery of services.
I. DIGITIZATION OF FARM HOLDINGS
Digitizing the farm holdings of all the four regions of the U.T.
II. SMART FARMER IDENTITY CARDS
A QR code based farmer identity card with a unique code to track the
personal and farming profile of an individual farmer and also track the history
of services availed
III. MIS ON EXTENSION AND SCHEME MANAGEMENT; INPUTS QUALITY CONTROL,
LICENSES AND AVAILABILITY MANAGEMENT – Applications on
o Extension Management Information System – to assess weekly crop
prospects and extension activities to plan and monitor
o Scheme Management Information System – for an effective MIS on
financial and progress reports
o Inputs Quality Control Management Information System– to have a MIS
on the fertilizer, insecticide and seed samples received for analysis and
their reports
o Inputs Licenses Management Information System – for a simple license
issue/renewal and
o Inputs Availability Management Information System– to keep a track on
the periodical report on the availability of agricultural inputs
IV. DEVELOPMENT OF MOBILE APPLICATION
o A bilingual mobile app to dispense information on schemes, subsidies
available, weather, availability of agricultural inputs, market information
etc.–bilingual

The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare New Delhi vide letter no.  Z -
11021/31/2020-IT dt. 02 -12-2021, conveyed that the GoI is giving a major push to Digital
Agriculture under the National e-Governance Plan in Agriculture (NeGP-A), a CSS, with an
objective to increase the income of the farmers. The major initiative of Government of
India is to support the State Governments to adopt new and emerging technologies such
as Artificial Intelligence (AI), Machine Learning (ML), Block chain and traceability, supply
chain platform, Agriculture Internet of Things (AIoT) based sensors and Robotics etc.,
Initially, it was proposed that a pilot project could be taken up at Perunthalaivar
Kamaraj Krishi VI gyan Kendra, Puducherry on “Design, Development, Implementation,
Operation and maintenance of farm operations ” in the Perunthalaivar Kamaraj Krishi
Vigyan Kendra, Puducherry. The proposal was submitted to the Secretary to Government
(Agriculture), Puducher ry stating that the Department of Information Technology,
Puducherry could be requested to shortlist a Consultant and after approval of the
Competent Authority, the consultant could be entrusted with the preparation of BPR,
DPRs, framing of RFP for tender,scrutinize and shortlist a vendor and further monitor the
execution of project and certify for its compliance to the RFP and DPR.
The proposal referred to the Secretary to Government (Agriculture), Puducherry
was further referred to the Secretary to Government (IT), Puducherry and on perusal, the
Secretary to Government (IT), Puducherry was pleased to refer the same to the State e -
Governance Mission Team (Se -MT) of the Department of IT, Puducherry. The SeMT
desired to have discussion with officials of this Department to study the scope of projects
enlisted by the GoI and to make suitable recommendations.
After discussions spread over five rounds and two field visits with the team of
SeMT, Puducherry along with senior Officials of the Department,the original proposal was
enlarged and the following three projects were proposed. Further to that the draft DPR
was shared to the SeMT of It, Puducherry and requested to work out the tentative
estimates. The details of the three projects are as follows.

KRUSHAK ODISHA 
Odisha’s Farmer Database 
DETAILED PROJECT REPORT (DPR)-PHASE-II 
 
 
 
 
 
 
(INTERVENTION UNDER NeGP-A ACTION PLAN-2021-22) 
 
 
  
 
Department of Agriculture and Farmers’ Empowerment 
GOVERNMENT OF ODISHA    
1. Background 
Odisha is a state in which majority of its population is dependent on agriculture for livelihood. It is essential 
that the state has a transparent view of all farmers of the state including cultivators, labourers and 
sharecroppers. This is important for various reasons, from streamlining schemes and service delivery to long-
term farmers’ welfare, among others. 
Therefore, while the need for a transparent and holistic view exists and cannot be questioned, existing systems 
and databases developed as part of various projects have only been able to create siloed lists of farmers from 
all over the state.  
To consolidate these lists, preliminary steps were taken as part of the KALIA (Krushak Assistance for Livelihood 
and Income Augmentation) Scheme launched by the Government of Odisha in December, 2018. As a result of 
the extensive data unification and verification exercises undertaken for preparation of the final beneficiary lists 
under KALIA, the state has been able to create an authentic database of roughly 50 lakh Small and Marginal 
Farmers and Landless Agricultural Households.  
To further leverage this base farmer list prepared under KALIA, the Department of Agriculture and Farmers’ 
Empowerment (DA&FE), Government of Odisha has been entrusted with the task of creation of a Unified 
Farmers’ Database for the state as one of the activities outlined under the Hon’ble Chief Minister’s vision of 5T 
– Technology, Teamwork, Transparency, Time and Transformation.  
The data set, so developed will act as a bridge and will supplement the effort of GOI for development of a 
federated data base of the land holding farmers and consequent feeder to the IDEA tech-architecture. 
2. Document Scope & Objective 
This document is a detailed project report for building Odisha’s Farmer Database (OFD).  
Topics Covered 
The document is divided into the following four parts –  
● Problem Statement: This part explains the problem statement that is being solved 
● Vision and Use cases: This part explains the broad vision and use cases that will be enabled till April, 
2025 
● Scope and Concepts: This part explains the concept framework along with relevant tech stack for 
development of the farmer database 
● Implementation Plan: This part explains the phased-out development and rollout plan till April 2025 
● Financial Requirement: This part explains the estimated financial requirement for the rolling out of the 
plan . 
3. Problem Statement 
Considering existing Farmer ID and other siloed dataset  in Odisha, in the current scenario there are multiple 
challenges faced by both the primary stakeholders i.e. farmers and the state.  
Some of the broader challenges that farmers’ face are as follows: 
 
● Multiple Farmer Registration Platforms (Farmer ID/PMFBY/P-PAS/MIDH/PMKSY/RAD) – There 
are multiple platforms on which a farmer registers today. Each portal generates a separate 
identification number for the farmer. Not only does this lead to an additional hassle for the farmer 
but also has led to the creation of information silos and duplicate datasets.  
 
● Lack of recognition of landless and tenant farmers – The current databases do not recognize a 
large chunk of sharecroppers and landless labourers, and even if they do, such categorization is not 
recorded in the system. Through KALIA, the state made its first attempt in this direction.  
● No centralized mechanism to check eligibility for a particular scheme/service – There are 95 
schemes for farmers, currently operational in Odisha including Central Sector, Centrally Sponsored 
and State Plan schemes. It is extremely difficult for farmers to check their eligibility for any of these 
schemes given the absence of a proper mechanism.  
● Low chances of customized advisory due to incomplete information – The absence of a single 
database with the contact details of all farmers prevents the services such as Ama Krushi and m-
Kisan being implemented comprehensively. This prevents farmers from receiving customized, on-
time and valuable advisory.  
In a similar way, there are challenges faced by the state: 
● Duplicate/Ghost records due to poorly verified and disconnected databases – In the past few 
years, the state has taken multiple steps to digitize information sets, however that has led to 
creation of information silos and therefore, duplication of records. There is a need to verify and 
consolidate all the available datasets into a unified database so that it can be utilized by all the 
departments. 
 
● No mechanism to estimate the number of beneficiaries for a scheme – Prior to KALIA, there was 
no data-backed mechanism for the state to estimate the number of beneficiaries eligible under a 
particular scheme. Each registration was driven by proofs submitted by the farmer and thereafter 
verified by the relevant government official. This entire process was time-consuming, costly and 
still left a substantial room for error. 
 
● Low usage of farmer level insights in policy formulation and decision making – Because of 
unavailability of consolidated farmer level information at the state level, there is a potential gap in 
state formulated agricultural policies.  
 
Please note, the above-mentioned list of challenges is not exhaustive and is only indicative.  
 
4. Vision 
The vision of Odisha’s Farmer Database is to create a golden record of farmers in the state to solve the 
problems outlined above and enable the following use cases – 
4.1. Identity Proof 
 Enrolment of all the farmers in Odisha onto a unified platform, will reduce a lot of hassles for farmers while 
availing different schemes and services. A Farmer ID card with a secure QR code will be generated for all 
farmers to streamline processes, and enable a hassle-free experience for them. It will also prevent any leakages 
in the system. 
4.2. Digital Governance (Integration with land records and other farmer systems) 
To avail any government scheme/service, there are multiple documents that are required to be 
submitted/uploaded by the citizen/farmer as proofs. It is important to note that most of these documents that 
are required as proofs, are often owned by other government departments only. However, the two systems 
exist in silos and in most cases, do not interact. 
On the other hand, the triangulation of data (interconnected data systems) is a very powerful tool, to check 
for anomalies/fraud detection. 
On similar lines, in the current scenario, the verification/authentication of beneficiaries during service delivery 
is largely left to the ability of field officers. This creates a lot of room for error. Farmer authentication through 
Image recognition is being planned for a robust authentication mechanism and to prevent any leakages in 
the system.  
Therefore, on one hand to make the process hassle-free for end beneficiaries and on the other to enable 
effective fraud/anomaly detection, it is important that an interconnected ecosystem be created, in which all 
relevant data systems co-create, co-exist and at all points in time interact.   
Odisha’s farmer database will be connected to all the existing systems at state and central levels, especially 
Bhulekh - Odisha’s land records and the National Farmer Database. Thus, an interconnected ecosystem will be 
created to enable triangulation of data and therefore, digital governance.   
4.3. Scheme Dissemination 
Across the three directorates of the Department of Agriculture, Government of Odisha, there are 80+ schemes 
across the year. It is important to track the delivery of these schemes and record progress/achievement in real-
time. This would not only help in getting a transparent view of the current status but also help in taking 
corrective action, wherever required.  
Odisha’s farmer database, supported by DAFE’s Decision Support System will enable online target allocation 
and real-time achievement submission across levels, making review and monitoring of progress transparent 
and data-backed.  
4.4. Geo-tagging, Geo-fencing and Beneficiary tagging 
The entire agricultural extension system is involved in last mile delivery of various schemes, including state and 
central. The Government of Odisha has a mobile application ‘Agri-extension App’ which is already being used 
by all the 4000+ extension officers to geo-tag and mark attendance for weekly Gram Panchayat sitting and 
farmer query resolution. The same application will be linked to Farmer Database and further be enabled with 
geo-tagging, geo-fencing and beneficiary tagging across schemes for various activities including field 
demonstrations, plantations, input/minikit distribution, execution of structures, etc. 
4.5. Customized Agro-advisory dissemination 
While Agriculture is a common phenomenon in India, there are different problems that farmers face on a daily 
basis. A blanket solution is not going to work for them.  
Odisha’s Farmer Database will form the base to enable such customized advisory that will be further 
disseminated by the state’s Ama Krushi advisory and helpline service.  
Scope and Concepts 
4.6. Design Principles 
The following design principles have and will guide the creation of the OFD’s technical architecture.  
 
 Creation of modular, reusable, scalable, extensible & deployable technology products using open-
source tools 
a. Modular - Can be configured by wiring together multiple open source tools  
b. Reusable - Can be reused with minimal effort for similar use cases 
c. Scalable - Can work well as number of end users begin to rapidly scale 
d. Extensible - Feature additions can be made on the products with ease 
 To leverage and integrate with existing government systems 
4.7. OFD Framework  
Keeping the design principles as a base, the overall framework diagram for OFD is as shown below -  
 
 
As per the framework, the entire concept of OFD can be divided into various components -  
 State Farmer Database: The central circle of State Farmer Database, with an external four part 
verification layer and internal Confidence Level algorithm and Anomaly Detection engine 
 Farmer’s Portal: Krushak Odisha web portal and Agri-extension mobile application for enrolment and 
verification 
 Farmer Systems: Integration with Bhulekh  (Land Records) and all the other existing transactional 
databases 
 Features and Enhancements: Communication & rule engine, GIS, Vernacular support, search algorithm, 
visualisation and analytics will be enabled through Department’s Decision Support System (DSS) 
Platform  
 Micro-services: Licensed API-services for enabling use-cases 
4.8. Definition 
To build a robust Farmer Database, it is essential to define an individual farmer.  
For the purposes of Odisha's Farmers’ Database, a farmer would include cultivators (including sharecroppers) 
and agricultural laborers, who may or may not own land, and are engaged in any of the following activities - 
Crop Production, Animal Production, Forestry and Fishing/Aquaculture. 
4.9. Database Record Types  
OFD will store two types of records –  
● Individual data - OFD will store individual farmers’ data. Aadhaar will be used to uniquely identify an 
individual. 
● Family structure - OFD will group farmers into a family and identify their relationships with each other. 
An internally generated ‘family ID*’ (or Ration no. if found in NFSA Database) will identify whether all 
farmers are part of one family while ‘position in family tree’ attribute will determine his/her 
relationships with others. 

4.10. Database Attributes 
After the analysis of farmer registration forms for schemes and services such as Farmer ID, KALIA, PM KISAN, m-
Kisan and PMFBY, it was observed that most of these forms have similar fields, except few fields relevant to 
the specific scheme.  
OFD will store the following attributes of a farmer (Details in Annexure I) –  
# Attribute  # Attributes 
1 Farmer Name 12 Residential Address 
2 Gender 13 Assets - Land Details including geo-coordinates 
3 Date of Birth / Year of Birth 14 Assets - Crop Details 
4 Community 15 Whether Sharecropper 
5 Marital Status 16 Other Profession 
6 Mobile number 17 Bank Details 
7 Email ID 18 Aadhaar No. 
8 Photograph 19 Farmer ID 
9 Relative Name 20 Family ID 
10 Relation with Farmer 21 Position in family tree 
11 Relative Aadhaar No. 22 Farmer Consent 
Please note, new assets (similar to land and crops) can be added in future 
4.11. OFD ID Format 
Please note, for all the landowning farmers i.e. part of the National Farmer Database, a centralised farmer ID as 
generated by the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare, Government of India will be used.  
Only in scenarios, such as for landless farmers, who are not covered under the National Farmer Database a 
state ID will be generated, in the following format - a 10 digit randomised alpha-numeric code. 
4.12. OFD ID Revision Principles 
Please note, once a farmer is allotted a unique ID, that ID cannot be revised/altered at any point in time in the 
future. In cases where a particular farmer expires/becomes ineligible, the same information may be recorded 
against that particular ID.   
4.13. OFD ID Card Components 
Following principles will govern the generation of digital copies of OFD ID Cards 
● To enable offline verification in the future, all ID Cards to have a secure QR code that can be scanned by 
the department QR code scanning application only 
● The QR code would store basic and static information of the farmer such as OFD ID, Farmer name*, 
Gender, Date of Birth and Photograph 
● Only static information is stored to prevent further generation of QR code 
● In case an existing static data-point is recorded incorrectly and therefore is updated by the farmer, a 
new QR code will be generated 
o Farmer will have to take a new print out of his/her Farmer ID card 
o The exercise will also include discarding the older QR code so that it becomes non-functional 
for any further verifications 
*Farmer Name, even though a slow-moving field has been added to the QR Code because it is fundamental to 
farmer identification

4.14. Database Connection and Sync Strategy 
In order to prevent the creation of information silos in the future, and cover all the multi-dimensional aspects 
of a farmer profile, it is important to create an integrated tech system i.e. link existing and relevant datasets to 
the OFD.  
There are four types of databases relevant in this context –  
● Registered list of farmers - Presently several farmer lists exist under various systems such as Farmer ID, 
KALIA, PMFBY, P-PAS, M-PAS, PMKSY, etc. Four farmer lists* have been shortlisted namely KALIA, 
PMFBY, FS&CW and Farmer ID because of the following reasons –  
o High degree of Aadhaar seeding 
o Most validated farmer lists 
o Cover majority farmers of the state 
 
As highlighted below, these four lists have been consolidated and a base list of OFD with approx. 81 
lakh unique Aadhaar numbers has been prepared.

Odisha

Name: Lorem Ipsum 
Gender : M  
 
 
  
KALIA 48 Lac  Farmers 
PMFBY 25 Lac  
 
FS&CW 15 Lac  
 
DBT 25 Lac  
 
81 lakh Unique Aadhaar numbers 
*Please note, in future, other farmer lists can be uploaded in a bulk manner  
 
● Bhulekh (Land Records) - Odisha’s land records i.e. Bhulekh will be linked to the Farmer Database to 
authenticate and maintain the actual ownership and size of landholdings. Preliminary work on this has 
already been done for PMFBY (Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana) Registration, where the land records 
have been integrated with PMFBY portal. This will further be expanded, to ensure all landowning 
farmers have land details recorded that are linked to and verified from Bhulekh. 
● Transactional data - Each recorded farmer has been transacting with entities in the agriculture 
ecosystem for activities such as purchase of inputs, sale of final produce, etc. However, currently all 
these transactions are recorded in different databases.  
o For Farmer authentication - All these transactional systems will be linked to the main farmer 
database for farmer authentication while transactions are being executed.  
o This authentication will happen through Aadhaar eKYC in the online environment and Face 
Recognition/Match (stored in the QR Code of the Farmer ID card) in the offline environment 
o Smart-Contracts - Odisha government has recently rolled out the BALARAM scheme 
(Bhoomihina Agriculturist Loan and Resources Augmentation Model) to finance tenant farmers 
on the basis of cultivation certificates. These cultivation certificates will be linked to OFD and 
will be converted into smart contracts in the future to enable faster service dissemination.  
▪ On similar lines, in the future other such G2F (Government to Farmer) agreements are 
planned to be done through smart contracts 
 
● Authentication Ecosystem - All farmer records will go through a layer of authentication before they are 
added to the farmer database. While some of the following checks are mandatory for inclusion in the 
database, others are optional -   
o Aadhaar Demographic/Biometric Authentication (Mandatory) 
o Field verification (Mandatory) 
o Mobile verification (Optional) 
o Liveness verification (Optional during enrolment; Mandatory for transactions) 
 
● Occupational data - A farmer may be a main worker or marginal worker, the implication of this being 
that he/she may take up additional occupations/professions apart from farming during any financial 
year.  
o This data point is relevant for creation of beneficiary lists under various schemes, where such 
other occupations are considered as exclusions 
o For this purpose, OFD will record other occupation of all the farmers and this will be 
maintained through a combination of existing databases and field verification 
o The following state databases will be linked to capture the other occupations of all farmers -  
▪ HRMS 
▪ Teachers 
▪ Police 
 
● National Farmer Database - The state farmer database will be integrated with the National Federated 
Farmer Database through micro-services to be provided by the state and consumed by the central 
team. This is to ensure that there is no duplication of work at state and central levels, and there is 
seamless flow of data between the two entities. Additionally, Farmer ID generated for the 
landowning farmers can be synced with the state database. Micro-services are explained in C2.10. 
4.15. Micro-services for Use-Case Enablement 
To enable OFD use-cases, the following micro-services will be made available: 
● Access to farmer data or/and aggregate counts as defined in the licensing agreement 
● Access specific to geography defined in the licensing agreement 
● A set of pre-defined/suggested data-points combination available to be selected from 
o Rule engine to further customise/create new access as per requirement 
4.16. Micro-services Licensing 
● Each license will be tied down to: 
o One entity 
o A set of applications 
o A list of use-cases for which data will be used 
● A user’s license will determine: 
o The micro-services are available to the user 
o The data within a micro-service available to the user 
o Usage-limit for each micro-service, if applicable 
● The scope of a license can be revised, whenever required   
4.17. Micro-services Terms-of-Usage 
● Micro-services can only be used 
o by licenses bodies  
o for licensed application 
o for licensed use-cases 
● Micro-services cannot be used for policing purposes

1. INTRODUCTION AND BACKGROUND: 
Agriculture is the mainstay of the economy in the state of Mizoram; more than 60 per cent of 
the total population depends on agriculture and allied activities. It is the ba ckbone of the 
economy, provides employment opportunities and helps reduce the  pressure of the increasing 
population . Even though the total net sown area has been increasing during the last decades, 
the share of  Gross State Domestic Product (GSDP) is onl y 14 percent and this is due to the 
old and traditional agricultural practices in the state. and there is a need to implement 
advanced agricultural practices to improve the GSDP of the State and livelihood of farmers. 
Digital Agriculture is a trending conc ept which is revolutionizing the agriculture field 
through the use of new and advanced technologies, integrated into one system. Technologies 
used include sensors, communication networks, Unmanned Aviation Systems (UAS), 
Artificial Intelligence (AI), robot ics and other advanced machinery and often draws on the 
principles of the Internet of Things (IoT). Each one of these brings something valuable to 
farming from data collection, to management and processing, as well as guidance and 
direction. This integrate d system offers new insights that enhance the ability to make 
decisions and subsequently implement them.   
Digital agriculture has the potential to make agriculture more productive, more consistent and 
to use time and resources more efficiently. This bring s critical advantages for farmers and 
wider social benefits across the world. It also enables organizations to share information 
across traditional industry boundaries to open up new, disruptive employment opportunities. 
2. OBJECTIVE OF THIS PROJECT: 
This pro ject is designed to increase efficiency and transparency in the Agriculture Sector 
across the state of Mizoram  while maximising utilisation and potential that is available. It 
will also enable efficient planning for further programmes and schemes while en hancing the 
capability to deliver benefits of Current Schemes as well. 
The Key Objectives are as follows: 
A. Reducing input cost and wastage by employing digital agriculture and precision 
farming techniques and technologies 
B. Soil and water survey, planning and management. 
C. Identification of ideal locations for creation of ponds for water harvesting as well as 
pisciculture 
D. Geo fenced database of every farm linked with its owner/tiller 
E. Quarterly UAV based inventory of farms in project area 
F. Platform for planning of  farm link roads, MSME cluster roads, storage infrastructure 
etc. 
G. Increasing productivity and better price with real time information exchange and 
access to market prices across the country and for export markets as well 
 
 
4 
H. Creation of an Online GIS Enabled ERP Platform with a robust Database, wherein the 
relevant data of every farmer and farm will be available so as to create a robust 
decision support system and address issues related to crop insurance, yield estimation 
and productivity enhancement activitie s. Decision making for farm roads for better 
access, can be done using this GIS platform. 
I. Development of Mobile Application for all farmers as well as administrator level app 
for village councils and officers in Mizo language  and  English which will not o nly 
provide useful techniques and access to market prices but also to receive weather 
information and pest advisories without the need for SMSs 
J. Development of Machine Learning Algorithms for crop varieties indigenious to the 
State using AI and Machine Learning

Selected Vendor will Supervise and provide fully trained, equipped and motivated Drone

1.Soil and water survey, planning and management: 
Using the high accuracy of drones (2.5 -5 CM GSD) versus 100 to 150 cm accuracy from 
satellite data, Accurate simulation of Hydrodynamics and water flows can enable the 
 
8 
management of water resources to a much higher level of efficiency. As Water resources 
are vital for both agricultural and the  general population of the State, this will enable the 
Dept to propose measures which can be implemented for better watershed management in 
the State. This will primarily impact the maintenance of the levels of surface and 
groundwater, which recharge the m any water bodies in the state. Further, rainwater 
harvesting, creation of artificial reservoirs and artificial recharge of groundwater is also 
vital, in order to effectively recharge the groundwater that has been depleted due to 
overuse and contamination. Further Planning can be executed for creation of Farm Level 
and Village Level Ponds so as to enable the Farmers to have additional Income while 
having a backup water supply. 
UAVs (drones) can play a vital role in providing a new dimension in agricultural 
management through providing an aerial perspective, which is far more advantageous than 
ground based surveys for planning and implementation purposes. These offer unique 
benefits in the acquisition of real time high resolution data with Geo -referenced mapping 
and imagery for better management and planning of agriculture and allied activities. 
Other than the imagery, which is clear enough to be analysed by Agri Scientists to find 
problematic areas (which will replace random field visits to Planned Field Visit s where 
Deficiencies in nutrition are observed or pest infestation is Suspected) soil Test Data will 
also be aggregated from the respective departments and co -related with the GIS Platform 
to be developed. 
 
2.Identification of Ideal Locations for Creation of Ponds for Water harvesting as well 
as Pisciculture. 
Pisciculture is land based activity for economic upliftment of the rural fish farmers, The 
Central Governments focus on development of inland fisheries will help in realizing the 
goal of doubling the i ncome of farmers. There is vast scope for development of fisheries 
by developing infrastructures like fish ponds by utilizing high amounts of surface run -off 
at hill slopes of study areas that will be providing nutritional security and livelihood 
support t o the rural population.   This type of aquaculture is found mostly in the ponds 
which are adjoining the main river course where favourable conditions like clayey soils 
exist. Fish farms are seen extensively in the middle parts of the watershed where extensi ve 
plain lands exist with clayey soils. Fish farms having considerably bigger size may also 
serve the sources for drip-irrigation.

7. USE OF AI/ML: 
Background of Artificial Intelligence 
Artificial intelligence is based on the principle that human intelligence can be defined in a 
way that a machine can easily mimic it and execute tasks, from the  simplest to those that are 
even more complex. The goals of artificial intelligence include learning, reasoning, and 
perception. Some examples include vision -recognition systems on self -driving cars, in the 
recommendation engines that suggest products you might like based on what you bought in 
the past, speech, and language recognition of the Siri virtual assistant on the Apple iPhone. 
The global population is expected to reach more than nine billion by 2050 which will require 
an increase in agricultural pr oduction by 70% to fulfill the demand. As the world population 
is increasing due to which land and  water resources become insufficient to continue the 
demand-supply chain. So, we need a smarter approach to become more efficient about how 
we farm and can be most productive 
Through this project we aim to create an AI based Computer Vision system for detection of 
anomalies, nutritional deficiencies, pest Infestation,etc in crop varieties that are 
Indigenious/Unique to Mizoram. 
Hi - Resolution Imagery and Data  acquired from the UAVs will be used to train the Neural 
Networks of the Created AI System to enable automated detection of the above problem 
statement over time. 
Applications of Artificial Intelligence in Agriculture 
The industry is turning to Artificial Intelligence technologies to help produce healthier crops, 
control pests, monitor soil, and growing conditions, organize data for farmers, help with the 
workload, and improve a wide range of agriculture -related tasks in the entire food supply 
chain. 
 
 
 
 
17 
Soil Monitoring 
Analyzing crop health by drones: the Department  will use drone -based Aerial imaging 
solutions for monitoring crop health. In this technique, the drone captures data from fields 
and then data is transferred  to the GIS Lab and analyzed by experts. 
This project will essentially develop algorithms to analyze the captured images and provide a 
detailed report containing the current health of the farm. It will also help the farmers to 
identify pests and beneficial bacteria biomes, helping farmers in  timely pest control and 
required action. 
Precision Farming and Predictive Analytics: AI applications in agriculture have developed 
applications and tools which help farmers by providing them proper guidance about water 
management, crop rotation, timely ha rvesting, type of crop to be grown, optimum planting, 
pest attack and nutrition management. 
While using the machine learning algorithms in connection with images captured by drones, 
AI-enabled technologies predict weather conditions and analyze crop sustai nability and 
evaluate farms for the presence of diseases or pests and poor plant nutrition with data like 
temperature, precipitation, wind speed, and solar radiation. 
Farmers without connectivity can get AI benefits right now, with tools as simple as an SM S-
enabled phone and the Sowing App. Meanwhile, farmers with Wi -Fi access can use AI 
applications to get a continually AI -customized plan for their lands. With such IoT - and AI-
driven solutions, farmers can meet the world’s needs for increased food sustaina bly growing 
production and revenues without depleting precious natural resources. 
In the future, AI developed will help farmers evolve into agricultural technologists, using 
data to optimize yields down to individual rows of plants 
AI-enabled system to det ect pests: Pests are one of the worst enemies of the farmers which 
damages crops.AI systems use UAV images and compare them with historical data using AI 
algorithms and detect pest incidence and the type of insect that has landed like the locust, 
grasshopper, etc. The system  sends alerts to farmers to their smartphones so that farmers can 
take required precautions for pest control  
Artificial Intelligence in agriculture not only helps farmers to automate their farming but also 
shifts to precise cultivation  for higher crop yield and better quality while using fewer 
resources.This Project will help in laying the foundation for improving machine learning or 
Artificial Intelligence -based services  specifically for the purposes of Development of 
Precision Agriculture Services for Farmers in the State. 
During the course of this project we will create an ecosystem of imagery data sets and AI/ML 
engines which only focus on identification of soil types and plant identification. Over time 
we can cover other scopes men tioned above like pest detection, fertilizer advisory and 
weather predictions.

Below is a Summary of  the primary 
Indices to be used to train the System 
employing Multispectral Imagery: 
The vegetation index is an indicator that 
is calculated as a result of operations 
with different spectral ranges of remote 
sensing data and is related to the 
vegetation parameters in a p articular 
pixel of the image. The effectiveness of 
vegetation indices is determined by the 
characteristics of reflection. The 
calculation of most of the vegetation  
indices is based on two the most stable 
sections of the curve spectral reflectance of plants. 
 
NDVI - normalized difference vegetation index. The most common in agriculture, 
characterizes the density of vegetation and allows farmers to assess germination, growth, the 
presence of weeds or diseases, as well as to predict the productivity of the f ields. Index 
indicators are generated through satellite images of green mass, which absorbs 
electromagnetic waves in the visible red range and reflects them in the near infrared range. 
The red region of the spectrum (0.62 - 0.75 μm) accounts for the maximum abs orption of 
solar radiation by chlorophyll, and the near infrared zone (0.75 - 1.3 μm) has the maximum 
energy reflection by the leaf cell structure. That is, high photosynthetic activity leads to lower 
values of the reflection coefficients in the red region  of the spectrum and large values in the 
near infrared region of the spectrum. The ratio of these indicators to each other allows you to 
clearly separate the vegetation from other natural objects. As a result, it is possible to obtain a 
complete spectral a nalysis and identify areas that need reseeding, application of plant 
protection products or fertilizers. The index is moderately sensitive to changes in soil and 
atmospheric background, except in cases of poor vegetation, and can be saturated in 
conditions of dense vegetation, when the level of the leaf area index (LAI) becomes high. 

 
19 
 
EVI - enhanced vegetation Index. Developed to improve NDVI by optimizing the vegetation 
signal in areas with a high leaf area index (LAI). The index uses the blue reflection region to 
correct background soil signals and reduce atmospheric effects, including aerosol scattering. 
Most useful in high LAI regions where NDVI can be saturated. EVI values should range from 
0 to 1 for vegetation pixels. Bright features such as clouds a nd white buildings, along with 
dark features such as water, can result in anomalous pixel values in an EVI image. It is used 
to assess the variability of crop development both in conditions of dense vegetation cover and 
in conditions of sparse vegetation. 
 
GNDVI - green normalized difference vegetation index. It is similar to NDVI except that 
instead of the red spectrum it measures the green spectrum in the range from 0.54 to 0.57 
microns. This is an indicator of the photosynthetic activity of the vegetati on cover; it is most 
often used in assessing the moisture content and nitrogen concentration in plant leaves 
according to multispectral data which do not have an extreme red channel. Compared to the 
NDVI index, it is more sensitive to chlorophyll concentra tion. It is used in assessing 
depressed and aged vegetation. 
 
CVI - chlorophyll vegetation index. It has an increased sensitivity to the content of 
chlorophyll in the deciduous cover. It is used from the beginning to the middle of the crop 
growth cycle fo r a wide range of soils and sowing conditions by analyzing a large set of 
synthetic data obtained using the reflection model of the leaf surface. The increased 
sensitivity of the index to the concentration of chlorophyll in the leaf is due to the effective  
normalization of various LAI values obtained with the introduction of red and green colors. 
True Color - visual interpretation of the earth cover. A method of presenting and storing an 
image that allows you to display a large number of colors, halftones a nd shades. The true 
color image is displayed in a combination of red, green and blue stripes. 
8. WEB GIS APPLICATION 
Proposed Web Application will host the processed and analyzed agricultural data. On the web 
portal we will be able to view the current stat us of a crop, its health, details of the farmer and 
its crop, also we can generate all kinds of reports which will be helpful in taking decisions 
about the crops and land Usage in and around the farm areas. 
 
20 
Dashboard: Online Platform  
In order to analyse g rowth stages of agriculture in near real -time to determine field -specific 
crop status and discover crop growth trends, there will be a need to develop and rollout an 
Agriculture information system, wherein all the datasets which will be created in the 
aforementioned mapping systems will be utilized to decipher appropriate scenarios. These 
datasets are: 
● Crop Map 
● Land Use Land Cover map 
● Agricultural parcel mapping 
● Soil Nutrient Map 
● Site Suitability Mapping 
● Land Suitability Mapping 
● Watershed Intervention Map 
● Forest Demarcation map 
● Farm Link Road demarcation 
The system should integrate with the RoR information of land record and State SDI for the 
needed GIS/ non -GIS data access as OGC / Map services. The ROR information should be 
integrated with both web and mobile applications as part of the AIS solution. Any experience 
with such integrations should be demonstrated in the technical proposal and solution 
presentation.  
Agriculture information system will essentially comprise below technological cloud -
based components: 
1. The proposed GIS solution should be designed on SOA based web architecture, using 
proven open, inter -operable standards to be scalable in order to add or interface 
additional functionalities as part of change request or future redevelopment, up 
gradation when required. 
2. The System will support integration through Enterprise Service Bus. Capability to 
server data and application through an SOA. The application will support LDAP 
(Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) Active Directory Authentication 
3. Single sign-on, authentication module. 
4. Support of various metadata standards including FGDC Best Practice, Dublin Core, 
ESRI ISO, ISO 19139/19119 Web Services, ISO 19139/19115 Datasets etc. 
5. Should support unlimited Desktop client connection. Desktop GIS applic ations with 
the capability to consume WMS/ WFS services should be able to connect and use data 
from the server. 
6. The System will support database check in – check out / two -way replication 
functionalities hence maintaining the parent child relationship of Master Database. 
7. The System will support server-side geoprocessing tasks. 
8. The application will provide an out -of-the-box, configurable mobile application that 
allows dynamic query and update server data remotely. The mobile application should 
be able to integrate with GPS devices. 
 
21 
9. A robust Security layer: The Agriculture information system will be comprise of a 
robust Geospatial Security layer wherein it will be based on a role -based security 
system where each role should have a different level of access in terms of datasets as 
well as Portal functions, for example a particular group of users must be only able to 
visualize some part of Certain part of a satellite Image, without altering the RAW 
Image on the server side. It will be possible to have the security Information including 
the credentials in the database or in an Active directory. 
10. For geospatial datasets it will include OGC Web Service Provider Security including 
both Coarse grained and Fine- grained security. 
11. Additionally, The Agriculture Management system will include a robust security 
mechanism for the geospatial services which will include both method level security, 
object level security along with HTTP/HTTPS Authentication for URL security. The 
application should support TLS versions 1.0, 1.1 and 1.2. 
12. A Comprehensive OGC CAT CSW Catalog: The Agriculture information system 
Catalog will be a repository of metadata for OGC services and related entities 
published using above mentioned datasets. It will provide a central means to publish, 
discover and administer such services. 
The main features of this Catalog will be: 
● Capability to serve and consume REST, SOAP & OGC Complied web services. 
● support for OGC services such as WMS WFS, WCS, WFS-T, KML, CSV etc. 
● various encodings such as KML, RSS, JSON, ebXML 
● Feature Rich Geoportal: The Agriculture information system needs to have a 
presentation layer based on a geoportal which will  have below mentioned features: 
● Zoom in, Zoom Out, Fit to Layer, Scale, Zoom by rectangle, previous view, next view 
○ User Registration and Login 
○ Managing Data Sources directly on the geoportal – (WMS, WFS, WCS, WFS-
T and GeoRSS) 
○ Downloading data using secured URL 
○ Query Builder using configurable widget 
○ Data Filter, 
○ Configurable software code for the Geo-portal with necessary documentation 
○ Geoprocessing via WPS, whereby various Vector and Raster based spatial 
models created can be published. 
● The Agricultural application designed using Configurable web app builder should 
give the option to quickly create various status dashboards based on the Web GIS 
server published Spatial/Geo processing models and using the wizards. The Web and 
Mobile App should be able to connect various external spatial data repositories for 
any agricultural resource data. Build custom web applications with the API for 
JavaScript, customize an existing configurable app or build and deploy applications 
with Web App Builder. 
● ERP and GIS integration : After the data has been collected in the ERP and GIS 
platforms separately, amalgamation of both the datasets is one of most important steps 
 
22 
in this project, this will be helpful in correlating the overall picture of agriculture in 
the state. 
This will facilitate transition to Digital Agriculture, whereby all departments besides 
the agriculture will be able to base their plan ning with respect to the irrigation, 
horticulture crops and rural infrastructure on current and relevant data.

10.  SOIL SURVEY AND CLASSIFICATION: 
Soil nutrients are important indicators of cultivated land quality, which is determined by two 
aspects: soil fertility and spatial location.  
Soil fertility, which is closely related to the concentrations of soil nutrient elements, i s the 
foundation of soil productivity. Soil nutrient elements can be classified into essential, 
beneficial, and toxic elements. Essential elements are critical for all plants under all growth 
conditions, and can be divided into two categories on the basis of their essentiality: (1) macro 
elements that are required in high amounts, e.g., nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium 
(K), and (2) micro elements that are required in lower amounts, e.g., zinc, manganese and 
copper. A sufficient supply of soil nut rients allows plants to grow and develop normally, and 
the abundance and chemical form of nutrients directly determines whether the soil is fertile. 
 
24 
Therefore, it is essential to measure the concentrations of soil nutrient elements to estimate 
farmland quality as well suitable sites for crop. 
Soil nutrient properties can vary spatially due to many factors, such as pedogenic processes, 
climate, parent material, topography, and human influences. When the soil nutrient content 
exhibits high spatial variability , it is difficult to estimate soil nutrient status among regions 
based directly on the different geographical features. 
In this Project we will be using UVA Imagery based geographic map and geo tagged and 
tested soil samples to prepare a soil fertility map  which will be a guide to understand the 
nutrients in the soil and their location and also will help to determine the suitable crop 
according to soil nutrients. Geotagged Soil samples will be tested for following nutrients in 
the laboratory 
 
Basic Properties Macro-nutrients Secondary-nutrients Micro-nutrients 
pH Nitrogen Sulphur Zinc 
EC (Electrical Conductivity) Potassium   Iron 
OC (Organic Carbon) Phosphorus   Copper 
      Manganese 
      Boron

3 Overview of the Himachal Pradesh and its agriculture sector 
Himachal Pradesh "Province of the Snow laden Mountains “is a state in the northern part of India. 
Situated in the Western Himalayas, it is one of the eleven mountain states and is characterized by an 
extreme landscape featuring several peaks and extensive river systems. Himachal Pradesh is the 
northernmost state of India and shares borders with the union territories of Jammu and 
Kashmir and Ladakh to the north, and the states of Punjab to the west, Haryana to the 
southwest, Uttarakhand to the southeast and a very narrow border with Uttar Pradesh to the South. 
The total population of Himachal Pradesh is 68.75 Lakh of which 62%, i.e., 42.56 Lakh population is 
engaged in agriculture and allied services. The total area of the state is 55.67 Lakh Ha of which only 
9.55 Lakh Ha is net sown area. The agricultural economy of the state is characterized by the 
existence of many small and marginal farmers owning approximately 1 Ha average land. 
Himachal Pradesh is largest fruit exporting state in the country and known as fruit bowl of India.  
Major crops sown in the state are as below:
