4 Department of Agriculture, Government of Himachal Pradesh 
The Department of Agriculture was established in the year 1948. In 1950, it was merged with Forest 
Department of the state. The Department started functioning independently in the year 1952. In the 
early years, the Department catered to the needs both of Research and Extension for Agriculture 
and Horticulture Crops. In 1970, the Horticulture wing of the Department was separated, and a 
separate Horticulture Department was established to look after the needs of horticulturists in the 
State. In 1971, Government entrusted the Research Work to Agriculture University. 
Draft proposal of Transformation in Agriculture Using Emerging Technologies in the Department of 
Agriculture - GoHP 
 
7 | P a g e 
 
Because of the mountainous terrain, only a little over 10 percent of the total available land area is 
cultivated in the state. Population pressure on cultivated land is high and the holdings of most of the 
cultivators are small and scattered, i.e., 88.00% of the farmers are small and marginal. About 20% of 
the cultivated area is under irrigation and remaining 80% of the area is rainfed. The cultivation is 
carried out right from 300 to 3000 meters above Mean Sea Level. Agro-climatically the region is more 
suitable for growing off-season vegetables and temperate fruits. Horticulture, Animal Husbandry and 
Fisheries also generate a significant wealth and employment in Agriculture Sector. 
4.1 Organization 
Key Functional Entities 
The Department of Agriculture is headed by the Director of Agriculture, headquartered at Shimla. The 
Director of Agriculture is assisted by one Additional Director and two Joint Directors of Agriculture at 
Headquarter. One post of Additional Director of Agriculture has been sanctioned for North Zone at 
Dharamshala, District Kangra, who monitors all the department activities in Kangra, Chamba, Una, 
Hamirpur and Mandi districts. 
 
In each district (except Lahaul & Spiti and Kinnaur), the Deputy Director Agriculture is responsible for 
implementation of all Agriculture Development Programs in the respective districts. In Lahaul, District 
Agricultural Officer at Keylong, in Spiti Division, Assistant Project Officer (Agriculture) at Kaza and in 
Kinnaur District, District Agricultural Officer at Reckong Peo are responsible for implementation of 
Agriculture Development Programs. One Project Director in each District is also responsible for 
implementation of ATMA & PKKKY Programs. 
 
Deputy Directors of Agriculture are assisted by SMS for Agriculture Extension and by SDSCOs for 
Soil and Water Conservation. Technical consultation is being provided by three Divisional Engineers 
situated at Shimla, Palampur and Bhangrotu (Mandi). With a view to strengthen the extension network 
at grass-root level, one SMS, two ADOs and 4 to 6 AEOs are working in each Development Block.  
For Soil & Water Conservation activities, 22 Subdivisions are in existence and each Subdivision is 
headed by the Sub Divisional Soil Conservation Officer. However, their administrative and financial 
control is with the Deputy Director of Agriculture of the concerned District. 
 
One Agriculture Extension Training Centre, upgraded as State Agriculture Management Extension & 
Training Institute (SAMETI), at Mashobra & two Farmers Training Centers (FTC) at Sundernagar and 
Dharamshala, headed by the Principal are in operation in the State. A number of training courses for 
the grass root functionaries viz. Agriculture Development Officers, Agriculture Extension Officers and

4.3 Activities 
4.3.1 The primary activities of the Department 
Himachal Pradesh is predominately an agricultural State where Agriculture provides direct 
employment to about 71 percent of the total population. The Agriculture sector contributes nearly 
13.62 percent of the total state domestic product. The Department of Agriculture is dedicated to serve 
the farming community by implementing various Developmental Programmes and disseminating the 
relevant technology to increase productivity, of field crops. The natural endowments like soil, land, 
water etc. are being harnessed in such a way, that cherished goals of ecological sustainability and 
economic upliftment of farming community are achieved. 
 
The Department is committed and responsible for rapid economic upliftment of farming community of 
the state through planned agriculture development. The strategy for the future is for sustainable 
agriculture and production, improvement in productivity and quality.

5 Objectives of Automation 
The basic objective of this project proposal titled “Transformation in Agriculture Using Emerging 
Technologies” is to develop in-house competency in providing ICT based services to farmers, thereby 
reducing administrative processes and increasing efficiencies, and ensure the on-demand delivery of 
agriculture services to the famers with the aim to improve quality of agriculture produce. The other 
objectives are: 
1. Integrated farmer database: Create a unique farmer database across the state which will act 
as single source of truth for identifying the farmers / beneficiaries and applying for various 
state and central schemes.    
2. Integrated Portal for Scheme management system & DBT: Create a single sign-on 
platform for various schemes of the department (State and Centrally Sponsored) which will 
standardize and simplify procedures for applying to various schemes (Cash and In-Kind) of the 
department and transfer the benefit directly into the accounts of the beneficiaries while cutting 
down on the hops in the disbursement process. 
3. Market Information System: The market information system will provide visibility on the 
projection of demand and supply, price realization of the produce and access to national e-
market platform. Centralized directory for input suppliers and status availability of seeds, 
fertilizers, pesticides, equipment/ implements, and machinery with their GIS locations. 
4. API Integration: For information exchange and onboarding additional facility to the farmers, 
system will be open for API integration and API for exposing the data through secure and 
authorized channels.  
5. Traceability Platform: The traceability platform with the goal to improve the way food is 
tracked, transported, and sold to consumers across markets i.e., tracking food from farm to 
plate. The traceability platform will provide transparency and efficiency in value chain, record-
keeping and help the farmer get premium prices of their produces. 
6. Automated Farm extension serveries: Use of GIS, Data Analysis, AI & ML for Farmer 
Advisory. Use of Decision Support System to provide timely information to farmers and other 
stakeholders. 
7. Customized Dashboards, Data Analytics, and centralized reports: Data Analytics & 
Visualization based Decision Support System for Planning, Procurement and Farm Advisories; 
to help management at various levels in decision making, monitoring and evaluation of 
operations in an efficient and transparent manner through an extensive reporting system 
8. Farmer Field School (FFS) App: Farmer trainings, capacity building workshops, knowledge 
sharing best practices can be provided through this application.

5.1 Setting up of Digital Agriculture Ecosystem 
The Department of Agriculture will use IT enabled ecosystem which will benefit the farmers, 
department and other stakeholders as defined below: 
5.1.1 Farmers 
The department does not have a centralized farmer database and in the absence of such a database, 
for the selection of PM KISAN beneficiaries, the work of identifying farmers was assigned to the 
Revenue Department in State. A well organized, verified, and scrutinized farmer database will enable 
farmers to apply online as per their eligibility without the need to submit required documents multiple 
times for every scheme.  
Once approved by the department, the subsidy funds will be transferred to farmers through DBT so 
that benefit is given to the genuine and eligible farmers only. Farmers will be able to share their 
success stories and best practices in their language (Hindi and other local languages- Pahari, Dogri, 
Kangri, Kinnauri, etc.) with supported images and videos which will have wider impact for adoption of 
such practices. 
5.1.2 Department 
The Department will benefit due to reduced administrative delays, increased transparency, efficiency 
in identification of beneficiary farmers, real time tracking of applications and lesser grievances. Due to 
Aadhaar based identification of farmer, chances of same person getting multiple benefits in the same 
scheme/program will be negligible. Integration will Aadhaar, eHimBhoomi (Land Records database), 
Public Distribution System and Election Photo ID Cards will ensure such a fool-proof system. The 
single sign-on online application system for all Schemes of the Agriculture, the Department will ensure 
that applications are processed quickly, and finances required for meeting the subsidy requirement of 
all applicants are worked out at State level within the financial year itself. 
5.2 Existing Status of Digitization 
5.2.1 Department Website 
Department website (www.hpagriculture.com) has been developed by a private company and is 
static, non-responsive website in English which needs to be upgraded. 
5.2.2 Application Software 
Presently, Department of Agriculture does not have any type of Software/Mobile Applications for the 
Officers/ Farmers for implementation and monitoring of the State Sponsored Agriculture Schemes. 
Further, there is no online interface for the officers for training/interaction purpose.

hops

6. Knowledge sharing platform for FAQs, training materials, videos, best practices, slandered 
operating procedures and etc. 
7. All above services shall be accessible through single integrated Mobile App so that farmers 
can interact and avail the required services from the field itself. 
 
Therefore, currently, the project is targeted on following areas: 
1. Farmer’s Database and registration Portal so that farmers can register themselves on the 
portal and may apply for any scheme as per their eligibility. 
2. Computerization of State or Central Sponsored Agriculture schemes with end-to-end solution

6.1.4 Automated Farm extension serveries  
This would require use of artificial intelligence, machine learning and IoT based services for 
collaboration with the different stakeholders in the agri ecosystem.  
6.1.4.1 Drone technology: 
Agriculture drone empowers the farmer to adapt to specific environments and make mindful choices 
accordingly. The gained data helps regulate crop health, crop treatment, crop scouting, irrigation, and 
Draft proposal of Transformation in Agriculture Using Emerging Technologies in the Department of 
Agriculture - GoHP 
 
19 | P a g e 
 
carry out field soil analysis and crop damage assessments. The drone survey helps boost crop yields 
and minimize time and expenses. 
 
This is proposed to be carried out in as a pilot / PoC in the following stage 
1. Analyzing the area: This identifies the territory being tested. Therefore, the first step 
includes establishing a boundary, analyses of the area, and then finally, uploading the 
technical GPS information into the drone's navigation system. 
2. Using Autonomous Drones: Since Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are 
independent, they enter flight patterns into their already established system to collect 
required data. 
3. Uploading the data: After capturing all the required data through sensors such as the 
multispectral sensor/RGB sensor, it is processed through numerous software for 
further analysis and interpretation. 
4. Output: After collecting the data, they format it so that farmers can understand the 
data with no hassle, bringing them a step closer to precision farming. 3D mapping or 
Photogrammetry are popular methods to display extensive data collected. 
The Department of Agriculture, GoHP envisions the following benefits from the use of such 
technologies.  
1. Enhanced Production- The farmer can improve production capabilities through 
comprehensive irrigation planning, adequate monitoring of crop health, increased 
knowledge about soil health, and adaptation to environmental changes. 
2. Effective and Adaptive Techniques- Drone usage results in regular updates to 
farmers about their crops and helps develop strengthened farming techniques. They 
can adapt to weather conditions and allocate resources without any wastage. 

Draft proposal of Transformation in Agriculture Using Emerging Technologies in the Department of 
Agriculture - GoHP 
 
20 | P a g e 
 
3. Greater safety of farmers- It is safer and more convenient for farmers to use drones 
to spray pesticides in terrains challenging to reach, infected areas, taller crops, and 
power lines. It also helps farmers prevent spraying the crops, which leads to less 
pollution and chemicals in the soil. 
4. 10x faster data for quick decision-making- Drone surveys back farmers with accurate 
data processing that encourages them to make quick and mindful decisions without 
second-guessing, allowing farmers to save the time invested in crop scouting. Various 
sensors of the drone enable capturing and analyzing data from the entire field. The 
data can focus on problematic areas such as infected crops/unhealthy crops, different 
colored crops, moisture levels, etc. The drone can be fixed with several sensors for 
other crops, allowing a more accurate and diverse crop management system. 
5. Less wastage of resources- Agri-drones enables optimum usage of all resources 
such as fertilizer, water, seeds, and pesticides. 
6. 99% Accuracy rate- The drone survey helps farmers calculate the precise land size, 
segment the various crops, and indulge in soil mapping. 
7. Useful for Insurance claims- Farmers use the data captured through drones to claim 
crop insurance in case of any damages. They even calculate risks/losses associated 
with the land while being insured. 
8. Evidence for insurance companies- Agricultural insurance sectors use Agri-drones 
for efficient and trustworthy data. They capture the damages that have occurred for the 
right estimation of monetary payback to the farmers.

The image and data can be captured through the mobile devices of the farmers and push on the 
server for analysing and advisory from experts, AI & ML. It can be extremely useful as it can be used 
for gaining new and more insightful information regarding different aspects of agriculture such as field

6.1.4.3 Chatbots for farmers: 
Building a chatbot with the help of text classification for specific problem statements has been carried 
out for the past several years. Instead of solving a single problem with the help of a chatbot, all 
necessary problems can arrive at a solution with the help of a chatbot. Developing a multi-purpose 
chatbot will help the users with lots of conveniences and also will solve a lot of use cases. Multi-
purpose chatbots can be very useful tool for farmers. People can use the chatbot whenever they need 
as well as for whatever purpose they need. The following activities can be explored by using chatbot 
services.  
 Plant Protection: In the KCC dataset, 60.6% of the potato farming calls were related 
to remedies for protecting and hence a solution can be provided to multiple farmers on 
similar challenge through chatbots. 
 Pests and diseases: This service would help the farmers seek suggestions on which 
fertilizers to spray for a particular crop disease. 
 Weather: In the KCC dataset, 39.4% of the overall calls were about weather-related 
questions; 13.5% of potato farming questions were about weather. Farmers eagerly 
sought weather information, as rains can wash away expensive sprayed pesticides and 
weather conditions determine the best time to harvest crops. 

Draft proposal of Transformation in Agriculture Using Emerging Technologies in the Department of 
Agriculture - GoHP 
 
22 | P a g e 
 
 Best Practices: Information related to best practices can help increase yield in terms 
of the quantity or quality of potatoes. The chatbot platform would help farms to ask 
information related to ways to increase yield and, consequently, income. 
 Unbiased Recommendations on Products: Farmers wanted recommendations from 
agri-experts on products they should purchase. By providing this service through 
chatbots, it will become convenient and cost effective for farmers to seek unbiased 
recommendations on farm products which are recommended by government agencies.  
6.1.5 Farmer Field School (FFS) App 
 Farmer extension services can easily spread-out till the grassroot level by developing the application 
like Farmer Field school. Proposed features of the application are as under: 
1. The FFS will help the farmers in understanding the best practices followed by successful 
farmers. They can be called for guest lectures and teach the farmers on what different they 
have done to achieve success in farming. 
2. The repository of such lectures can be uploaded on the server and can be watched by other 
farmers who are interested in changing the technique of farming. 
3. The portal will also promote the farmers who provide good information to farmers and through 
which other farmers too have succeeded. 
4. The FFS will be a participatory approach that uses non-formal education methods based on 
experimental learning techniques and participatory training methods.  
5. FFS will emphasize learning by doing. The learning process takes place in the field and is 
normally designed to last for a full growing/cropping cycle. This enables farmers to participate 
fully in implementation of all components of the technology from planting to harvesting. 
6. Farmers Field School (FFS) will be a season long on-field interactive learning process 
recommended by Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO). 
7. These FFS are being implemented by Facilitators under the technical guidance of Krishi 
Vighyan Kendra (KVKs). 
8. As FFS is time bound programme based on field observations, they are to be recorded along 
with photographs. The activities performed in the FSS are to be noted. Hence to record all 
these activities in real time and on field, a tool in the form of smartphone application is 
developed.

6.1.6 Traceability Platform 
A traceability solution provides the ability to track and trace the entire process from sapling 
distribution, tracking agronomy practices, measuring quality at harvest and tracking the food till it 
reaches the end consumer. It empowers every stakeholder in the value chain and strikes the right 
balance between the farmer and the consumer. 
 
Traceability platform with the goal to improve the way food is tracked, transported and sold to 
consumers across markets. It will be generated transparency and efficiency in value chain record-
keeping with following expected benefits: 
1. The increased food quality and safety levels helps the farmers get a better ROI on their 
produce 
2. Age & quality of product validated through the technology 
3. Enable businesses to make informed management decisions and reduce operating costs 
4. Improvement in customer trust also increased brand value and equity 
5. Help reduce fraud bulk selling by seller against a single Certification

Executive Summary 
The economy of Haryana is primarily based on agriculture. It contribut es 15% to India’s agricultural 
production while occupying only 1.5% of the total area. The contribution of agriculture sector to the 
GSVA of Haryana was 19% (at constant prices) for FY 2019 -20. 86% of the land is arable and around 
96% is cultivated. 75% of the area is irrigated by tube wells and an extensive system of canals. 70% of 
the residents are engaged in agriculture and Haryana is the 2nd largest contributor to the central pool 
of food grains in India. In alignment with the long -term vision of the Farmer Welfare and Agriculture 
Development Department, Government of Haryana, to promote application of information 
technologies in Agr iculture and doubling of farmers income, as part of NeGPA FY2020 -21 plan, 
Haryana Govt is planning to implement projects wi th emerging technologies such as Data analytics, 
Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning, Block Chain Technology, Internet of Thin gs and GIS in 
Agriculture Sector. 
After the discussions with Director on Digital Interventions and deployment of emerging technologies 
in the agricultural sector, National Institute of Smart Government (NISG) has crafted this proposal 
which contains high l evel architecture, solution, budgetary estimate, and overall costing for the 
project, which will be submitted subsequently  to seek Govt of India funding to implement in the 
identified priority areas by the department. 
The objectives of the department in application of emerging technologies are: 
1.  To ensure unbiased and accurate crop yield estimation for timely insurance related 
compensation to farmers and for accurate state-wide reporting 
2.  To ensure unbiased and accurate kharaba/localised loss assessment for fair compensation to 
farmers 
3.  To prevent the loss of yield due to pest, weed and disease related events by technology-based 
surveillance and early identification of affected areas 
4.  To bolster the entire agricultural value chain through transparent sourcing of  farm inputs 
utilising blockchain based applications 
5.  To enhance farmer income by enhancing yield through efficacious application of fertilisers and 
pesticides and lowering per unit input costs using drones for precision farming 
To meet the above objectives, the department intends to initiate a pilot study in 4 blocks of 4 
geographically varied districts of the state to establi sh the efficacy and efficiency of application of 
emerging technologies in the below mentioned priority areas: 
a) Prompt and accurate Yield assessment of the crops using satellite imagery and drone mapping 
to ensure fair compensation to the insured farmers for lower yield within the same cropping 
cycle/season as well as to eliminate disputes with IC 
b) Prompt and accurate Localised loss (Kharaba) assessment using drones & satellite imagery to 
ensure fair compensation to the insured farmers  
c) Pest, Weed and disease surveillance for minimising crop yield loss due to biotic stressors. This 
would be achieved using drones and satellite imagery for proactive identification of affected 
areas and remediation measures 
d) Traceability of farm inputs and mapping over a timeline f or certified seeds as a full -scale 
intervention across the state using Blockchain based technologies. Traceability of seeds from 
breeder to certified seeds level using Blockchain technology for authenticity of information. 
e) Precision Farming  for enhanced yi eld and lowered input costs through soil nutrient 
management, disease, and pest control by efficient applicat ion of farm inputs such as 
fertilizers and pesticides using autonomous programmed drones. The department wants to

set a new standard of agriculture  by deploying IoT/analytics -based Precision Farming 
technology 
Envisaged implementation timeline for the pilot is 15 months, post sanction of the project with three 
key stages: 
1. DPR/RFP, tender, selection & onboarding of System Integrator (SI), site and equip. setup (3 
months),  
2. Pilot for rice (kharif) & wheat (rabi) incl. data collection & analysis and evaluation (9 months) 
3. Stabilisation & Evaluation of the results (3 months)

1. About Department of Agriculture & Farmer’s Welfare (DA&FW), 
Haryana 
Haryana state has a strong agricultural background. About 70% of residents are engaged in agriculture. 
Haryana is self-sufficient in food production and the second largest contributor to India's central pool 
of food grains. The main crops of Haryana are Wheat, Rice, Sugarcane, Cotton, Oilseeds, Gram Barley, 
Corn, Millet etc. 
Department of Agriculture & Farmer's Welfare (D&FW) is concerned with the developments regarding 
agricultural activities and looks after the welfare of the farm ers of Haryana. With initiatives towards 
Doubling of Farmers Income (DFI), Farm Act’s 2020, and NeGP -A alignment acting as enablers, the 
department is heading towards creating a new era of prosperity in the agriculture sector of Haryana.  
The Department of Agriculture & Farmer's Welfare is divided into various branches and divisions which 
are Soil Conservation Branch, Plant Protection Branch, Quality Control Br anch, Planning Branch, 
Engineering Branch, Ground Water Cell, Seed Section, Sugarcane Division, Statistics Branch, World 
Bank Branch, IT Cell, ATMA, RKVY, NFSM, etc. 
 
2. Background of the project 
Doubling of farmers income  
The past strategy of the agriculture sector of India was primarily focussed towards raising agricultural 
outputs and improving food security. The strategy was to focus on productivity through better 
technology and inputs, remunerative prices and subsidies, public investments in agriculture sector and 
facilitating institutions. This has largely resulted into making India, not only self -sufficient at the 
aggregate level, but also a net food exporting country. The strategy  does not explicitly recognize the 
need to raise the farmer’s income and did not mention any direct measure to promote farmers 
welfare. The net result has been that farmer’s income remains low, which is evidenced from the 
incidence of poverty among farm households. The NSSO data on consumption expenditure survey for 
the year 20 11-12 reveals that more than one fifth of the rural households with self -employment in 
agriculture as their principal occupation were having income less than the poverty line. Haryana  is 
among the states which have the lowest proportion of rural households  with self -employment in 
agriculture below the poverty line.   
Farmer’s income also remained low in relation to the income of those working in non-farm sector. The 
disparity appears quite large and required a policy response to raise the farmer’s income at  a faster 
rate. The low and fluctuating farm income is causing detrimental effect on the interest in farming and 
farm investments, and is also forcing more and more cultivators, parti cularly younger age group, to 
leave farming. This can cause serious adverse effect on the future of agriculture in the country.  
Agriculture is the largest enterprise in the country. An enterprise can survive only if it can grow 
consistently. And growth is incumbent upon savings & investment, both of which are a function of 
positive net returns from the enterprise. The net returns d etermine the level of income of an 
entrepreneur, farmer in this case.  
It is apparent that income earned by a farmer from agriculture is crucial to address agrarian distress 
and promote farmers welfare. In this background, the goal set by the Honourable Pr ime Minister to 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
260
Page | 9  
 
double farmers income by 2022 -23 is central to promote farmers welfare, reduce agrarian distress 
and bring parity between income of farmers and those working in non-agriculture professions. 
In view of the above, the Government of India constituted DFI (Doubling Farmers’ Income) Committee 
and considered farmers’ income into the core of its deliberations and incorporates it as the fulcrum 
of its strategy.  
The Committee has adopted a basi c equation of Economics to draw up its strategy, which s ays that 
net return is a function of gross return minus the cost of production. This throws up three (3) variables, 
namely, productivity gains, reduction in cost of cultivation and remunerative price,  on which the 
Committee has worked its strategy. In doin g so, it has drawn lessons from the past and been 
influenced by the challenges of the present & the future. In consequence, the strategy platform is built 
by the following four (4) concerns: 
1. Sustainability of production  
2. Monetization of farmers’ produce 
3. Re-strengthening of extension services 
4. Recognizing agriculture as an enterprise and enabling it to operate as such, by addressing 
various structural weaknesses  
The Committee on Doubling Farmers’ Income  (DFI) also deliberated the status, role and approach 
desired from Science and Technology interventions. The recommendations of the committee are 
categorized into:  
a. How future scientific research and development can be prioritized to bring direct focus on 
areas that will have immediate impact on farmers’ income . For meeting the national agenda 
to double farmers’ income, there is a need to direct the scientific research into areas that can 
bring income gains in the comparatively shorter term for all agricult urists, especially for 
underdeveloped and poorly resourc ed farmers. The focus is to move from the ‘Science of 
Discovery’ to ‘Science of Delivery’. 
b. Availability of various digital technologies and associated applications, which can be utilised 
to enhance farmers’ income. A systematic approach, that promotes conv ergence within the 
technologies, to develop farms and farmers as enterprises, is deliberated. 
 
3. National e-Governance Plan in Agriculture (NEGPA –A) Implementation 
Guidelines 
The National e-Governance Plan in Agriculture (NeGPA) aims to achieve rapid develo pment in India 
through use of Information & Communication Technology (ICT) for timely access to agriculture related 
information for the farmers. The scheme which was to end on 31.03.2020 has been extended.  
Under NeGPA (Phase II), funds were released to St ates for carrying out the activities viz. Site 
preparation of offices where hardware is to be installed and establishment of computer training labs, 
Procurement, installation, and accounting of Hardwa re/system software. Back -up power 
arrangements, wherever required, Setting-up of State Project Management Unit (SPMUs) and hiring 
of manpower on contract basis (Data Entry Operators), Connectivity for the locations where hardware 
is being installed and Dat a Digitization Customization of applications as per State/UT’s specific 
requirements.  
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
261
Page | 10  
 
Under the scheme, funding pattern between the Centre and the State is 90:10 for Northeastern States 
& Himalayan States, 60:40 for other States and 100:0 for UTs. 
Further, the Committee on Doubling Farmers’ Income in its report appreciated the role of Digital 
Technology, which can play a transformational role in modernizing and organizing how rural India 
performs its agricultural activities. The po ssible components for modern management of agriculture 
are i) Remote Sensing; ii) Geographical Information System  (GIS); iii) Data Analytics; iv) Artificial 
Intelligence & Machine Learning (AI&ML) and v) Internet of Things (IoT).  
To start with, for incorporating recommendations made by the DFI Committee about use of AI & ML, 
Big Data Analysis, and Internet of Things etc. in agriculture, NeGPA guidelines had been revised in June 
2020. Under the revised NeGPA guidelines, funds would be released to the States /UTs only for the 
projects involving use of modern information technologies such as such as Artificial Intelligence & 
Machine Learning, Block Chain Technology, Internet of Things, Robotics etc., and for customization / 
shifting of web & mobile applications already developed by the States, to the platform to be developed 
using digital technologies mentioned above.   
 
4. Objectives of the Project 
A farmer's net income from agricultural operations is dependent on several factors such as  
I. Farm level productivity of the crop yield per hectare of land 
II. Efficiency of use of agricultural inputs such as fertilizers and pesticides 
III. Risk coverage for loss of crops due to weather  
IV. Risk coverage for loss of crops due to pest related events 
V. Accuracy of field data collected by various agencies  
The Department is constantly striving to ensure the sustainability of farm incomes, but the present 
means of collecting data and estimating outcomes are not only time consuming but also prone to 
inefficiency and lead to poor  reporting. This delays compensation to  farmers for their loss and can 
even lead to varying estimates for future planning and policy level strategy formulation. 
To address the above issues and lead to unbiased, accessible, and integrated processes, this project 
aims at outlining the approach and methodology of implementing digital and technology interventions 
in application areas using emerging technologies in the agriculture sector. The department has 
engaged NISG to develop a proposal for submission to the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmer Welfare, 
Government of India for availing funds, followed by managing the project and program level activities. 
Department has identified the following areas for the implementation  of e merging technology in 
agriculture:  
1) Yield Assessment  
2) Assessment of Crop Loss/Kharaba 
3) Pest, weed and disease surveillance  
4) Block chain application for tracking agricultural input/output  
5) Precision Farming

5. Projects 
Scope of the pilot:  
Geography: The pilot would be carried out in 4 blocks identified by the Department. 
Crops: Wheat (Rabi) & Rice (Kharif) 
Assumptions:  
• Yield assessment - Area considered under cultivation per block is 10,000 ha 
• Localised loss assessment - Assumed to be 25% of the total crop under cultivation 
• Pest, weed and disease surveillance – Area considered under cultivation per block is 10,000 
ha 
• Block chain - pilots 1 & 2 would consider a test area of 25 ha per block or 100 ha in 4 blocks 
with approximately 100 farmers to be onboarded 
• Precision farming - Loss due to pest and disease infestation is approximately 15% as per 
studies carried out by various agencies, hence 15% of 10000 hectares i.e., 1500 ha per block 
is considered for pesticide spraying. Crops under consideration would be wheat, paddy 
(basmati/non-basmati), mustard and cotton. 
• Precision Farming - Since fertiliser spraying requires intensive soil testing & regular periodic 
application its efficacy would be tested only on approx. 150 ha area per block per crop. 
Crops under consideration would be wheat, paddy (basmati/non-basmati), mustard and 
cotton. The project shall be harmonised with the existing initiatives carried out by the state, 
such as spray of nano-fertilisers etc. 
Functional: Efficiency, efficacy & cost benefit analysis of emerging technologies in the pilot phase to 
estimate the effort and cost required for full state-wide implementation.  
Study of best practices: Although such large-scale pilots specific to the crops in scope have not been 
implemented in the state yet a few pilots have been carried out by emerging technology-based start-
ups in other states of India in coll aboration with the respective state governments and state 
agricultural universities. Careful consideration is given to such short scale pilots and their results while 
developing the framework for this pilot. 
Department support: Since the farms would mostly be privately owned, the Department is expected 
to coordinate with the property owners to obtain necessary clearances and cooperation. Also 
provisioning for last 10-year yield, kharaba & disease related data is expected from the department. 
5.1. Yield Assessment 
The primary purposes of estimating crop yield are to: 
1. Prepare crop -wise estimates of yield for planning procurement, storage, distribution, 
import/export, and other Government Programs. 
2. Process farmer claims under the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY). 
Reliable and timely information on crop area, yield, and production act as fundamental inputs to the 
planners/policy makers who are responsible for formulating efficient agricultural policies, and for 
making important decisions concerning procurement, storage, public distribution, import, export, and 
other related issues. Availability of crop yield statistics is an essential requirement of the agricultural 
statistical system, as it is a key variable in estimating crop production.  
Also, timely and accurate yield estimates are critical for farmers who suffer losses in production due 
to unpredictable factors and have insured themselves against such losses proactively. 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
263
Page | 12  
 
The yield estimates of major crops are obtained throug h analysis of Crop Cutting Experiments (CCE) 
conducted under scientifically designed General Crop Estimation Surveys (GCES). Field Operations 
Divisions (FOD) of the National Statis tical Office (NSO) has been providing technical guidance to the 
States and Union territories for organizing and conducting Crop Estimation Surveys for estimating yield 
rates of principal crops. Yield assessment is done by performing CCE in Kharif & Rabi season at the 
field level. Consequent to the implementation of PMFBY in 2014, the earlier practice of collecting 80 
CCE samples for major and 40 samples for minor crops at the district level has been replaced by 
performing 4 CCE per crop per Gram Panchayat i n all Districts and submitting the yield report within 
a month of harvest. This has necessitated deployment of a greater number of manpower resources 
across the State.

Recently the Government has adopted the use of “Smart Sampling” for selection of the sites for crop 
cutting experiments based on inputs (latitude/longitude) received  from the Mahalanobis National 
Crop Forecast Centre (MNCFC). After the plot selection, the harvest from that area gets evaluated on 
different parameters like grain weight, biomass weight, moisture, and other relevant factors. Based 
on the data collection, it is generalized for the entire region and an approximate estimate is derived 
for the state or region. 
5.1.1. As- is Assessment  
As part of the As-Is Study, NISG has had interactions with officials of 
• Directorate of Agriculture at Krishi Bhawan, Panchkula  
• District officials of Department of Agriculture, Yamuna Nagar &  
• District officials of Department of Agriculture, Karnal.  
The following observations were made of the current state of processes related to Yield Assessment 
in the state of Haryana:  
Crop Cutting Experiment  
Crop cutting experiments are carried out for major crops notified by the State Level Coordination 
Committee on Crop Insurance at the start of the crop season. Frequency of Crop cutting experiments 
is 4 CCE’s per Gram Panchayat per crop per season. 
1. Smart Sampling Technique  - For identification of the farm/khasra no., coordinates are 
provided by MNCFC using its point generation SOP for the crops of wheat and paddy. Random 
Sampling Method for field (Annex ure I) is followed for other crops. Since the accuracy of 
these coordinates can be limited due to lower resolution of satellite imagery (10-30 m), three 
coordinates are provided (primary, secondar y & tertiary) for one CCE. For ex. If the primary 
coordinate leads to side roads, midpoint of two farms or cases where harvesting is complete, 
then others can be used in that order). Therefore 12 coordinates are provided per crop per 
gram panchayat. These coordinates are then fed into the GPS application (e.g., Google Earth) 
to zero in on the exact location. 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
264
Page | 13  
 
2. Random Sampling Method for plot (Annex ure II) - Upon identification of the field, random 
sampling method is followed for identifying the plot of size (5m x 5m) for carrying out the CCE. 
This is followed for all the crops including wheat and paddy. 
3. Yield Measurement - During this experiment functionaries from the Agriculture Dept, Farmer 
and Insurance representative are present to avoid any moral hazards and maintain objectivity. 
The produce from this piece of land is then measured after cutting & threshing to arrive at the 
wet weight of the crop. This is then multiplied by the conversion factor and 400 to arrive at 
the yield in kg/ha. 
4. Data Capture & Repor ting - All the steps of the CCE should be captured in the CCE app but 
there are also Forms No. 1, 2 & 3 [Annex III] to record and report the entire experiment while 
are primarily used. 
5. Yield Estimate – The data collected from these experiments is then aver aged and multiplied 
by the entire area under cultivation in the co ncerned GP to arrive at the estimated yield for 
that crop.  
6. Crop Insurance claim - As per the basic cover, the farmers are compensated for reduced yield 
as compared to threshold/guaranteed yield uniformly across the village or the insurance unit.

Limitations & Challenges 
a. Lack of accuracy – There continues to be huge variation in the yield assessment across spatial 
and temporal dimensions. Random table method offers objectivity but in terms of accuracy it 
falls short as the CCE patch sample  is still selected by the random sampling method w here 
footsteps are used for measurement. Also, the GPS coordinates provided by MNCFC are only 
partially accurately located by the village level functionaries and there are issues of low 
network connectivity . Also, the Remote sensing technology used for SST  to identify CCE 
locations has a lower resolution leading to deviations of 10 -30m in accuracy in identification 
of the most suitable sample. 
 
 
Figure 2 Root Cause Analysis for limited accuracy in yield estimation 
b. Delay in insurance claims to the farmer – The overall CCE exercise takes close to 45-60 days 
for completion at the district level. Since it is a huge exercise where 4 CCE need to be carried 
out per village per crop, the total crop cutting experiments required for 2 crops can be close 
to 8000 for a District like Yamuna Nagar with 654 villages. There are also instances where the 
IC disputes the yield assessed and a revalidation is then required as per the PMFBY OG using 
technology enabled solutions to assess yield. 
 
 
Figure 3 Root Cause Analysis for delay in yield estimation

5.1.2. Recommendation for Business Process Improvement 
Technology has a role to play in reducing the logistical burden of planning, execu ting, and collecting 
CCEs and the data they gather. With different technological advancements and satellite imagery, the 
traditional method of CCE can be made more efficient and accurate. This would help in rationalization 
and optimization of the CCEs.  The PMFBY recommends the use of technology in agriculture. For 
example, using drones to reduce the use of crop cutting experiments (CCEs), which are traditionally 
used to estimate crop loss; and using mobile phones to reduce delays in claim settlements by 
uploading crop-cutting data on apps/online.  
As a component of the Pilot/Proof of Concept, yield assessment from drone-based and satellite-based 
imagery and subsequent ML analysis can be compared with the yield assessment from the field. This 
can assist in arriving at a cost benefit analysis by determining the efficacy and efficiency of the new 
process in terms of time, effort and suitability for large scale roll out. 
Pilot objectives 
1. Usage of Satellite based imagery/Drone based imagery for crop mapping & yield assessment 
using Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning algorithms. Here vegetation indices such as 
NDVI & SAVI can be mapped against typical crop signatures collected from a ground truthing 
exercise to arrive at a model which predicts the yield within satisfactory intervals of accuracy. 
2. The accuracy of the prediction model will be validated against physically conducted Crop 
Cutting Experiment outcomes in the pilot phase.

Advantages of the proposed method of yield estimation

1. Unbiased estimation of the yield and hence valid compensation to the insured farmer & with 
proper incentives to the IC  
2. Reduction/rationalisation of the number of CCEs needed to be conducted at the village level. 
This would lead to adoption dependent cost saving ranging from 25-50% 
3. Reduction in delay of compensation for low yield from current approximately 180 days (120 
days as per PMFBY) by almost 25-50%.  
4. Tremendous time saving and increased efficiency of data collection & reporting 
5. A solution well integrated with the present platforms and portals  used in localised loss 
assessment 
 
5.1.3. Solution Requirements – Yield Assessment 
 
The objective of this project is to develop a Computer Vision ML algorithm which (upon training) can 
provide yield estimation primarily based on crop type data, image processing data (images captured 
in multi/hyper spectral ranges through drones and satell ites) and weather data. The model will also 
incorporate classifying factors (such as field data, soil data etc. mapped to GIS services) that enable 
better predictive outcomes. 
This yield estimation solution will aim to provide: 
1. Statewide coverage via satellite : Satellite images at suitable resolution (resolution to be 
determined during pilot based on sensitivity and cost factors) that can provide yield prediction 
in conjunction with drone-based image capture (at specific identified locations). 
2. Focused coverage via drones: Drones will capture detailed images of limited locations that will 
be used along with satellite data to predict yield. Once trained, drone -based image capture 
will supplement CCE (when image data is used in conjunction with variables such as crop, soil, 
and weather data) to predict yield for the covered area. 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
270
Page | 19  
 
3. Scaling of drone data: Yield estimates generated from drone based focused coverage will be 
scaled across all blocks (blocks covered in pilot) using s atellite-based ML model to generate 
yield estimates for all blocks. If the pilot is successful, this scaling method will be used to scale 
yield estimation process across Haryana. 
4. Continuous training of ML model: Ground truthing through physical CCE will be continued at 
a reduced scale (reduced from current 4 CCE per gram panchayat as in section 18.2 of the 
revamped operational guidelines of PMFBY) to continuously train the ML model every season 
for improved predictive capabilities. 
 
5.1.4. Solution Approach & methodology – Yield Assessment 
 
With the objective of creating a solution that is viable and can be scaled across the state, the project 
will proceed in three phases: 
 
Figure 6 Phases of solution development and deployment - Yield assessment 
 
Phase 1 – Definition of detailed project requirements and execution plan.  
The NISG consulting team will work on the projects approved by the government and define detailed 
requirements such that the desired project outcome is measurable and scalable. This phase will lead 
to creation of project reports (with acceptance criteria for pilot success/failure, and execution and 
scale-up plans) and RFPs for on-boarding of suitable SI/vendors. 
Phase 2 – Pilot phase

Key outcome of this phase will be development of an AI/ML model that can optimize the target 
agricultural operations. This model will be developed via the below stages: 
1. Data Collection: 
a) Ground truthing  
b) GIS map integration with field boundaries (through rev enue map, or via Ground 
Truthing) 
c) Weather data (standard weather services) 
d) Rainfall data (moisture sensors, weather stations, or standard weather services) 
e) Agriculture zone mapping 
f) Terrain features/topography 
g) Remote sensing data from satellites  
h) Multi/Hyper spectral image and data capture using drones  
i) Data from soil testing  
j) Crop type (and variety) data 
k) On ground sensors and weather stations  
l) Crop information portals such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’ portal of Govt. of Haryana 
2. Model Creation – A Computer Vision model that is optimized using controls such as: 
a) Multi/Hyper spectral image input – visible, near and far infrared, microwave data to 
support multilevel analysis of vegetation indices (such as moisture stress index, NVDI, 
LWCI etc.). Image dat a is captu red via satellite remote sensing and drone -based 
sensors. 
b) Image capture parameter management – Ensuring that drone image data is factored 
for parameters such as angle of capture, angle of sun on horizon, season, height of 
capture etc. 
c) Establishment of appropriate data cleaning and ETL processes to ensure usable data 
is fed to the model. 
3. Prediction & Accuracy analysis – Model will be tested against set acceptance levels (model 
accuracy levels to be finalized in phase 1) such that the model can: 
a) Achieve predi ctions and classification/clustering accuracy that helps optimize 
agricultural operations 
b) Can help increase efficiency (reduce turnaround time and effort) for government 
enabled farmer support in areas such as loss assessment, yield estimation etc. 
  
Phase 3 – Scale-Up across state   
Pilots that pass the acceptance criteria will be analyzed by the appointed NISG consulting team and 
Government agencies (Haryana and central agencies as applicable) to determine viability of scaling 
the pilots across state. These scaled go-live projects may be based on a combination of different pilots 
(such that the scaling project c an provide maximum benefit in terms of better and optimized 
operations) and may consist of multiple stages.  
These scaled go -live projects will the n provide actionable data and recommendations to all 
stakeholders (including farmers, businesses working in agricultural space, Govt. of India, and Govt. of 
Haryana). Examples of such interactions and integrations are:

 Description of key terms 
1. Ground truthing - In remote sensing, "ground truth" refers to information collected on 
location. Ground truth allows image data to be related to real features and  materials on the 
ground. The collection of ground truth data enables calibration of remote -sensing data, and 
aids in the interpretation and analysis of what is being sensed. 
2. Predictors are the variables in a statistical regression model which determine the value of the 
outcome variable. 
3. Satellite based spectral imaging 
a. Multi-spectral imaging captures image data within specific wavelength ranges across the 
electromagnetic spectrum . It measures light in a small number (typically 3 to 15) of 
spectral bands. The wavelengths may be separated by filters or detected via the use of 
instruments that are sensitive to wavelengths, including light from frequencies beyond 
the visible light range, i.e., infrared, and ultra-violet.  
b. Hyper-spectral imaging is a special cas e of spectral imaging where often hundreds of 
contiguous spectral bands are available. 
4. Spectral Imaging based Vegetation indices 
Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI)It is a standard method for 
comparing the density & health of vegetation using satellite images. The NDVI 
values tentatively ranges between -1 to +1, the values close to +1 denotes the 
good health of vegetation NDVI is sensitive to the effects of soil and 
atmosphere and saturates at high density of vegetation:

5.1.5. Indicative solution architecture for pilot 
Indicative reference architecture for training a regression ML model for Yield Assessment is shown in 
the figure below. 
 
Figure 8 Indicative ML model for Yield Estimation 
 
As shown in the diagram above, the Yield prediction model can include the following aspects: 
1. Physical Layer – Physical data is captured such that a predictive regression model can be built. 
a. Yield Estimate: Crop cutting experiment as  required for yield assessment ( refer 
section on Crop cutting experiment in as -is & Annex I and II) will act as training data 
to the model. This data will be used in 75:25 ratio for training: validation (or 
prediction).  
b. Multi/Hyper spectral images: Images captured for multiple spectrum ranges through 
drones and satellites will act as the primary independent variable based on which the 
ML model will provide its yield estimate (images taken along with crop and weather 
data). 
c. Capture of predictor and cl assification factors: Yield prediction model will consider 
factors such as: 
i. Soil testing data at beginning of sowing season (if available) 
ii. Predefined factors built into model layers such as: Field boundaries, soil type, 
topography, built on a GIS based map 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
274
Page | 23  
 
iii. Crop type – will be received from portals such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’ 
iv. Weather profile (including parameters such as rainfall, temperature, pressure 
etc.)  
2. Computational Layer – Development of the model will require the following: 
a. Processing Engines: These are a set of engines and modules that will allow training for 
ML model 
i. Complex Event Processing – Event engine that triggers appropriate modules 
based on input trigger  
ii. Infrastructure engines – Depending on the platform used for training the 
model (platform such as cloud based in AWS or Azure etc., or on -premises 
platforms) the infrastructure engine will need to support scaling and 
virtualization. 
iii. Big Data Algorithms – Suitable Big data processing algorithm will need to be 
setup to manage and process all incoming data streams 
iv. ML algorithm: Drones – ML algorithm based on a suitable framework 
(framework that is supported by other infrastructure components proposed 
by pilot SI) such as TensorFlow, Pytorch, keras (or other) will be set up as the 
ML engine for drone operations.  
v. ML algorithm: Satellite - ML algorithm based on a suitable framework will be 
setup as the ML engine for satellite sourced data. The prediction from drone-
based ML model will act as an input to satellite model that projects the drone 
sourced data across state using its own multivariate prediction curve.  
b. Data services: These are databases and data warehouse structures that store and 
manage factors used for training the model 
i. Soil data – Captures data about soil quality through lab testing of areas under 
pilot. 
ii. GIS and pre -defined data – Data for factors (including field boundaries, soil 
type, topography) that are expected to be static during the pilot are mapped 
to a GIS platform for use in training the algorithm 
iii. Crop database – Captures crop, field, and farmer data (which can be retrieved 
from Haryana Govt. initiatives such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’) and sets the 
data as a layer on GIS map and as an input to the ML multivariate regression 
algorithm 
iv. Weather databa se – Captures factors su ch as precipitation profile, rainfall 
profile etc. for building the model. 
v. Image processing – Captures and saves image data (and metadata) for use in 
training the algorithm. This image data will act as the primary independent 
variable for yield prediction 
vi. CCE database – Crop cutting experiment data is fed to the model for training 
prediction capabilities of the model. 
3. Control Layer: During training, this layer will be the means of controlling, training, and 
monitoring the development of ML model. Both SI users and Govt. users will access the model 
being trained through web portal as well as app. 
a. Web Portal – Will be used to monitor and manage the model being produced. 
b. Mobile App – Will be used to provide data to the model (data such as ground truthing, 
GPS data for GIS mapping) as well as to monitor the model.

5.2. Localised Loss Assessment (Kharaba) 
PMFBY covers loss/damage to notified insured crops resulting from occurrence of identified localised 
risks of hailstorm, landslide, inundation, cloud burst and natural fire due to lightning affecting isolated 
farms in the notified area. 
5.2.1. As-is assessment 
As part of the As-Is Study, NISG has had interactions with officials of the Directorate of Agriculture at 
Krishi Bhavan, Panchkula, District officials of Department of Agriculture, Yamuna Nagar & District 
officials of Department of Agriculture, Karnal. The following observation s were made of the current 
state of processes related to crop loss due to localized calamity in the state of Haryana: 
1. Intimation by farmer - For localised loss due to water logging, inundation (except paddy), the 
farmer must intimate the concerned authority within 72 hrs. (Form -1) [Annex III] 
2. Pre-assessment - A team for loss assessment is formed where an agriculture dept 
representative, farmer representative and insurance rep are involved. (Form-2) [Annex III] 
3. Assessment and reporting - The team does an eye estimate of the scale of loss and prepares 
a final report (Form-3) [Annex III] 
4. Compensation - The farmer is compensated in terms of the per hectare input cost fixed by 
the district level committee. i.e., claim would be scale of loss (e.g., 50%) * per ha input cost* 
sown area

Limitations & Challenges  
1. Subjective assessment  – Visual assessment of the damage is subject to inaccuracies as it 
depends on human judgement.  
2. Manpower availability - Non availability of qualified manpower for assessment 
3. Multiple interfaces – Farmer must deal with multiple interfaces for getting his/her due 
4. Disputes – The insurance agency may dispute the assessment, and thi s further delays the 
compensation of loss to the farmer 
 
 
Figure 11 Root Cause Analysis for subjective localised loss assessment 
5.2.2. Recommendation for process improvement 
With different technological advancements and satellite imagery, the traditional method of crop loss 
assessment can be made efficient and fool-proof.  
The PMFBY recommends the use of technology in agriculture. For example, using technology solutions 
for assessing the damage to crops due to localized calamity in case of  a dispute between the 
stakeholders concerne d. Also, mobile phone applications can be integrated with the proposed 
solution to reduce delays in claim settlements by uploading crop damage data online. 
As a component of the Pilot Study/Proof of Concept, loca lized calamity-based crop loss assessment 
from drone-based and satellite-based imagery can be used to arrive at the accurate scale of loss. This 
can assist in arriving at a cost/benefit analysis by determining the efficacy and efficiency of time, effort 
and suitability for large scale roll out of the same.  
Recommended process 
Usage of satellite imagery to build the ML model for kharaba driven change detection & drone imagery 
for on-the-spot assessment & reporting of damage.

Advantages of the recommended process 
1. Technology-based unbiased loss assessment for all the stakeholders involved thus re ducing 
human error or any moral hazard  
2. Reduced incidences of disputes by the IC thus leading to timely release of compensation to 
the farmer - shortening the present cycle of minimum 32 days by 50% at least 
3. Long term objective of proactive prediction of k haraba events and prompt identification of 
kharaba sites for drone-based survey without the need for any intimation by farmer  
4. Reduced risk of non-availability of qualified loss assessors for physical loss assessment surveys  
5. A solution well integrated wit h the present platforms and portals used  in localised loss 
assessment 
 
5.2.3. Solution Requirements – Kharaba  
 
The objective of this project is to develop a Computer Vision ML algorithm which upon training can 
provide kharaba assessment (identification and verification) primarily based on crop type data, image 
processing data (images captured in multi/hyper spectral ranges through drones and satellites) and 
weather data. The model will also incorporate classifying factors (such as field data, soil data etc. 
mapped to GIS services) that enable better predictive outcomes. 
This kharaba solution will aim to provide: 
1. Crop profiles: Crop profiles for target crops (rice and wheat) will be created. These profiles 
will track crops through their agricultural cycle (from sow ing till harvesting) via mu lti/hyper 
spectral imaging and develop day -to-day models of what a healthy crop should look like (in 
their spectral signature) at various stages in the crop’s life.  
2. Identification of kharaba event: Crop profiles will also be trained using kharaba events in the 
state of Haryana. Multi/Hyper spectral images of fields with such kharaba incidents will be 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
282
Page | 31  
 
used to train the model such that upon completion of model training: the model can identify 
areas of kharaba based on deviations fr om the crop’s expected (pre vious day’s) spectral 
signature. i.e., the trained model will look at the crop’s spectral progression from day-to-day 
(all days of data capture at determined intervals) and identify any spectral deviations from 
expected spectral signature that indicate a k haraba incident (such as hailstorm, landslide, 
inundation, cloudburst, fire due to lightning etc.)  
3. Statewide coverage via satellite : Satellite images at suitable resolution (resolution to be 
determined during pilot based on sensitivity and cost factors) will be captured and processed 
frequently (daily, or multiple times in a week) such that the model develops a crop spectral 
signature based on the satellite’s data. Once the model is built successfully (during pilot), any 
deviations from expected spectral s ignature will indicate possible kharaba events (that can 
further be verified and validated through focused drone-based coverage). Satellite model will 
be trained to cover all areas of the state through integration with GIS mapped factors such as 
topography, weather profile etc. 
4. Focused coverage via drones : Once farmer reports a kharaba event, drones will be used to 
capture detailed images of the identified sites. Then the drone-based model will use its crop 
spectral signature model to identify actual extent and area of kharaba. The identified kharaba 
area (which will be mapped to fields and farmers through integration with GIS and farmer 
information portals such as MFMB) will enable f urther action as required ( e.g., government 
record keeping and compensation actions). 
5. Services: Once pilot is successful and capable of reliable kharaba identification (and 
verification) through satellite and drone-based models, services layer (detailed in section 
5.2.7) will be developed for state-wide go-live and adoption.  
5.2.4. Solution Approach & methodology – Kharaba  
With the objective of creating a solution that is viable and can be scaled across the state, the project 
will proceed in three phases:

 Phase 1 – Definition of detailed project requirements and execution plan.  
NISG team will work on the projects approved by the government and define detailed requirements 
such that the desired project outcome is measurable and scalable. This phase will lead to creation of 
project reports (with acceptance criteria for pilot success/failure, and execution and scale -up plans) 
and RFPs for on-boarding of suitable SI/vendors. 
Phase 2 – Pilot phase 
Key outcome of this phase  will be development of an AI/ML model that can optimize the target 
agricultural operations. This model: 
1. Will utilize inputs such as: 
• Ground truthing  
• GIS map integration with field boundaries (through revenue map, or via Ground Truthing) 
• Weather data (standard weather services) 
• Rainfall data (moisture sensors, weather stations, or standard weather services) 
• Agriculture zone mapping 
• Terrain features/topography 
• Remote sensing data from satellites 
• Multi/Hyper spectral image and data capture using drones  
• Data from soil testing  
• Crop type (and variety) data 
• On ground sensors and weather stations  
• Crop information portals such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’ portal of Govt. of Haryana 
  
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
284
Page | 33  
 
2. Will be a Computer Vision model that is optimized using controls such as: 
· Multi/Hyper spectral image input – visible, near and far infrared, microwave data to 
support multilevel analysis of vegetation indices (such as moisture stress index, NVDI, 
LWCI etc.). Image d ata is captured via satellite remote sensing and drone -based 
sensors. 
· Image capture parameter management – Ensuring that drone image data is factored 
for parameters such as angle of capture, angle of sun on horizon, season, height of 
capture etc. 
· Establishment of appropriate data cleaning and ETL pro cesses to ensure usable data 
is fed to the model. 
  
3. Will be tested against set acceptance levels (model accuracy levels to be finalized in phase 1) 
such that the model can: 
· Achieve predictions and classification /clustering accuracy that helps optimize 
agricultural operations 
· Can help increase efficiency (reduce turnaround time and effort) for government 
enabled farmer support in areas such as loss assessment, yield estimation etc. 
  
Phase 3 – Scale-Up across state   
Pilots that pass the acceptance criteria will be analyzed by the appointed NISG consulting team and 
Government agencies (Haryana and central agencies as applicable) to determine viability of scaling 
the pilots across state. These scaled go-live projects may be based on a combination of different pilots 
(such that the scaling project can provide  maximum benefit in terms of better and optimized 
operations) and may consist of multiple stages.  
These scaled go -live projects will then provide actionable data and recommendations to all 
stakeholders (including farmers, businesses working in agriculture space, Govt. of India, and Govt. of 
Haryana). Examples of such interactions and integrations are:  
• GoI PMFBY 
• GoI app – Crop cutting 
• Portal for agriculture businesses (such as seed sellers, private buyers, shelling mills etc.) 
• Interface for farmers 
• Interface for farming service providers (such as harvester owners, stubble management 
machine providers, transporters etc.) 
• Planning and futures businesses (commodities markets, Govt. planning departments etc.)

As shown in the diagram above, the Kharaba ML model can include the following aspects: 
1. Physical Layer – Physical data is captured such that a classification/clustering model can be 
built that identifies crop spectral signatures (and defines threshold for deviations). 
a. Kharaba Validation (Event Processing): Images of physically verified kharaba incidents 
(verified as per pre-existing process) will be captured via drones and satellites to train 
their classification/clustering models (in conjunction with multi/hyper spectral crop 
profile being d eveloped for crop areas without kharaba). This data will be used in 
75:25 ratio for training: validation.  
b. Multi/Hyper spectral images: Images of healthy crops captured for multiple spectrum 
ranges through drones an d satellites will act as the primary inde pendent variable 
(based on which the ML model will identify area and extent of kharaba). These images 
will create a crop profile (with spectral signature) by tracking crops through their 
agricultural cycle.  
c. Capture of predictor and classification factors:  Yield prediction model will consider 
factors such as: 
i. Soil testing data at beginning of sowing season (if available) 
ii. Predefined factors built into model layers such as: Field boundaries, soil type, 
topography, built on a GIS based map 
iii. Crop type – will be received from portals such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’ 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)

iv. Weather profile (including parameters such as rainfall, temperature, pressure 
etc.)  
2. Computational Layer – Development of the model will require the following: 
a. Processing Engines: These are a set of engines and modules that will allow training for 
ML model 
i. Complex Event Processing – Event engine that triggers appropriate modules 
based on input trigger  
ii. Infrastructure engines – Depending on the platform used for tr aining the 
model (platform such as cloud based in AWS or Azure etc., or on -premises 
platforms) the infrastructure engine will need to support scaling and 
virtualization 
iii. Big Data Algorithms – Suitable Big data processing algorithm will need to be 
setup to manage and process all incoming data streams 
iv. Event Analytics Engine – Processes events to determine trends in frequency 
and special distribution of kharaba events. This would include analytics such 
as: 
1. Analysis of patterns in specific villages or specific farmers facing 
repeated kharaba events. 
2. Analysis of geo-spacial patterns in lands more susceptible to 
kharaba events 
v. ML algorithm: Satellite - ML algorithm based on a suitable framework will be 
setup as the ML engine for satellite model. Satellite model wil l be trained to 
identify healthy crop signatures, and kharaba affected crop signatures (that 
can be verified through drone operations)  
b. Data services: These are databases and data warehouse structures that store and 
manage factors used for training the model 
i. Soil data – Captures data about soil quality through lab testing of areas under 
pilot. 
ii. GIS and pre -defined data – Data for factors (including field boundaries, soil 
type, topography) that are expected to be static during the pilot are mapped 
to a GIS platform for use in training the algorithm 
iii. Crop database – Captures crop, field, and farmer data (which can be retrieved 
from Haryana Govt. initiatives such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’) and sets the 
data as a layer on GIS map and as an input to the ML multiva riate 
clustering/classification algorithm 
iv. Weather database – Captures factors such as precipitation profile, rainfall 
profile etc. for building the model 
v. Image processing – Captures and saves image data (and metadata) for use in 
training the algorithm. Thi s image data will act a s the primary independent 
variable for kharaba processing 
vi. Verified Kharaba database – Data of verified kharaba incidents is fed to the 
model for training classification/clustering capabilities of the model. 
vii. Claims Management – Manages data of claims processed for kharaba events 
such as: compensation paid, area damaged, event history etc. 
viii. Event Profiles - Manages event profiles and history such that analytics can 
be performed for special and historical trends of kharaba events.

3. Control Layer: During training , this layer will be the means of controlling, training, and 
monitoring the development of ML model. Both SI users and Govt. users will access the model 
being trained through web portal as well as app. 
a. Web Portal – Will be used to monitor and manage the model being produced. 
b. Mobile App – Will be used to provide data to the model (data such as ground truthing,

5.3. Pest, Weed & Disease management 
Pest, weed, and disease surveillance requires regular assessment of farms to identify early signs of 
infestation and damage. The present methods are based on routine surveys by the district officials 
and intimation of crop damage locations by the farmers. The PMFBY covers the risk of crop loss only 
due to widespread pest and disease incidence under the regular basic cover. In case of early onset or 
localised damage due to pests and diseases the farmer is left with limited options to recover his loss. 
This necessitates regular monitoring of crop health and early identification of pests in disease 
infestation to proverbially nip the problem in the bud. 
Major crop diseases and pest infestations in Haryana are as below

As part of the As-Is Study, NISG has had interactions with officials of the Directorate of Agriculture at 
Krishi Bhavan, Panchkula, District officials of Department of Agriculture, Yamuna Nagar & District 
officials of Department of Agriculture, Karnal. The following observations were made of the current 
state of processes related to pest, weed & disease management in the state of Haryana: 
5.3.1. As-Is Assessment 
1. Monitoring – Self monitoring by farmer & field surveys conducted by Plant Protection officials 
during favourable weather 
2. Detection - Farmer detects infestation and approaches district officials for further sur vey or 
infestations are manually located during regular surveys by field officials 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
291
Page | 40  
 
3. Management – Mostly manual spray pump is used for pesticide spraying. Battery operated 
and tractor mounted pumps are also used occasionally 
4. Education and awareness - Timely advisories are issued in local newspapers to the farmers 
regarding pest attacks based on the field surveys. Farmer Training camps are conducted to 
make the farmers aware of how to protect their crops against pest and diseases 
5. Technology - No ICT channel is used as of now 
 
 
Limitations & Challenges 
1. Manual methods of monitoring and survey can lead to slippage of early detection of crop 
infestations leading to extensive crop damage 
2. Limited manpower availability at the district headquarters limits extens ive surveys  and 
hampers early detection and damage control 
3. Manual application of pesticides by the farmers is bound to create health problems for the 
farmers and lead to additional cost burden of medical aid 
4. Blind and extensive application of pesticides, herbicides, fungicides not only hamper the 
nearby crops but has damaging effect on the soil and sub soil fauna health 
 
 
Figure 16 Root Cause Analysis for issues in pest, weed and disease surveillance 
5.3.2. Recommendation for process improvement 
Most effective emerging technologies solutions are based on capture of data in the form of 
images/photos and processing the data through image pro cessing-based ML algorithms. An ML 
algorithm running in the backend can ingest crop information and image data from  field cameras to 
develop its knowledge of the crops and pests/weed. Such algorithms have developed weed detection 
accuracies of 70-96% when using algorithms such as SVM, RF, KNN in pilot tests.  
a. The ML algorithm (post training) will start identifying inci dents of pest infestation 
(such as beetles/locusts) through cameras and will provide advance warning of 
upcoming infestation events. 
b. ML algorithm (post training) will also start predicting weed and disease events based 
on its database of such events in the past.

Recommended process 
Drone surveillance can be used to do field surveys and regular monitoring. The images collected by

Advantages of the proposed process 
1. No slippage of early disease detection

unfavorable weather 
3. No manual spraying required hence positively impacting farmer health 
4. Precise application of pesticides to the affected area and not overuse 
 
5.3.3. Solution Requirements – Pest, Weed and Disease Surveillance  
The objective of this project is to develop a Computer Vision ML algorithm which upon training can 
identify an infestation/infection event (caused by pest, weed or disease) through changes in the crop’s 
spectral signature.  
1. Spectral signature for the healthy crops will be developed using multi/hyper spectral imaging. 
Infestation/infection free fields will be used for development of this signature. 
2. Spectral signatures for infestation/infectio n affected crops will be developed using 
multi/hyper spectral imaging.  
a. Fields with infestation/infection will be identified during the pilot and monitored for 
developing this signature. 
b. Tie-up with the state agriculture university may also be required to develop signatures 
for infestation/infection not prevalent in the p ilot district blocks, and for controlled 
development of such spectral signatures. 
3. Only infestation/infection events that create a spectral signature statistically distinguishable 
from healthy crop will be worked upon further in the pilot.  
a. Statistically significant spectral differences can be expected in diseases that develop 
visible changes (or non-visible changes that can be observed in other electromagnetic 
spectrum ranges). Diseases such as brown rust of wheat, rice blast and others can be 
considered for development of such signatures. 
4. Spectral signatures will be developed using both drone sourced and satellite sourced data. 
Data captured from drones will be used to further train satellite model such that satellite data-
based ML model can learn to predic t/identify suspected infestation/infection events. Once 
infestation/infection is identified by satellite-based model (or through drone-based coverage, 
or reported by ground staff/farmers), drones will be used to assess the extent and location of 
damage (using ML model with GIS integration). 
5. Services – Once pilot is successful, and capable of infestation/infection detection, a services 
layer will be built on top of the ML model (generated in pilot) such that services can be 
provided to farmers (prediction an d/or verification of infestation/infection), government 
agencies (GIS mapped infestation/infection data to predict expected damage in the season) 
and to other stakeholders involved in agriculture.

5.3.4. Solution Approach – Pest, Weed and Disease Surveillance  
With the objective of creating a solution that is viable and can be scaled across the state, the project 
will proceed in three phases: 
 
Figure 17 Phases of solution development and deployment - Pest, weed & disease surveillance  
Phase 1 – Definition of detailed project requirements and execution plan.  
NISG consulting team will work on the projects approved by the government and define detailed 
requirements such that the desired project outcome is measurable and scalable. This phase will lead 
to creation of project reports (with acceptance criteria for pilot success/failure, and execution and 
scale-up plans) and RFPs for on-boarding of suitable SI/vendors. 
Phase 2 – Pilot phase 
Key outcome of this phase will be de velopment of an AI/ML model that can optimize the target 
agricultural operations. This model would be built through the following phases: 
1. Data Collection - Utilization of inputs such as: 
• Ground truthing  
• GIS map integration with field boundaries (through revenue map, or via Ground Truthing) 
• Weather data (standard weather services) 
• Rainfall data (moisture sensors, weather stations, or standard weather services) 
• Agriculture zone mapping 
• Terrain features/topography 
• Remote sensing data from satellites
