• Multi/Hyper spectral image and data capture using drones  
• Data from soil testing  
• Crop type (and variety) data 
• On ground sensors and weather stations  
• Crop information portals such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’ portal of Govt. of Haryana 
  
2. Model Creation - Creation & optimization using controls such as: 
· Multi/Hyper spectral image input – visible, near and far infrared, microwave data to 
support multilevel analysis of vegetation indices (such as moisture stress index, NVDI, 
LWCI etc.). Image data is captured via satellite remote sensing and drone -based 
sensors. 
· Image capture parameter management – Ensuring that drone image data is factored 
for parameters such as angle of capture, angle of sun on horizon, season, height of 
capture etc. 
· Establishment of appropriate data cleaning and ETL processes to ensure usable data 
is fed to the model. 
  
3. Prediction & accuracy analysis - Model to be tested against set acceptance levels (model 
accuracy levels to be finalized in phase 1) such that the model can: 
· Achieve predictions and classification/clustering accuracy that helps optimize 
agricultural operations 
· Can help increase efficiency (reduce turnaround time and effort) for government 
enabled farmer support in areas such as loss assessment, yield estimation etc. 
  
Phase 3 – Scale-Up across state   
Pilots that pass the acceptance criteria will be analyzed by the appointed NISG consulting team and 
Government agencies (Haryana and central agencies as applicable) to determine viability of scal ing 
the pilots across state. These scaled go-live projects may be based on a combination of different pilots 
(such that the scaling project can provide maximum benefit in terms of better and optimized 
operations) and may consist of multiple stages.  
These scaled go -live projects will then provide actionable data and recommendations to all 
stakeholders (including farmers, businesses working in agriculture space, Govt. of India, and Govt. of 
Haryana). Examples of such interactions and integrations are:  
• GoI PMFBY 
• GoI app – Crop cutting 
• Portal for agriculture businesses (such as seed sellers, private buyers, shelling mills etc.) 
• Interface for farmers 
• Interface for farming service providers (such as harvester owners, stubble management 
machine providers, transporters etc.) 
• Planning and futures businesses (commodities markets, Govt. planning departments etc.)

As shown in the diagram above, the Pest, Weed and Disease surveillance ML model can include the 
following aspects:

built that identifies spectral signatures for healthy crops, spectral signatures for 
infestation/infection affected crops and defines threshold for differentiation between the 
two. 
a. Infestation/infection event processing: Images of physically verified 
infestation/infection incidents (verified as per pre -existing process) will be captured 
via drones and satellites to train their classification/clustering models (in conjunction 
with multi/hyper spectral crop profile being developed for crop areas without 
infestation/infection). This data will be used in 75:25 ratio for training: validation. In 
addition, farmers will be given an app interface to upload pic s of suspected 
infestation/infection events that the model can identify and advice farmer as to the

suspected cause of infection as well as appropriate remedial measures that the farmer

signature) by tracking crops through their agricultural cycle.  
c. Capture of predictor and classification factors: Yield predi ction model will consider 
factors such as: 
i. Soil testing data at beginning of sowing season (if available) 
ii. Predefined factors built into model layers such as: Field boundaries, soil type,

iii. Crop type – will be received from portals such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’ 
iv. Weather profile (including parameters such as rainfall, temperature, pressure 
etc.)  
2. Computational Layer – Development of the model will require the following: 
a. Processing Engines: These are a set of engines and modules that will allow training for 
ML model 
i. Complex Event Processing – Event engine that triggers appropriate modules 
based on input trigger  
ii. Infrastructure engines – Depending on the platform used for training the 
model (platform such as cloud based in AWS  or Azure etc., or on -premises 
platforms) the infrastructure engine will need to support scaling and 
virtualization

iv. Analytics Engine – An analytics engine will be built to determine areas of high 
and/or repeated infestation/infection events.

(framework that is supported by other infrastructure components proposed  
by pilot SI) such as TensorFlow, Pytorch, keras (or other) will be setup as the

1. Drone ML model will be trained to identify actual

2. Drone ML model will also be trained to identify healthy crop spectral 
signatures (through classification/clustering). 
vi. ML algorithm: Satellite - ML algorithm based on a suitable framework will be 
set up as the ML engine for satellite model. Satellite models will be trained to 
identify healthy crop signatures, and infestation/infection affected crop 
signatures (that can be verified through drone operations) and trigger actions 
for drone processing as required.  
b. Data services: These  are databases and data warehouse structures that store and

5.4. Use of blockchain as emerging technology in farm input tracing 
Farmers suffer from a lack of transparency around market demand/price which needs to be 
transformed, there is a need for trusted information/real -time market data using Blockchain 
framework which can help stakeholders to avoid multiple intermediaries. Blockchain technology has 
the potential to make the agriculture-supply chain more secure, transparent, and efficient. It promises 
end-to-end supply chain visibility and allows one to trace the origin of produce and trac k a 
product/produce during its journey in a supply chain. Blockchain solutions, if implemented, may lead 
to the elimination of intermediari es or middlemen, thereby leading to improved pricing, decreased 
transaction fees, thus eliminate issues of hoarding.  
As part of the As-Is Study, NISG has had interactions with officials of the Directorate of Agriculture at 
Krishi Bhavan, Panchkula, Distri ct officials of Department of Agriculture, Yamuna Nagar & District 
officials of Department of Agriculture, Karnal. Th e following observations were made of the current 
state of processes related to farm input management in the state of Haryana: 
5.4.1. As-Is Assessment 
- State Govt. Is the licensing authority for manufacturing/production/storage 
- Licensing authority for Wholesale (C &F), retail, distribution is the Deputy Director (District 
level) 
1. Seeds 
a. Licensing - Seed processing plant license is valid for 5 years. Manufacturing license 
given by Directorate and verification done by the district. Selling license is granted by 
district authority 
b. Production – Seeds are produced in seed production plants  
c. Testing - Seeds are tested for germination, moisture content, spuriousness (Refer 
Annex III for sample lab reports) 
d. Certifications: 
i. Certified Seeds (Authenticated by Seed Certification agencies) 
ii. TL (Truthful Level) -Truthful level Seeds (Self-Certified by manufacturers) 
e. Sampling - Random sampling of the seeds is carried out by QCI, subdivisional 
agricultural officers and field staff at retailer, distributer, and wholesaler levels. Which 
is then sent for testing at one of the state seed testing laboratories. 
f. Testing & Certification agency – Online process exists for seed testing at labs in 
Karnal, Sirsa, Kurukshetra, Rohtak & Panchkula. Certification is carried out by the state 
seed certification agency through its HQ & Regional offices  
g. Sample failure - If sample fails, then sale of entire lot is stopped, and a case is lodges 
against the company and the dealer 
h. Supply chain  – Manufacturing plant --> Wholesaler --> Distributer --> Retailer --> 
Farmer 
 
 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
302
Page | 51  
 
2. Pesticides 
a. Licensing – Lifelong validity of the license for the pesticide manufacturing plant. 
Manufacturing license given by Directorate and verification done by the district. 
Selling license is granted by district authority 
b. Production - Registration by C entral Insecticide Board (Faridabad) required for 
manufacturing.  
c. Sampling/Testing/Certification and failure management steps are like above 
d. Supply chain  – Manufacturing plant --> Wholesaler --> Distributer --> Retailer --> 
Farmer 
 
3. Fertiliser  
a. Licensing – Fertiliser manufacturing license is valid for 5 years. Manufacturing license 
given by Directorate and verification done by the district. Selling license is granted by 
district authority. 
b. Technology Portal - Mobile Fertiliser Monitoring System (M FMS) exists for end-to-
end monitoring of the fertiliser from production to sale  
c. Production - Manufacturer must enter the production value at the Portal 
d. Allocation - GoI allocates state wise allocation, State Gov allocates district wise, and 
District allocates Retailer wise. Rack allocation is done for fertilisers at the retail level 
e. Supply chain  – Manufacturing plant --> Wholesaler --> Distributer --> Retailer --> 
Farmer 
f. Last mile delivery  - Farmer pr ocures the fertiliser from the retailer through POS 
machines after thumbing (mandatory) and giving Aadhar Number. 
g. Subsidy - Bulk fertilisers are subsidised. If a farmer purchases more than 50 bags per 
month per single tran saction, then it gets highlighted in the MIS available at the 
district.  
h. DBT (Direct Beneﬁt Transfer) - Farmers can avail subsidy for the purchase of fertilisers 
via DBT through online purchase via Haryana  Agriculture website but as of now it is 
unverifiable. 
The present process of input monitoring is based on manual process except the online platforms used 
for testing in insecticides and MFMS in fertilisers. Lack of integration of all these platforms necessitates 
an over – arching system which can trace the produce from production to application by farmers and 
help in ascertaining the authenticity and efficiency of use of these inputs 
5.4.2. Recommended Process Improvement 
The following figure provides a schematic of various interacting parties and their activity data that is 
collected by using Point of Sale (POS)/Internet of Things (IoT) devices and t hen recorded on a 
Blockchain for offering transparency and traceability of the inputs/produce from farm to the 
processor. 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
303
Page | 52  
 
 
Figure 20 Process flow of Agri-output from farm to processors – Transparency through traceability 
Pilot 1 - Pesticide, Seed, and Fertilizer Tracking: Tracking of seeds, pesticides, and fertilizers from the 
factory to the farmer, ensuring that counterfeit products are not introduced in the intermediary 
stages. As in the case of Haryana state, 60% of pesticides are  sourced from outside the state (from 
nearly 170 pesticide units) and the remaining percentage of pesticides is locally manufactured in 
approximately 150 manufacturing units. Affixing QR codes on the pesticide batches when they are 
manufactured can aid in capturing key details such as date of m anufacture, batch number, place of 
manufacture, quantity, factory accreditation and information about the chemical composition of the 
fertilizers. Once the QR code is scanned at the factory and the consignment leaves the site, the data 
is fed into the blockchain. This process is repeated at all subsequent stages till selling to the farmer in 
the supply chain to guarantee the chain of custody and thus precluding the entry of expired, non -
standard pesticides into the market.  
 
Figure 21 Pilot 1 - High level Pesticide, Seed and Fertilizer Tracking diagram 
Pilot 2 - Food Grain/Produce Tracking : The above -mentioned solution deploys a blockchain 
application designed to operate with a simple smartphone interface, either through the applicati on 
itself or by linking it with existing interfaces and systems for data capture. The following diagram 
describes one of the potential use cases of a blockchain-based supply chain:

Figure 22 Pilot 2 - High level Food Grain/Produce Tracking diagram 
Advantages 
1. Disintermediation of the multiple agents involved between harvest and selling the crop in the 
mandis. This will benefit the farmer helping them get better value for their produce 
2. Immutability and source tracking for the use of farm inputs. This will clearly establish the 
quality of the inputs used, hence help the buyer get access to better produce and help the 
farmer get value for it produce. 
5.4.3. Solution Requirements – Blockchain in Agriculture 
  
The purpose of this pilot is to use the immutability and disintermediation aspects of blockchain 
solutions for the benefit of farmers. This can be achieved by implementing the following solution in a 
phased manner (phases 1 and 2 are part of the current proposed pilot. If the current pilot is adjudged 
successful, then steps 3 through 5 will be tested through a subsequent pilot before state-wide rollout): 
1. Input traceability: Agricultural inputs will be tracked such that the nature and quality of the 
harvested crop can be controlled. This will include: 
a. Seed type – Source/type of seed will be recorded on blockchain through tie-ups with 
seed sellers. 
b. Fertilizer – Fertilizers that are used by the farmer in their crop will be recorded on 
blockchain and tied with the time of purchase. 
c. Pesticide – Pesticide sales in the pilot area will be tracked such that pesticides used in 
a farmer’s crop can accurately be identified. 
2. Harvest: Crop details and metrics will be recorded on blockchain (with immutable 
characteristics) 
a. Farmers will enter their harvesting date and their planned date of mandi arrival on a 
farmer app (app links to agricultural procurement blockchain).  
i. In subsequent pilot (pilot #2) this will allow for load planning at the Mandi, 
and features such as: if the Mandi does not have space on the planned day of 
farmer arrival, the app will ask farmer to arrive on a subsequent day (on FCFS 
basis). 
b. Samples of the harvest will be tested for parameters such as protein content, 
pesticide residue etc.  
3. Smart storage: Harvested (and tested) crop will be next tracked on blockchain from harvest 
to its arrival at Mandi. At the Mandi: 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
305
Page | 54  
 
a. The farmer’s harvest  will be transferred to storage silos. These silos will contain 
harvest of a single farmer, or multiple farmers where th e harvest has similar quality 
profile (similar nutrition profile, and similar fertilizer and pesticide use history).  
b. The silos will be published on the blockchain, and the crop (in silo lots) will be made 
available for procurement. 
4. Procurement: When the crop is published for procurement (where the time of availability for 
procurement and the quality of crop is immutably recorded on blockchain) it will be shown to 
procurement agencies. Here the procurement agencies can be state agencies, central 
agencies and private buyers who will bid for the crop on silo basis. This can be designed as: 
a. State agencies will publish their MSP rate based on crop quality 
b. Central agencies will publish their MSP rate based on crop quality 
c. Private buyers can bid at rates higher than the MSP rate to buy the crop. 
d. Farmer can set minimum acceptable bid price for private buyers with the option to 
negotiate the price. 
5. Payment: Once a bid is successful and procurement is finalized, the payment will be recorded 
on blockchain. Once payment  is transferred, the ownership of silo containing the farmer’s 
harvest will be transferred to the buyer. This will be done through a smart contract such that 
no other intermediaries are required. 
  
5.4.4. Solution Approach – Blockchain in Agriculture 
  
With the objective of creating a solution that is viable and can be scaled across the state, the project 
will proceed in four phases: 
Phase 1 – Definition of detailed project requirements and execution plan.  
A consulting team will work on the projects approved by t he Government and define detailed 
requirements such that the desired project outcome is measurable and scalable. This phase will le ad 
to creation of project reports (with acceptance criteria for pilot success/failure, and execution and 
scale-up plans) and RFPs for on-boarding of suitable SI/vendors. 
Phase 2 – Pilot-1 
Pilot will involve on -boarding of farmers and on -boarding of seed, fertilizer, and pesticide sellers in 
the pilot areas onto the common platform (with blockchain in the data layer). Once operat ions are 
stabilized and tested through two cropping seasons ( kharif & rabi ), the pilot will be assessed to 
understand the accuracy of data being generated to determine if a second pilot is to be attempted or 
not. Data accuracy will be adjudged by comparing  expected harvest parameters (such as expected 
nutrition content based on agricultural inputs: seeds, fertilizers etc.) against the actual/tested harvest 
parameters (parameters based on testing of crop samples). 
Phase 3 – Pilot-2 
If Pilot-2 is to be conduc ted, it will involve on -boarding of select Mandis and on -boarding of buyers 
onto the common platform (with blockchain in the data l ayer). Once operations are stabilized and 
tested through subsequent cropping seasons (kharif & rabi), the pilot will be assessed to understand 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
306

the benefits realized, and a decision will be taken on whether the pilot should be scaled across the

5.4.5. Pilot for Blockchain in Agriculture 
  
Indicative reference architecture for blockchain enabled procurement is shown in the figure below.  
Note that pilot-1 will focus on farmer services and farming inputs. If pilot -2 is to be executed, it will 
expand the work into Mandi and Procurement services. 
 
Figure 23 Indicative architecture for Blockchain based solutions 
  
As shown in the diagram above, the solution can include the following aspects: 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
307
Page | 56  
 
1. Data Layer – These are databases and data warehouse structures that store and manage 
factors used for operationalizing the blockchain based solution. 
a. Farmer Data and Profiles (pilot-1) – Farmer and farming databases will carry data such 
as farmer location, farm size, crop type etc. either as a local database on pilot servers, 
or on Govt. of Haryana servers (in portals such as Meri Fasal Mera Byora). 
b. Farming Inputs Database (pilot -1) – Will be databas es that manage data of farming 
inputs such as pesticide/fertilizer and data of the sellers of these inputs. 
c. Mandi Data (pilot-2) – Carries data related to procurement and sales at fasal Mandis. 
This will include data such as Mandi capacity, Silo availability, Mandi labor availability, 
Registered bidders (for procurement), MSP data etc. 
d. Buyer Data (pilot -2) – Carries data of the buyers such  as buyer profiles, planned 
purchase volume, banking information etc. 
2. Blockchain – Blockchain will act as a data management and smart transaction processing layer 
such that applications and modules can be built on top of the facilities provided by this layer. 
This layer will contain features such as: 
a. Verified Crop History (pilot -1) – Crop history based on events such as pur chase of 
seed, purchase of fertilizer/pesticide will be maintained in its immutable nature so 
that: 
i. The data is validated by farmer and seller when the transaction is executed 
ii. The data is available to all permissioned actors on the blockchain platform. 
b. Sampling and test reports (pilot -1) – Data of the tested crop characteristics 
(nutritional content, pesticide residue etc.) will be saved and linked to a farmer’s crop 
data. These tests will be performed by state labs and entered onto the blockchain by 
the transacting parties i.e., the farmer and the testing lab. 
c. Compliance Certifications (pilot-1) – Compliance certifications (if any) received by the 
farmers will be saved on the blockchain by the transacting parties i.e., the farmer and 
the certifying agency. This will include certificates based on tie-ups with procurement 
agencies (private players included) as well as certifications provided by Govt. and 
Semi-Government agencies.  
d. Smart Contracts (pilot -2)– Smart contracts will be set up to provide transaction  
processing without human intervention such as: 
i. Automatic transfer of ownership of silos to buyer upon realization of 
payment. 
ii. Automatic classification of harvest based on crop parameters (such as 
nutritional content, pesticide residue etc.) and grouping o f silos with similar 
contents at the Mandi for auction purposes. 
e. User Account Management – Users will be set up on a permissioned blockc hain 
platform such as IBM’s Hyperledger (as envisioned by DITECH Haryana as per their 
blockchain CoE plans) or similar. Users will be added to pilot-1 and pilot-2 as required. 
f. Certificate Authority – Provides services (to users of blockchain) such as enrol lment 
services, transaction services and triggers, TLS certification etc. 
3. Application Modules – The following processing module groups will be developed 
a. User Onboarding – Users will be set up on the platform such that they can: 
i. Access data from platform 
ii. Buy/Sell/Provide services 
iii. Perform transactions 
iv. Save, process and receive/request verified and certified data

b. Bid Managemen t and Transaction processing – This module will process all non -
blockchain transactions such as financial transactions, bids, mandi acce ss charges, 
labor payment schedules etc. 
c. Auxiliary Services – These services will integrate with modules required to 
operationalize the blockchain based procurement solution, and will include modules 
and linkages such as integration with government portal, integration with crop testing 
labs etc. 
4. Services Layer: A services layer will be built on top of the computational back end and will 
provide services such as: 
a. Farmer Services – Farmers will be able to access their farm data such as fertilization 
and pestic ide use schedules, crop profiles (nutritional analysis), a history of their 
implemented farm practices and payment management services. 
b. Farming Services – These are services for farming services providers such as seed, 
pesticide, and fertilizer sellers, and will include: 
i. Solution where service providers and farmers will be able to validate each 
other through a smartphone app based QR code. i.e., both farmer and seller 
can scan a QR code on the other party’s mobile app (mobile app to be created 
in the pilot) to identify the other party and authenticate transactions (sale or 
purchase). 
ii. Will include payment services such as linkage to UPI 
iii. Will provide for credit mechanism based on the arrangement between buyer 
and seller (using a smart contract-based payment transaction to be triggered 
on a later date). 
c. Mandi Services – Mandi services will include all serv ices related to workflow and 
capacity management such as silo management, farmer scheduling, 
loading/unloading service scheduling, labor service management etc. 
d. Procurement Services – These services will manage the sale/purchase processes for 
the crop, and  will include features such as bid management, tax management, 
transaction management, payment management etc. 
e. Integration Services – Integrations with platforms such as MFMB will be provided as 
part of integration services. 
  
5.4.6. Scaling-Up of Blockchain in Agriculture pilot   
  
Upon successful completion of pilot 1, the pilot will be evaluated by the consulting and state agencies 
on metrics such as adoption (of the solution) and accuracy (does the crop produce match the practices 
declared on blockchain). 
If pilot-1 is deemed successful, a decision is taken to proceed with pilot-2, pilot-2 will be run by adding 
crop buyers and mandis on the platform, and by proceedi ng with automation of the procurement 
process. Upon completion of pilot 2, it will be evaluated o n metrics such as disintermediation (have 
redundant processes and players been re-allocated) and farmer impact (impact on farmer income and 
farming practices). 
If pilot-2 is also deemed successful with a decision scale across state, the pilot will be made live across 
mandis in Haryana one -by-one (in a sequence and schedule to be decided by the pilot evaluation

5.5. Precision Farming 
Precision Agriculture can be defined as a farming management concept based on the science of 
improving crop yields and assisting decision making using advanced technology equipment/sensors 
and analysis tools. It can be realized through multiple technological interventions using AI/ML and 
drones for better farm management. 
As part of the As-Is Study, NISG has had interactions with officials of the Directorate of Agriculture at 
Krishi Bhavan, Panchkula, District officials of Department of Agriculture, Yamuna Nagar & District 
officials of Department of Agriculture, Karnal. The following observations were made of the curren t 
state of processes related to farm input management in the state of Haryana: 
5.5.1. As-Is Assessment  
1. No major precision farming techniques are followed presently except projects being run by 
state agencies such as MICADA with respect to micro irrigation 
2. Drip irrigation is currently sought on a limited scale for horticultural applications only. 
5.5.2. Recommendation for process improvement 
Usage of drone based localised insecticide spraying and fertiliser application technologies could 
significantly improve the efficie ncy of input usage and lead to higher returns to the farmer for the 
same or better yield. The efficacy of application and consequent yield improvement could also be 
tested during the pilot of major crops during the two seasons in Haryana.  
Pesticide spraying would be operationalised through autonomous drones which are programmed to 
identify the flight path based on the validated inputs from the ML model (developed using 
hyperspectral/multispectral images and consequent ground truthing from experts) 
Fertiliser spray would be operationalised by creating a plan for spray of foliar fertilisers (water soluble) 
and pellet-based fertilisers depending on the soil nutrient profile derived from on ground soil testing 
activities. 
 
Advantages 
1. Reduction in input usage per ha of the sown area 
2. Prevention of health hazards to the farmer due to manual application of insecticides and 
fertilisers 
3. Better soil and local fauna health due to reduced chemical usage 
4. Faster application with little manual interface 
5. Synchronisation with  Drone based  disease monitoring and pest control for effective area 
identification for pesticide application 
 
 
 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
312
Page | 61  
 
5.5.3. Solution Requirements – Precision Farming 
The objectives of this project are two-fold: 
1. To apply pesticide/weedicide/fungicide etc. for infestation/infection events that have been 
detected by ML model developed for pest, weed and disease surveillance (ref section on pilot 
for pest, weed and disease) 
a. Infestation/infection control solutions will be applied through automated interfaces 
such as drones. 
b. Cost benefit analysis will be done that compares cost and efficacy of manual 
application vs. automated application. This analysis will determine if this process 
should be scaled or not.  
2. To carry out initial soil quality testing with respect to t he crop type and  apply fertilizer 
accurately as per precision farming practices. 
a. Based on soil testing and crop type, a plan for fertilizer application will be created with 
type of fertilizer and application intervals. 
b. Required quantities of fertilizer will be appl ied through drone as per the drawn-up 
schedule (water soluble in liquid form and others in pellet form) 
c. Cost benefit analysis will be done based on  
i. crop yield,  
ii. and manual fertilization vs. automated fertilization costs. 
To implement these solutions: 
1. Fields in the pilot will be mapped to a GIS platform (with layers of soil type, crop type, 
topography, field boundaries etc. built into the map). 
2. Soil testing will be done prior to crop sowing such that a fertilization plan can be created (using 
support from Agriculture universities). 
a. Drones will be used for precision application of fertilizer according to the drawn-up 
schedule. 
3. Infestation events will be identified based on predictions from ML model (model from weed, 
pest, and disease pilot), or based on reporting from farmers. 
a. Drones will be used to precisely apply infestation/infection solutions (chemicals). The 
drones may cover areas in multiple fields (depending on the payload capacity) in a 
single flight.  
  
5.5.4. Solution Approach – Precision Farming 
With the objective of creating a solution that is viable and can be scaled across the state, the project 
will proceed in three phases: 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
313
Page | 62  
 
 
Figure 24 Phases of solution development and deployment - Precision Farming 
Phase 1 – Definition of detailed project requirements and execution plan.  
A consulting team will work on the projects approved by the government and define detailed 
requirements such that the desired project outcome is measurable and scalable. This phase will lead 
to creation of proje ct reports (with acceptance criteria for pilot success/failure, and execution and 
scale-up plans) and RFPs for on-boarding of suitable SI/vendors. 
Phase 2 – Pilot phase 
Key outcome of this phase will be development of precision farming practices that can o ptimize the 
target agricultural operations. These practices: 
1. Will utilize inputs such as: 
• Soil Quality data 
• GIS map integration with field boundaries (and other layers) 
• Weather data (standard weather services) 
• Infestation/infection data identified by ML algorithms, or by human observation. 
• Data from soil testing  
• Crop type (and variety) data 
• Crop information portals such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’ portal of Govt. of Haryana 
  
2. Will improve precision in application of fertilizer and pesticide/fungicide etc. by: 
· Use of GPS enabled drones to precisely apply the desired solution (fertilizer or 
pesticide/weedicide/fungicide etc.). 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)

· Automatic triggers for drone-based operation (reducing human dependence) 
· Allowing optimization of procurement (fertilizer or pesticide/ weedicide/fungicide 
etc.) by planning resource (and device/drone) utilization for the coverage area. 
  
3. Will be tested against set acceptance levels (to be finalized in phase 1) such that the solution 
passes cost benefit analysis that factors in: 
· Impact on yield (if any) 
· Cost factors 
· Time factors (time-based precision, and time saved in replacing manual practices) 
  
Phase 3 – Scale-Up across state   
Pilots that pass the acceptance criteria will be analyzed by the appointed NISG consulting team and 
Government agencies (Haryana and central agencies as applicable) to determine viability of scaling 
the pilots across state. These scaled go-live projects may be based on a combination of different pilots 
(such that the scaling project can provide maximum benefit in terms of better and optimized 
operations) and may consist of multiple stages.

5.5.5. Indicative solution architecture for pilot 
 Indicative reference architecture for precision farming pilot is shown in the figure below. 
 
Figure 25 Indicative solution for Precision Farming  
As shown in the diagram above, the solution can include the following aspects: 
1. Physical Layer – Physical Layer will rely on drone -based interfaces to collect imaging data as 
well as for precision agricultural operations such as spraying (fertilizer or infestation/infection 
control). 
a. Drones with multi/hyper spectral cameras will capture images of crops to determine 
infection/infestation events (using algorithm for infestation/infection created in pilot 
described in section 5.5.2).  
b. Drones with spraying (spray, stream, and misting) capabilities will be used to precisely 
apply fertilizer / pesticide / weedicide / fungicide etc. to affected crop areas. This will 
prevent overuse of chemicals and fertilizers. 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
316
Page | 65  
 
c. Soil Test Kits and IoT (Ground) Sensors – The data captured from soil test kits and on-
ground IoT sensors will help build and validate soil profile such that fertilization 
schedules and event can be setup. 
2. Middleware Layer – Development of the model will require a middleware layer that provides: 
a. Device Management: Devices including drones will be managed through this module. 
b. Energy Management: Devices will be tracked for energy dependence, energy 
efficiency and energy use. 
c. Data Capture: Data capture interfaces will allow consumption of data generated from 
field devices and will work through a network and encapsulation layer. 
3. Data Layer – These are databases and data warehouse structures that store and manage 
factors used for training the model. 
a. Soil data – Captures data about soil quality through lab testing of areas under pilot. 
b. GIS and pre -defined data – Data for factor s (including field boundaries, soil type, 
topography) that are expected to be static during the pilot are mapped to a GIS 
platform for use in training the algorithm. 
c. Crop database – Captures crop, field, and farmer data (which can be retrieved from 
Haryana Govt. initiatives such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’) and sets the data as a layer 
on GIS map. 
d. Fertilization Schedule – Captures fertilization needs of the crop based on soil testing 
data and the crop specific needs. This schedule will capture the time and quantity of 
specific fertilizers that need to be applied to the crop until harvesting.  
e. Image processing – Captures and saves image data (and metadata) for use in training 
appropriate algorithms and in verification of precision agriculture interventions.  
f. Infestation/Infection Event Database – Data of verified infestation/infection events is 
used to determine the precision agriculture intervent ion required (and to schedule 
the intervention). 
4. Processing Engines – The processing logic modules that will enable precision agriculture 
a. Schedule Management – Event engine that triggers appropriate modules based on 
input trigger or based on predefined events.  
b. Information Management – Information management system engine that enables all 
farmer, government, and agriculture services. 
c. Big Data Algorithms – Suitable Big data processing algorithm will need to be set up to 
manage and process all incoming data streams. 
d. Predictions Engine – Generates new events that require action from the precision 
agriculture system. The events are sent to schedule management module for 
processing. 
5. Services Layer: A services layer will be built during the pilot to enable precisi on agriculture 
operations. Once the pilot is completed and adjudged successful, the service layer will provide 
its users with features such as: 
a. Farmer Information management system – Farmers will be able to access their farm 
data such as fertilization and pesticide use schedules, soil health data and index, 
expected weather events data, recommended irrigation schedules etc. 
b. Data Management System – Usable and consumable data will be made available to 
farmers and other actors using the system. This data will allow for trend analysis and 
will act as a repository of past actions and schedules. 
c. Prediction and Alert Services – Theses alert services will keep stakeholders appraised 
of their events of interest. For instance, farmers will be notified of upcoming sur vey

or spray operations, agriculture services providers will be appraised of the demand of 
specific agricultural consumables (such as fertilizer) in their areas of operation. 
  
5.5.6. Indicative solution architecture for Scaling pilot  
Upon successful completion of pilot, the pilot can be scaled across Haryana state by repli cating the 
model across different blocks in the state.  
1. Analysis of the pilot data will determine the applications where automation developed in this 
pilot project provide a net gain based on cost benefit analysis (for example certain operations 
may only be suitable to farms of a minimum/maximum size) 
2. Training and sensitization of farmers new to the system will include training on the platform, 
and presentation of the cost benefit analysis. 
3. New Farmers can be brought on board through a scale up and replication plan. 
4. The platform and code developed for pilot will be installed at a data center (secure data center 
with scaling capabilities).

e. Real time synchronisation 
2. State/District Department officials 
a. Interface for issuing advisories to farmers in local language 
b. Spot checking /surveying 
c. Access to drone survey report 
d. Mobile application for ground truthing

7. Unified Architecture Envisioned 
  
All pilots under this project are designed such that they can be combined under a unified 
architecture. This approach towards architectural design will ensure that different successful pilots 
can use common hardware, computing and services layers leading to: 
1. Lower costs: since the project elements do not need to be setup and maintained separately. 
2. Better user experience: since user can access features for all pilots on a common interface.

8. Project Implementation approach & timeline  
Four blocks would be selected for the conduct of the pilot. The selectio n of the same would be done 
in ag reement with the Department. The department would be responsible for the provision of 
administrative clearances for ex. clearances for flying drones, clearance for usage of cadastral maps 
from the revenue dept., clearances,  support, and orders required for  the conduct of pilot/CCEs on 
non-governmental farms etc.  
Two cropping cycles are intended to be covered in the pilot. Here Rice/Paddy crop would be assessed 
in the Kharif season (Jun -Oct) and wheat crop would be assessed in the Rabi cropping season (Nov -
Apr). The pilot will run for 15 months (01 Apr 2022 – 30 Jun 2023).

9. Rollout/scale-up plan 
Roll out plan to be finalised based on evaluation of the pilot in terms of accuracy and the cost benefit 
analysis with respect to existing methodologies. 
Annexures 
Annex I 
Random sampling method for field identification 
Random Stratified sampling to select the district and the plot for carrying out the Crop Cutting 
Experiment (CCE) 
Selection tables are provided to the district by the NSSO to select the field (Column A) and the plot 
(Column B) for carrying out CCE. 4 CCEs need to be carried out for every crop in every gram panchayat. 
Hence total no. of CCEs in the district become very large which is a huge logistical and operational 
exercise during the end of the season (both Kharif and Rabi). 
For a given district the column no. to be referred (from the random table) is provided in the selection 
table provided by NSSO. The first number in the column (let’s say 6310) is then noted down. To identify 
the field the patwaris Khasra book/Sijra/register is taken and Killa no. 6310 is identified. If no. of Killas 
is less than the noted number, then the noted number is divided by the total no. of Killas, and 
remainder is calculated. The Killa /filed no. corresponding to the remainder nu mber is then selected 
for the CCE. Normally the size of the field is taken as 80 x 72 footsteps (roughly 1 acre) 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
322
Page | 71  
 
 
Annex II 
Random Sampling method for plot identification (applicable to both SST and Randomly selected 
field) 
Starting from the SW corner of the field and depending on the size of the farm another table is referred 
(separate for single, double- & triple-digit farm lengths). Then based on the selection table B provided 
by NSSO column no. in the random table is iden tified. For locating the length w ise point, the first 
number in the list greater than 80 -7=73 (footsteps) is selected. Similarly, along the width a similar 
point is selected (no. in column greater than 72-7 = 65 footsteps). 7 footsteps are reduced from overall 
field length and width becau se it is roughly equal to 5m. Hence a pair is identified from the random 
table to identify coordinates for CCE plot. Form this coordinate e.g. (72 footsteps east and 21 footsteps 
north) a plot is marked extending 5 m in the  north and 5m in the east. This a rea is measured and 
cordoned off with sticks and tape.

pages.title: Home | Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare | MoA & FW | Government of India, India
pages.text: Home | Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare | MoA & FW | Government of India, India कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE & FARMERS WELFARE भारत सरकार GOVERNMENT OF INDIA कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण मंत्रालय MINISTRY OF AGRICULTURE & FARMERS WELFARE Digital Agriculture Division Overview Digital Agriculture Mission Overview Digital Agriculture Mission aims to establish a comprehensive farmer-centric digital and space-tech ecosystem to enhance transparency and efficiency in the execution of government programs while providing crucial support for evidence-based policy making for farmers’ welfare. Key objectives of the Digital Agriculture Mission are highlighted as under: (a) To create a robust and holistic farmer-centric digital ecosystem in the country. (b) To improve transparency and efficiency in the implementation of government initiatives. (c) To support the government in making informed policy decisions. (d) To promote public and private innovation and partnerships, encouraging Agri techs in India’s digital economy. Key Components of Digital Agriculture Mission (a) Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) for Agriculture, i.e.Agristack, Krishi Decision Support System, Comprehensive Soil Fertility, and Profile Maps. (b) Digital General Crop Estimation Survey (DGCES) (c) Support to Mahalanobis National Crop Forecasting Centre (MNCFC) (d) Activities undertaken in erstwhile NeGP- A scheme (subsumed in the Mission) which includes Development & maintenance of IT Infrastructure/Application of DA&FW and Support to States, Academic / Research Institutions for IT initiatives. Program & Schemes 1. Agristack Agri Stack is envisaged as a digital public good like “Aadhar” for efficient and effective scheme/service delivery to the farmers. It will be built in a ‘federated architecture’ as a collaborative project between the various agencies of the Central and State Governments and the Union Territories. It consists of three foundational registries or databases in the agriculture sector, i.e.: o Farmers’ Registry: Farmer’s Registry is a dynamic, accurate, and verified database of farmers created and managed by the States/ Union Territories. It will provide comprehensive and useful data on farmers comprising authenticated demographic details, land holdings, family details, crops sown, soil health, livestock owned, fisheries assets, and other vocations. o Geo-referenced village maps: Geo-referenced Village Maps are required for conducting digital crop surveys. Geo-referenced village maps will enable trustful ground truth data collection and mapping of the data points at the land parcel level during the Digital Crop Survey exercise, geography-based crop advisory system, etc. Survey-level geo-referenced village cadastral maps will serve as a base for meeting the objectives of Agristack. o Crop Sown Registry through Digital Crop Survey: A Digital Crop Survey(DCS) System will be established to collect crop - sown details through a mobile interface, ensuring data is captured directly from the field.This database will provide accurate, real-time crop area information for every agricultural plot, aiming to replace traditional survey methods like Girdawari.The DCS system will also enhance remote sensing capabilities for seamless crop area mapping, reducing the need to conduct surveys in the future. 2. Krishi Decision Support System Krishi-DSS, launched on 16th August 2024, integrates and standardizes geospatial and non-geospatial data, including satellite, weather, soil, crop signatures, reservoir, groundwater data, and government scheme information. Krishi-DSS offers crop maps, soil maps, automated yield estimation models, drought/flood monitoring systems, etc., which support the government's evidence-based decision-making and facilitate innovative solutions by research institutions and the agritech industry. This Krishi-DSS platform is aligned with the government's National Geo-Spatial policy. The information available on Krishi-DSS would support crop map generation for identifying crop sown patterns and crop diversification, drought/flood monitoring, and technology/model-based yield assessment for settling crop insurance claims by farmers. 3. Nationwide Soil Resource Mapping project A Nationwide Soil Resource Mapping project has been initiated by the Soil and Land Use Survey of India (SLUSI), which is inventorying soils at a village level on a finer and more detailed 1:10,000 scale using high-resolution satellite and ground data. Under this project, a detailed soil profile study will be carried out to create standardized soil maps for rational land use and crop planning, thus promoting sustainable agriculture. 4. DGCES DGCES is designed to streamline and automate the entire GCES process, encompassing state-level planning, recording the results of CCEs conducted on the ﬁeld, and report generation. By digitizing and enhancing the eﬃciency of CCEs, DGCES seeks to provide States/ UTs with crop yield data that is not only accurate but also accessible in a timely manner. 5. IT Initiatives Support This includes following two components: (a) IT Initiative support to States and Other Organizations under the Mission (b) Support for IT-related activities of DA&FW, Government of India 6. Support to MNCFC The key activities of MNCFC to be covered under the Digital Agriculture Mission are enlisted as follows: (a) Crop acreage and production estimation of major crops under the scheme “Forecasting Agricultural output using Space, Agro-meteorology and Land based observations” (FASAL). It also undertakes Horticulture Crop assessments. (b) Support to Crop Weather Watch Group, drought monitoring and support to the States for their capacity building on geospatial technologies for agriculture. (c) MNCFC is also the technical partner for the effective implementation of PMFBY for area discrepancy analysis, smart sampling, and yield dispute resolution. IT Initiatives for States, Academic & Research Institutions Revised Guidelines (Consolidated) Revision of erstwhile National e-Governance Plan in Agriculture (NeGP-A) implementation approach. मित्रवत पुस्तिका और देखें >> सोशल मीडिया Tweets by Agriculture INDIA Tweets by AgriGoI नया क्या है और देखें खरीफ अभियान 2026 के लिए कृषि पर राष्ट्रीय सम्मेलन खरीफ विपणन सीजन (केएमएस) 2026-27 के लिए खरीफ फसलों के न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एमएसपी)। वार्षिक रिपोर्ट 2025-2026 2026-27 मौसम के लिए कच्चे पटसन हेतु न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य चेतावनी: आधिकारिक वेबसाइट अस्वीकरण एवं जन परामर्श विपणन एवं निरीक्षण निदेशालय में तीन पदों की भर्ती – स्टेनोग्राफर ग्रेड-I, ग्रुप बी गैर-गजेटेड उप कार्यालय कैडर, जिन्हें डिप्यूटेशन पर भरा जाएगा (संक्षिप्त अवधि के अनुबंध सहित) – वेतन मैट्रिक्स के स्तर-6 में (सातवें CPC के अनुसार) {पूर्व-संशोधित वेतन बैंड-2 (₹9,300-34,800) के साथ ग्रेड पे ₹4,200}। -(अंग्रेजी) कीटनाशक प्रबंधन विधेयक, 2025 के मसौदे पर टिप्पणियाँ आमंत्रित की जाती हैं। -(अंग्रेजी) 2026 मौसम के लिए कोपरा के न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एमएसपी) गैर-रासायनिक उर्वरक अनुभाग, INM डिवीजन में दो युवा पेशेवरों (Young Professionals) की एक वर्ष की अनुबंध (contract) अवधि के लिए नियुक्ति/एंगेजमेंट की जा रही है। -(अंग्रेजी) स्टेनोग्राफर ग्रेड-I ग्रुप 'बी' अराजपत्रित उप कार्यालय संवर्ग के तीन पदों की भर्ती, वेतन मैट्रिक्स में लेवल-6 (7वें सीपीसी के अनुसार) {पूर्व-संशोधित वेतन बैंड-2 (रु. 9300-34800/-) में प्रतिनियुक्ति (अल्पकालिक अनुबंध सहित) पर भरी जाएगी। 2026-27 विपणन मौसम हेतु तोरिया के लिए न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एमएसपी) एफसीओ में नए रासायनिक उर्वरकों पर विचार के लिए चेकलिस्ट बीज विधेयक 2025 पर प्रारंभिक सलाह (पूर्व विधायी परामर्श) रबी विपणन मौसम (आरएमएस) 2026-27 के लिए रबी फसलों हेतु न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में "मार्केट इंटेलिजेंस निरीक्षक (अर्थशास्त्र)" के मसौदा भर्ती नियमों पर सभी हितधारकों से सुझाव/टिप्पणियाँ आमंत्रित की जाती हैं। -(अंग्रेजी) कैबिनेट ने प्रधानमंत्री धन-धान्य कृषि योजना को मंज़ूरी दी "2025-26 में चयनित फसलों के लिए प्रौद्योगिकी का उपयोग करके ग्राम पंचायत (जीपी) स्तर पर फसल उपज अनुमान" के लिए रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति हेतु निमंत्रण -(अंग्रेजी) राष्ट्रीय किसान कल्याण कार्यक्रम कार्यान्वयन सोसाइटी (NFWPIS) में प्रतिनियुक्ति (ISTC) के आधार पर तेरह (13) पदों को भरना खरीफ विपणन मौसम (केएमएस) 2025-26 के लिए खरीफ फसलों हेतु न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य कैबिनेट ने मौजूदा 1.5% ब्याज अनुदान (आईएस) के साथ वित्त वर्ष 2025-26 के लिए संशोधित ब्याज अनुदान योजना (एमआईएसएस) को जारी रखने की मंजूरी दी. स्टेनोग्राफर ग्रेड सी और डी परीक्षा, 2025 के लिए रिक्तियां फसलों के वास्तविक समय अवलोकन और फोटो के संग्रह के लिए रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति हेतु आमंत्रण (CROPIC) कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में (i) सहायक पुस्तकालय एवं सूचना अधिकारी तथा (ii) पुस्तकालय एवं सूचना सहायक के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियां/सुझाव आमंत्रित किए जाते हैं। एनएमएनएफ पर प्रतिस्पर्धी अनुसंधान अनुदान अधिसूचना वार्षिक रिपोर्ट 2024-25 रिक्ति वर्ष 2018, 2019 और 2020 के विरुद्ध अनुकंपा के आधार पर नियुक्ति के मामलों पर विचार करने के लिए 06-09-2024 और 14-11-2024 को आयोजित विभागीय स्थायी समिति की बैठक का कार्यवृत्त। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में स्टाफ कार ड्राइवर (साधारण ग्रेड) के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियां/सुझाव आमंत्रित किए जाते हैं। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में डिस्पैच राइडर के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियां/सुझाव आमंत्रित किए जाते हैं। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में डिस्पैच राइडर के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियां/सुझाव आमंत्रित किए जाते हैं। 2025-26 मौसम के लिए कच्चे पटसन हेतु न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य प्राकृतिक खेती पर राष्ट्रीय मिशन के संशोधित दिशानिर्देश प्राकृतिक खेती पर राष्ट्रीय मिशन के लिए अंतिम अनुमोदित दिशानिर्देश 2025-26 विपणन मौसम हेतु तोरिया के लिए न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एमएसपी) (शुद्धिपत्र ) (ईओआई) राष्ट्रीय किसान कल्याण कार्यक्रम कार्यान्वयन सोसायटी (एनएफडब्ल्यूपीआईएस) सोसायटी पंजीकरण अधिनियम, 1860 के तहत पंजीकृत है, जो कृषि और किसान कल्याण मंत्रालय के प्रशासन नियंत्रण में है। एनएफडब्ल्यूपीआईएस चौथी और छठी मंजिल, प्लांट अथॉरिटी भवन (पीएबी), पूसा, नई दिल्ली-12 पर चालू है, जो सिविल और इलेक्ट्रिकल कार्य, ए.सी की स्थापना, कला कार्य, फर्श/मैट आदि के लिए योग्य फर्मों/ठेकेदारों से रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति (ईओआई) आमंत्रित करता है। (ईओआई) राष्ट्रीय किसान कल्याण कार्यक्रम कार्यान्वयन सोसायटी (एनएफडब्ल्यूपीआईएस) सोसायटी पंजीकरण अधिनियम, 1860 के तहत पंजीकृत है, जो कृषि और किसान कल्याण मंत्रालय के प्रशासन नियंत्रण में है। एनएफडब्ल्यूपीआईएस चौथी और छठी मंजिल, प्लांट अथॉरिटी भवन (पीएबी), पूसा, नई दिल्ली-12 पर चालू है, जो सिविल और इलेक्ट्रिकल कार्य, ए.सी की स्थापना, कला कार्य, फर्श/मैट आदि के लिए योग्य फर्मों/ठेकेदारों से रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति (ईओआई) आमंत्रित करता है। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में आर्थिक अधिकारी के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियाँ/सुझाव आमंत्रित हैं कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में तकनीकी क्लर्क (अर्थशास्त्र) के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियाँ/सुझाव आमंत्रित हैं। कृषि विपणन पर राष्ट्रीय नीति ढांचे का मसौदा - सार्वजनिक टिप्पणियाँ/सुझाव के संबंध में। क्लाउड-आधारित एपीएम टूल के लिए रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति (ईओआई) हेतु अनुरोध हेतु शुद्धिपत्र 1 2024-25 (केवल खरीफ) के लिए खाद्यान्न, तिलहन और अन्य वाणिज्यिक फसलों के उत्पादन का पहला अग्रिम अनुमान रुचि की अभिव्यक्ति (ईओआई) के लिए अनुरोध कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में तकनीकी सहायक (अर्थशास्त्र) के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियाँ/सुझाव आमंत्रित आर्टिफिशियल इंटेलिजेंस (एआई) और डिजिटल कृषि पर सार-संग्रह डीए एंड एफडब्ल्यू के तहत कृषि लागत और मूल्य आयोग (सीएसीपी) में सदस्य (आधिकारिक) के एक (01) पद को भरना -(अंग्रेजी और हिंदी) कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग में योजना अधिकारी के भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों से टिप्पणी/सुझाव आमंत्रित हैं। कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग के तहत कृषि लागत और मूल्य आयोग (सीएसीपी) में सदस्य (आधिकारिक) के एक (01) पद को भरना -(अंग्रेजी) सीएसीपी में सदस्य सचिव के 01 पद को भरने की तिथि में विस्तार -(अंग्रेजी) डिजिटल कृषि पहल पर सार-संग्रह -(अंग्रेजी) वार्षिक रिपोर्ट 2023-2024 -(अंग्रेजी) खरीफ विपणन मौसम (केएमएस) 2024-25 के लिए खरीफ फसलों हेतु न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य। किसान उत्पादक संगठनों पर राष्ट्रीय नीति का मसौदा -(अंग्रेजी) महिलाओं के यौन उत्पीड़न की रोकथाम के लिए आंतरिक शिकायत समिति (आईसीसी) की संशोधित संरचना। -(अंग्रेजी) क्रेडिट प्रभाग, कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग (उचित) के तकनीकी पदों के संबंध में भर्ती नियमों के मसौदे पर सभी हितधारकों की टिप्पणियाँ/सुझाव आमंत्रित हैं। -(अंग्रेजी) G20 परिणाम दस्तावेज़ -(अंग्रेजी) कृषि क्षेत्र में स्वैच्छिक कार्बन बाजार के लिए रूपरेखा -(अंग्रेजी) कृषि वानिकी नर्सरी के लिए प्रत्यायन प्रोटोकॉल -(अंग्रेजी) ईएनएएम और अन्य ई-ट्रेडिंग प्लेटफार्मों के माध्यम से भंडारण और ईएनडब्ल्यूआर व्यापार के लिए किसानों को बढ़ावा देने के लिए संकल्पना नोट - पीएम किसान भाई योजना- टिप्पणियाँ / इनपुट - सादर। -(अंग्रेजी) 2024-25 विपणन मौसम की रबी फसलों के न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एम्एसपी) राष्ट्रीय बीज निगम लिमिटेड के अध्यक्ष सह प्रबंध निदेशक के पद के लिए चयन - एक अनुसूची बी - मिनीरत्न। -(अंग्रेजी) विपणन सीज़न 2023-24 के लिए ख़रीफ़ फसलों के लिए न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एमएसपी)। 2023-24 मौसम के लिए कच्चे पटसन की मूल्य नीति 2023-24 मौसम के लिए कच्चे पटसन के न्यूनतम समर्थन मूल्य (एमएसपी) की घोषणा । MINIMUM SUPPORT PRICES (MSP) FOR RABI CROPS FOR RABI MARKETING SEASON (RMS) 2023-24 -(अंग्रेजी) कृषि समाचार Cabinet approves the Clean Plant Programme under Mission for कृषि सुधार अधिनियम 2020 पर प्रकाशित लेखों का ई-संग्रह कृषि अवसंरचना निधि के तहत वित्त पोषण सुविधा की केंद्रीय क्षेत्र योजना। कृषि अवसंरचना निधि के तहत पात्र परियोजना के रूप में सीएचसी के बारे में स्पष्टीकरण ड्रोन के लिए एस.ओ.पी परियोजना निगरानी इकाई (पीएमयू) की प्रशासनिक लागत के लिए राज्य सरकारों को सहायता अनुदान जारी करने और उपयोग करने के लिए मानक संचालन प्रक्रिया (एसओपी) के संबंध में एग्रीकल्चर इन्फ्रास्ट्रक्चर फंड की हिंदी में दिशा-निर्देश कृषि अवसंरचना कोष के तहत वित्त पोषण के लिए केंद्रीय क्षेत्र योजना में ब्याज सबवेंशन के संबंध में स्पष्टीकरण कृषि समझौता और देखें मॉडल कृषि करार केला क्षेत्रीय भाषाओं में केले पर मॉडल खेती समझौता किसान कल्याण किसानों के डेटाबेस पर अवधारणाओं के प्रमाण विकसित करने पर समझौता ज्ञापन क्षेत्रीय भाषाओं में केले पर मॉडल कृषि करार सोशल मीडिया pages.title: G20 - Agriculture Working Group
pages.text: G20 - Agriculture Working Group About G20 G20 or a Group of Twenty is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union (EU). It works to address major issues related to the global economy, such as international financial stability, climate change mitigation, and sustainable development. The G20 is composed of most of the world's largest economies, including both industrialised and developing nations; it accounts for around 80% of gross world product (GWP), 75% of international trade, two-thirds of the global population, and 60% of the world's land area. Hence, authors call the forum as “The G20 and the Future of International Economic Governance” book by Mike Callaghan & Tristram Sainsbury and “G20 Governance for a Globalized World” book by John J. Kirton. G20 works with two tracks and one group namely: Finance Track, Sherpa Track and Outreach/ Engagement Group Agriculture Working Group Agriculture Working Group (AWG) was established in the year 2011, sixth meeting of G20 during Cannes Summit, France. Formation of AWG was the outcome of this summit, most notably in the area of agriculture and food security with a special launch of Agricultural Market Information System (AMIS) and the endorsement of an Action Plan on Food Price Volatility and Agriculture as tangible steps to address the world agriculture and food challenges. AWG – India’s Presidency Aligning with India’s G20 Presidency theme of ‘One Earth, One Family, One Future’, the Agriculture Working Group (AWG) aspires to enhance cooperation among G20 nations. The summit provides the member countries with an opportunity to coordinate at the policy level to work towards achieving global economic stability and sustainable growth. Concerning agriculture, the current challenges that member nations face are food insecurity, gaps in the global value chain, crop productivity due to climate change, and employment. Through the Issue note, the aim is to address these challenges Officers in Charge Shri Manoj Ahuja, Session Chair, AWG-G20 Secretary, DoA&FW, MoA&FW Shri Rakesh Ranjan, Additional Secretary Dr. Pramod Kumar Meherda, Additional Secretary Dr. Smita Sirohi, Session Co-chair, AWG-G20 Joint Secretary Shri Ritesh Chauhan, Joint Secretary Shri Rabindra Prasad, AWG-G20, Director Shri Muktanand Agrawal, Director, Logistics Shri Pankaj Singh, Under Secretary Shri Shashank Sahu, Under Secretary Ms. Parul Jain, Assistant Director (IC) AWG - pages.title: G20 - Agriculture Working Group
pages.text: G20 - Agriculture Working Group About G20 G20 or a Group of Twenty is an intergovernmental forum comprising 19 countries and the European Union (EU). It works to address major issues related to the global economy, such as international financial stability, climate change mitigation, and sustainable development. The G20 is composed of most of the world's largest economies, including both industrialised and developing nations; it accounts for around 80% of gross world product (GWP), 75% of international trade, two-thirds of the global population, and 60% of the world's land area. Hence, authors call the forum as “The G20 and the Future of International Economic Governance” book by Mike Callaghan & Tristram Sainsbury and “G20 Governance for a Globalized World” book by John J. Kirton. G20 works with two tracks and one group namely: Finance Track, Sherpa Track and Outreach/ Engagement Group Agriculture Working Group Agriculture Working Group (AWG) was established in the year 2011, sixth meeting of G20 during Cannes Summit, France. Formation of AWG was the outcome of this summit, most notably in the area of agriculture and food security with a special launch of Agricultural Market Information System (AMIS) and the endorsement of an Action Plan on Food Price Volatility and Agriculture as tangible steps to address the world agriculture and food challenges. AWG – India’s Presidency Aligning with India’s G20 Presidency theme of ‘One Earth, One Family, One Future’, the Agriculture Working Group (AWG) aspires to enhance cooperation among G20 nations. The summit provides the member countries with an opportunity to coordinate at the policy level to work towards achieving global economic stability and sustainable growth. Concerning agriculture, the current challenges that member nations face are food insecurity, gaps in the global value chain, crop productivity due to climate change, and employment. Through the Issue note, the aim is to address these challenges Officers in Charge Shri Manoj Ahuja, Session Chair, AWG-G20 Secretary, DoA&FW, MoA&FW Shri Rakesh Ranjan, Additional Secretary Dr. Pramod Kumar Meherda, Additional Secretary Dr. Smita Sirohi, Session Co-chair, AWG-G20 Joint Secretary Shri Ritesh Chauhan, Joint Secretary Shri Rabindra Prasad, AWG-G20, Director Shri Muktanand Agrawal, Director, Logistics Shri Pankaj Singh, Under Secretary Shri Shashank Sahu, Under Secretary Ms. Parul Jain, Assistant Director (IC) AWG - pages.text: Home | Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare | MoA & FW | Government of India, India कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण विभाग DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE & FARMERS WELFARE भारत सरकार GOVERNMENT OF INDIA कृषि एवं किसान कल्याण मंत्रालय MINISTRY OF AGRICULTURE & FARMERS WELFARE Department At A Glance Agriculture plays a vital role in India's economy. 54.6% of the total workforce is engaged in agriculture and allied sector activities (Census2011). Agriculture and Allied sector accounts for18.4 % of India's GVA at current prices during 2022-23. Given the importance of the agriculture sector, Government of India has taken several steps for its development in a sustainable manner. The Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare (DA&FW) is one of the two constituent Departments of the Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare. This Department is headed by Agriculture & Farmers Welfare Minister and is assisted by two Ministers of State. The Secretary (A&FW)is the administrative head of the Department. The Secretary is assisted by five Additional Secretaries including one Financial Adviser, 10 Joint Secretaries, one Agriculture Commissioner, Horticulture Commissioner, Sr. Economic Advisor, Horticulture Statistics Advisor, Advisor cost and Deputy Director General. In addition, Chairman of Commission for Agriculture Costs and Prices (CACP) advises Department on pricing policies for selected agricultural crops. The DA&FW is organized into 28 divisions and has five attached offices and twenty-one subordinate offices which are spread across the country for coordination with state level agencies and implementation of Central Sector Schemes in their respective fields. Further, one Public Sector Undertaking, seven autonomous bodies, and two authorities are functioning under the administrative control of Department. Land Use Statistics As per land use statistics for 2021-22, the geographical area of our country is 328.7 million hectares, of which around 54.8% is agricultural land, 141.00 million hectares is the reported net sown area and 219.158million hectares is the gross cropped area with a cropping intensity of 155.4%. Net area sown comprises of 42.8% of the total geographical area. At all India level, total cropped area has increased by 66.1 percent in 2021-22 as compared to 1950-51 (131.89 million hectares in 1950-51 to 219.15 million hectares in 2021-22). The net irrigated area during 2021-22was 77.9millionhectare, out of which 24.7% was accounted by canal source ofirrigation, 2.8 % by tanks, 47.3 % by Tube wells, 13.2 % by other wells and remaining 12.0 % is irrigated by other source of irrigation. The gross irrigated area in 2021-22 was estimated at 120.3 million hectare, which was 54.9 % of the gross cropped area. Gross Value Added of Agriculture & Allied Sector As per the Provisional Estimates of National Income, 2022-23, released by National Statistical Office (NSO), Ministry of Statistics & Programme Implementation, the agriculture and allied sectors contributed approximately 18.4 % of India's Gross Value Added, (GVA) at current prices during 2022-23.The absolute Gross Value Added of agriculture and allied sectors and its share in total GVA of the country at current prices during the last6years is as follows: Gross Value Added of Agriculture & Allied Sector (Rs. documents.extracted_text:
F.No.Z-11018/8/2020-IT FTS No. 81917
Government of India
Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare
 Department of Agriculture, Cooperation and Farmers Welfare
Digital Agriculture Division
***
Krishi Bhawan, New Delhi
Dated the:    July, 2023
To,
 
The Principal Secretaries/Secretaries/ Commissioner/Director (Agriculture) of all
States/UTs
 
Subject:
 Revision of guidelines of Digital Agriculture scheme
erstwhile National e-Governance Plan in Agriculture (NeGP-A) to
include Digital Crop Survey
 
and need for building applications
on advanced technologies.
Madam/Sir,
            The undersigned is directed to refer to this Department’s letter of even no.
dated 02.06.2020 regarding revision of National e-Governance Plan in Agriculture
(NeGP-A) implementation approach and subsequent letter dated 15.11.2022
wherein, directives issued to the State(s) for implementation of the scheme.
2.      The revised guidelines for consideration of projects received from
States/UTs are as under:
i
. 
Funds under NeGPA will be released to States only for the project involving
use of modern Information Technologies such as Artiﬁcial Intelligence &
Machine Learning, Block Chain Technology, Internet of Things, Robotics etc.,
and for customization / shifting of web & mobile applications already
developed by the States, to the platform to be developed using digital
technologies mentioned above.
ii
. 
Digital Public Infrastructures/Digital Public Goods: Applications/scheme
relates to the digital public infrastructure for agriculture that will be built as an
open source, open standard and interoperable public good, which will enable
inclusive, farmer-centric solutions through relevant information services for
crop planning and health, improved access to farm inputs, credit and
insurance, help for crop estimation, market intelligence, and support for the
growth of the Agri Techs industry and start-ups will be given priority. One of
the Digital Public Infrastructures will be built with three core registries
(Farmer, Farmland Plot, and the Crop Sown data captured through a digital
crop survey) and will have a Uniﬁed Farmers' Service Interface (UFSI) to
develop farmer-centric digital service delivery solutions across the agriculture
value chain.
iii
. 
Evaluation of the projects submitted by the States will be done by the
Assessment Committee headed by JS (Digital) with representative of the
concerned Division, NIC & DS/Director (Digital). Further, Director (MNCFC)
has also been invited as special invitee during the Assessment Committee
Z-11018/8/2020-IT
I/28366/2023
I/28366/2023
File No. Z-11018/8/2020-IT (Computer No. 81917)
Generated from eOffice by RAHUL SWAMI, ASO(Digital)RS, ASO, DAC on 17/07/2023 01:08 PM
1
meeting.
iv
. 
The States/ UTs should submit the proposal under NeGP-A preferably with in
First quarter but not later than Second quarter of the Financial Year.
v
. 
The proposal should contain DPR, Financial implication, clear timelines and
technology involved.
vi
. 
Proposal would be examined on the use of latest technology, innovation,
scalability study of success and viability of the proposed solution before
proposing for pilot. Accordingly, necessary information may be submitted by
the States in the proposal.
vii
. 
The States/ UTs are advised to come up with proposals whose life-cycle from
conception to implementation is preferably for one year with the clear
timelines of implementation.   
viii
. 
As per latest guidelines of the Department of Expenditure (DoE), funds to the
States would be released in 4 equal tranches. Accordingly, States are
requested to update the details of expenditure on PFMS before requesting
the next installments.   
ix
. 
Next installment to the States/ UTs will be released after the receipt of the
satisfactory status report from the States/ UTs.     
x
. 
Pilot project submitted by State(s) wherein, State(s) are requesting for their
requirement of basic infrastructure viz. Hardware & manpower for support
and execution of the project, the same shall be borne by the concerned state.
xi
. 
Centre would have the co-ownership on the intellectual property created
during the pilot and accordingly, States/ UTs have to share the details with
Centre alongwith the outcomes of the pilot.
xii
. 
As a part of feedback project monitoring, the States/UTs are requested to
share the Cost Beneﬁt analysis with the Central Government. This will help
identify best practices and projects that have had tangibly beneﬁcial results
for farmers and eventually extrapolate such projects at pan-lndia level.
xiii
. 
The project sanctioned should be completed within the stipulated time.
However, this may be extended for a further period of 1 year at a time with
the prior approval of the Department, upto maximum of two years. Further,
even if the project is not completed in the extended year, State will require
surrendering the unspent amount. Any new proposal would be considered,
only after confirmation from PAO on receiving the refund amount.
Digital Crop Survey:-
3.      Also, it is requested to refer to this Department’s letter of even no. dated
30.06.2023 regarding release of funds for Digital Crop Survey for FY 2023-24
wherein, State(s) were informed that the ﬁnancial assistance for the Digital Crop
Survey pilots during the FY 2023-24 will be provided under the existing scheme
viz. NeGP-A. Also, separate guidelines were issued to the States for the same vide
letter dated 30.05.2023 (copy enclosed).
4.      The Department has initiated the Pilot on Digital Crop Survey (DCS) from
Kharif - 2023, as a component of Digital Agriculture Mission (DAM). Given that the
Digital Crop Survey is part of DAM, it is proposed to make provisions for DCS
under NeGP-A guidelines:
i
. 
Since, DCS has to be carried out by State(s) and for this purpose the state
will require large no. of human resources, that will be required to carry out
physically ﬁeld/plot-based crop survey, and for PMU and helpdesk, hence
funds may also be released to States for manpower deployment to conduct
DCS/setting up of Help Desk/ State PMU etc.
Z-11018/8/2020-IT
I/28366/2023
I/28366/2023
File No. Z-11018/8/2020-IT (Computer No. 81917)
Generated from eOffice by RAHUL SWAMI, ASO(Digital)RS, ASO, DAC on 17/07/2023 01:08 PM
2
ii
. 
States may procure infrastructure related to DCS only. 
iii
. 
Funds already available with the State, which were released during last FYs
for different projects may be utilized for carrying out DCS.
iv
. 
States may be requested to submit the proposal under NeGP-A for DCS
during current FY i.e. 2023-24. All the proposals received from the States
w.r.t Digital Crop Survey may be approved at the level of Secretary (A&FW).  
 
Building of Digital Public Infrastructure:-
5.    The Department has taken note of the need of agriculture academic and
research institutes, such as ICAR, regarding their data requirements for the
purpose of Agri Stack/Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) in Agriculture.
6.    In this regard, 100% funds release may be considered to such academic and
research institutes for building scalable digital solutions using advanced and
emerging technologies which synchronizes with Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI)
in Agriculture requirement(s).
7.    Funds will be released to them only after examination of the need for the pilot
project and recommendation of a High-level Evaluation Committee constituted
under the chairmanship of AS (Digital) and DDG(NIC), Director (MNCFC),
DDG/ADG (ICT) DARE/ICAR, Agriculture Commissioner and subject matter expert
as nominated by the Chair based on the requirement(s).
 
8.    This issues with the approval of Hon'ble Agriculture Minister.
Encl: As above.
Yours faithfully,
 
 
Chandan Kumar
Under Secretary to the Govt. of India
email: us-it@gov.in
Ph: 011-23382926
 
Copy to
:
 
i
. 
PS to Hon'ble Agriculture and Farmers Welfare Minister
ii
. 
PS to Hon'ble MoS(PR) / MoS (KC).
iii
. 
PPS to Secretary, DA&FW.
iv
. 
PPS to Secretary, (DARE).
v
. 
Sr. PPS/PPS of all Spl. Secretaries/Addl. Secretaries/Joint Secretaries and
equivalent level officers, DA&FW

1 Introduction 
Agriculture is rightfully considered to be one of the most resource and labor intensive industries. 
The challenges farmers face today include, but aren’t limited to the following: Regular equipment 
maintenance, Correct water estimates , Eliminating water waste and overhead expenses , 
Estimating correct planting times, Measuring soil temperature and moisture levels, Pest control 
Regular satellite monitoring of crops, allows farmers to easily follow the growth status of plants 
and carry out crop yield estimation using remote sensing. The evolution of plant health status 
provides key information to decide on possible interventions to the needs of crops. 
Local governments are tasked with  significant responsibilities with budgetary constraints. This 
results in ownership and other administrative issues where, under such circumstances, farmers 
find it difficult to concentrate on improving their yield. In order to meet these critical challenges 
and remain competitive with other countries, local governments should turn towards innovative 
analytics that are able to economically, efficiently  and accurately meet their analytical needs -
primarily satellite and location intelligence – and systematically monitor resources and yields as 
a way to further positive economic, social and environmental outcomes. 
2 Scope of Work 
The proposed project is designed with four broad objectives.  
• The first component is generation of Geo-referenced village map with digital basemap 
Farm Plot/ Crop land extracted using high resolution satellite images. 
• The second component is Digital Farmer’s Database creation. 
• A dashboard will be developed  to present the results and analytics  along with mobile 
application. 
3 Area of Interest 
The proposed project shall be implement at 6 (Six) Districts namely Tawang, Namsai, East Siang, 
Lower Subansiri, Lower Dibang Valley and Pakke kessang. The area have been selected on the 
basis of various factors like topography, climate, soil type, etc. so that better approach can be 
made for project implementation for rest of the part. 
4 Approach & Methodology 
4.1 Rationale 
Remote sensing data is increasingly used in agriculture and water resources management. In the 
last two decades, improvements in spatial, spectral , and temporal resolution of the earth 
observation systems (EOS) data encouraged the adoption of this technology for managing 
agriculture to meet the demand of the increasing population. The most important application in 
this regard is the use of medium and high -resolution data to map agriculture crops, monitor the 
crop condition, and estimate crop productivity. The improvements in remote sensing technology

 Commercial in Confidence Page 5 of 21 
 
 
and the progressive development in computer software and hardware resulted in the 
development, calibration, and adoption of remote sensing models for managing agriculture.  
India’s agricultural production is mainly comprised of basic grains, such as rice, maize, and beans; 
fruits, including bananas, pineapples, and oranges; and vegetables such as onions and potatoes. 
The ag riculture sector experiences high fragmentation of land among farmers, preventing 
efficiency increases, which is further exacerbated by the slow adoption of technology. These 
challenges translate into the stagnation of agro-export, due to the decrease in local production, 
and high increases in agro import. This leads to variability in crop type, rotation, and their sowing 
dates, etc. in turn posing challenges in their delineation.  
In recent years, the integration of  machine learning algorithms to remote sensing  data 
with Geographic Information Systems  (GIS) has been used in automatic or semiautomatic crop 
extraction and mapping.  To determine the spatially accurate crop acreages, two methods have 
been explored: AI & ML-based image classification and spectral indexing.

9.5.2 Description of the Monitoring Indices 
Vegetation indices are an important parameter to develop crop analytics. 
Monitor the state of the crops using multiple vegetation indices and moisture index.  
1. Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI): NDVI compares the reflectance values of 
the red and near-infrared regions of the electromagnetic spectrum. 
o The NDVI value, which ranges from -1.0 to 1.0 for each pixel in an image, helps to  
identify areas of varying levels of plant biomass/vigor. Higher values indicate high 
biomass/high vigor.   
o NDVI values give accurate analytics in the middle of the season at the stage of 
active crop growth. 
1. Soil-Adjusted Vegetation Index (SAVI): SAVI accounts for the differential red and near-
infrared extinction through the vegetation  canopy. The index is a transformation 
technique that minimizes soil brightness influences from spectral vegetation indices 
involving red and near-infrared (NIR) wavelengths. 
o Empirically derived NDVI products have been shown to be unstable, varying with 
soil color, soil moisture, and saturation effects from high-density vegetation. 
1. Green Chlorophyll Vegetation Index (GCI): GCI is used to estimate the content of leaf 
chlorophyll in various species of plants. The health of vegetation is directly correlated 
with the chlorophyll content of the leaves.

Productivity in Agriculture By Machine Learning Technique 
 
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 
 Commercial in Confidence Page 13 of 21 
 
 
o GCI reflects the physiological state of vegetation; a lower value of GCI represents 
stressed conditions of plants and a higher value shows the healthy condition of 
the crop, hence GCI is very useful to measure vegetation health. 
1. Red-Edge Chlorophyll Vegetation Index (RECl): The ReCI vegetation index is 
responsive to chlorophyll content in leaves that is nourished by nitrogen. ReCI shows the 
photosynthetic activity of the canopy cover. 
2. Enhanced Vegetation Index (EVI): The EVI is an 'optimized' vegetation index designed 
to enhance the vegetation signal with improved sensitivity in high biomass regions and 
improved vegetation monitoring through a de-coupling of the canopy background signal 
and a reduction in atmosphere influences. 
3. Structure Intensive Pigment Vegetation Index (SIPI): SIPI index is a reflectance 
measurement designed to maximize the sensitivity of the index to the ratio of bulk 
carotenoids (for example, alpha -carotene and beta -carotene) to chlorophyll while 
decreasing the sensitivity to variation in canopy structure (for example, leaf area index).  
o Increases in SIPI are thought to indicate increased canopy stress (carotenoid 
pigment). Applications include vegetation health monitoring, plant physiological 
stress detection, and crop production and yield analysis 
1.  Normalized difference red edge index  (NDRE): NDRE is an index that can only be 
formulated when the Red edge band is available in a sensor. It is sensitive to chlorophyll 
content in leaves (how green a leaf appears), variability in leaf area, and soil background 
effects. High values of NDRE represent higher levels of leaf chlorophyll content than lower 
values.  
o Soil typically has the lowest values, unhealthy plants have intermediate values, 
and healthy plants have the highest values. Consider using NDRE if you are 
interested in mapping variability in fertilizer requirements or foliar Nitrogen, not 
necessarily Nitrogen availability in the soil. 
1. Normalized Difference Moisture Index (NDMI): The Normalized Difference Moisture 
Index (NDMI) is sensitive to the moisture levels in vegetation. It is used to monitor 
droughts as well as monitor fuel levels in fire-prone areas. It uses NIR and SWIR bands to 
create a ratio designed to mitigate illumination and atmospheric effects.
