Limitations & Challenges  
1. Subjective assessment  – Visual assessment of the damage is subject to inaccuracies as it 
depends on human judgement.  
2. Manpower availability - Non availability of qualified manpower for assessment 
3. Multiple interfaces – Farmer must deal with multiple interfaces for getting his/her due 
4. Disputes – The insurance agency may dispute the assessment, and thi s further delays the 
compensation of loss to the farmer 
 
 
Figure 11 Root Cause Analysis for subjective localised loss assessment 
5.2.2. Recommendation for process improvement 
With different technological advancements and satellite imagery, the traditional method of crop loss 
assessment can be made efficient and fool-proof.  
The PMFBY recommends the use of technology in agriculture. For example, using technology solutions 
for assessing the damage to crops due to localized calamity in case of  a dispute between the 
stakeholders concerne d. Also, mobile phone applications can be integrated with the proposed 
solution to reduce delays in claim settlements by uploading crop damage data online. 
As a component of the Pilot Study/Proof of Concept, loca lized calamity-based crop loss assessment 
from drone-based and satellite-based imagery can be used to arrive at the accurate scale of loss. This 
can assist in arriving at a cost/benefit analysis by determining the efficacy and efficiency of time, effort 
and suitability for large scale roll out of the same.  
Recommended process 
Usage of satellite imagery to build the ML model for kharaba driven change detection & drone imagery 
for on-the-spot assessment & reporting of damage.

Advantages of the recommended process 
1. Technology-based unbiased loss assessment for all the stakeholders involved thus re ducing 
human error or any moral hazard  
2. Reduced incidences of disputes by the IC thus leading to timely release of compensation to 
the farmer - shortening the present cycle of minimum 32 days by 50% at least 
3. Long term objective of proactive prediction of k haraba events and prompt identification of 
kharaba sites for drone-based survey without the need for any intimation by farmer  
4. Reduced risk of non-availability of qualified loss assessors for physical loss assessment surveys  
5. A solution well integrated wit h the present platforms and portals used  in localised loss 
assessment 
 
5.2.3. Solution Requirements – Kharaba  
 
The objective of this project is to develop a Computer Vision ML algorithm which upon training can 
provide kharaba assessment (identification and verification) primarily based on crop type data, image 
processing data (images captured in multi/hyper spectral ranges through drones and satellites) and 
weather data. The model will also incorporate classifying factors (such as field data, soil data etc. 
mapped to GIS services) that enable better predictive outcomes. 
This kharaba solution will aim to provide: 
1. Crop profiles: Crop profiles for target crops (rice and wheat) will be created. These profiles 
will track crops through their agricultural cycle (from sow ing till harvesting) via mu lti/hyper 
spectral imaging and develop day -to-day models of what a healthy crop should look like (in 
their spectral signature) at various stages in the crop’s life.  
2. Identification of kharaba event: Crop profiles will also be trained using kharaba events in the 
state of Haryana. Multi/Hyper spectral images of fields with such kharaba incidents will be 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
282
Page | 31  
 
used to train the model such that upon completion of model training: the model can identify 
areas of kharaba based on deviations fr om the crop’s expected (pre vious day’s) spectral 
signature. i.e., the trained model will look at the crop’s spectral progression from day-to-day 
(all days of data capture at determined intervals) and identify any spectral deviations from 
expected spectral signature that indicate a k haraba incident (such as hailstorm, landslide, 
inundation, cloudburst, fire due to lightning etc.)  
3. Statewide coverage via satellite : Satellite images at suitable resolution (resolution to be 
determined during pilot based on sensitivity and cost factors) will be captured and processed 
frequently (daily, or multiple times in a week) such that the model develops a crop spectral 
signature based on the satellite’s data. Once the model is built successfully (during pilot), any 
deviations from expected spectral s ignature will indicate possible kharaba events (that can 
further be verified and validated through focused drone-based coverage). Satellite model will 
be trained to cover all areas of the state through integration with GIS mapped factors such as 
topography, weather profile etc. 
4. Focused coverage via drones : Once farmer reports a kharaba event, drones will be used to 
capture detailed images of the identified sites. Then the drone-based model will use its crop 
spectral signature model to identify actual extent and area of kharaba. The identified kharaba 
area (which will be mapped to fields and farmers through integration with GIS and farmer 
information portals such as MFMB) will enable f urther action as required ( e.g., government 
record keeping and compensation actions). 
5. Services: Once pilot is successful and capable of reliable kharaba identification (and 
verification) through satellite and drone-based models, services layer (detailed in section 
5.2.7) will be developed for state-wide go-live and adoption.

5.3. Pest, Weed & Disease management 
Pest, weed, and disease surveillance requires regular assessment of farms to identify early signs of 
infestation and damage. The present methods are based on routine surveys by the district officials 
and intimation of crop damage locations by the farmers. The PMFBY covers the risk of crop loss only 
due to widespread pest and disease incidence under the regular basic cover. In case of early onset or 
localised damage due to pests and diseases the farmer is left with limited options to recover his loss. 
This necessitates regular monitoring of crop health and early identification of pests in disease 
infestation to proverbially nip the problem in the bud. 
Major crop diseases and pest infestations in Haryana are as below 
Crops Insect/Pest Diseases

hopper, stem borer, gandhi

mildew 
Cotton  Thirps, jassid, leaf hopper,

Source: Working Group Report on Productivity Enhancement of Crops in Haryana, 2013 
 
As part of the As-Is Study, NISG has had interactions with officials of the Directorate of Agriculture at 
Krishi Bhavan, Panchkula, District officials of Department of Agriculture, Yamuna Nagar & District 
officials of Department of Agriculture, Karnal. The following observations were made of the current 
state of processes related to pest, weed & disease management in the state of Haryana: 
5.3.1. As-Is Assessment 
1. Monitoring – Self monitoring by farmer & field surveys conducted by Plant Protection officials 
during favourable weather 
2. Detection - Farmer detects infestation and approaches district officials for further sur vey or 
infestations are manually located during regular surveys by field officials 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
291
Page | 40  
 
3. Management – Mostly manual spray pump is used for pesticide spraying. Battery operated 
and tractor mounted pumps are also used occasionally 
4. Education and awareness - Timely advisories are issued in local newspapers to the farmers 
regarding pest attacks based on the field surveys. Farmer Training camps are conducted to 
make the farmers aware of how to protect their crops against pest and diseases 
5. Technology - No ICT channel is used as of now 
 
 
Limitations & Challenges 
1. Manual methods of monitoring and survey can lead to slippage of early detection of crop 
infestations leading to extensive crop damage 
2. Limited manpower availability at the district headquarters limits extens ive surveys  and 
hampers early detection and damage control 
3. Manual application of pesticides by the farmers is bound to create health problems for the 
farmers and lead to additional cost burden of medical aid 
4. Blind and extensive application of pesticides, herbicides, fungicides not only hamper the 
nearby crops but has damaging effect on the soil and sub soil fauna health 
 
 
Figure 16 Root Cause Analysis for issues in pest, weed and disease surveillance 
5.3.2. Recommendation for process improvement 
Most effective emerging technologies solutions are based on capture of data in the form of 
images/photos and processing the data through image pro cessing-based ML algorithms. An ML 
algorithm running in the backend can ingest crop information and image data from  field cameras to 
develop its knowledge of the crops and pests/weed. Such algorithms have developed weed detection 
accuracies of 70-96% when using algorithms such as SVM, RF, KNN in pilot tests.  
a. The ML algorithm (post training) will start identifying inci dents of pest infestation 
(such as beetles/locusts) through cameras and will provide advance warning of 
upcoming infestation events. 
b. ML algorithm (post training) will also start predicting weed and disease events based 
on its database of such events in the past. 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
292
Page | 41  
 
Recommended process 
Drone surveillance can be used to do field surveys and regular monitoring. The images collected by 
the done loaded camera can be fed to a trained ML algorithm to detect any early disease onset and 
create timely alerts. This will allow the scaling up of any pilots easily to increase the command area. 
Further the same drones can be used for targeted pesticides of the af fected area. (Also covered in 
Precision farming)  
 
Advantages of the proposed process 
1. No slippage of early disease detection 
2. No manual labor involved in surveys hence they can be carried out even during very warm or 
unfavorable weather 
3. No manual spraying required hence positively impacting farmer health 
4. Precise application of pesticides to the affected area and not overuse 
 
5.3.3. Solution Requirements – Pest, Weed and Disease Surveillance  
The objective of this project is to develop a Computer Vision ML algorithm which upon training can 
identify an infestation/infection event (caused by pest, weed or disease) through changes in the crop’s 
spectral signature.  
1. Spectral signature for the healthy crops will be developed using multi/hyper spectral imaging. 
Infestation/infection free fields will be used for development of this signature. 
2. Spectral signatures for infestation/infectio n affected crops will be developed using 
multi/hyper spectral imaging.  
a. Fields with infestation/infection will be identified during the pilot and monitored for 
developing this signature. 
b. Tie-up with the state agriculture university may also be required to develop signatures 
for infestation/infection not prevalent in the p ilot district blocks, and for controlled 
development of such spectral signatures. 
3. Only infestation/infection events that create a spectral signature statistically distinguishable 
from healthy crop will be worked upon further in the pilot.  
a. Statistically significant spectral differences can be expected in diseases that develop 
visible changes (or non-visible changes that can be observed in other electromagnetic 
spectrum ranges). Diseases such as brown rust of wheat, rice blast and others can be 
considered for development of such signatures. 
4. Spectral signatures will be developed using both drone sourced and satellite sourced data. 
Data captured from drones will be used to further train satellite model such that satellite data-
based ML model can learn to predic t/identify suspected infestation/infection events. Once 
infestation/infection is identified by satellite-based model (or through drone-based coverage, 
or reported by ground staff/farmers), drones will be used to assess the extent and location of 
damage (using ML model with GIS integration). 
5. Services – Once pilot is successful, and capable of infestation/infection detection, a services 
layer will be built on top of the ML model (generated in pilot) such that services can be 
provided to farmers (prediction an d/or verification of infestation/infection), government 
agencies (GIS mapped infestation/infection data to predict expected damage in the season) 
and to other stakeholders involved in agriculture.

5.3.4. Solution Approach – Pest, Weed and Disease Surveillance  
With the objective of creating a solution that is viable and can be scaled across the state, the project 
will proceed in three phases: 
 
Figure 17 Phases of solution development and deployment - Pest, weed & disease surveillance  
Phase 1 – Definition of detailed project requirements and execution plan.  
NISG consulting team will work on the projects approved by the government and define detailed 
requirements such that the desired project outcome is measurable and scalable. This phase will lead 
to creation of project reports (with acceptance criteria for pilot success/failure, and execution and 
scale-up plans) and RFPs for on-boarding of suitable SI/vendors. 
Phase 2 – Pilot phase 
Key outcome of this phase will be de velopment of an AI/ML model that can optimize the target 
agricultural operations. This model would be built through the following phases: 
1. Data Collection - Utilization of inputs such as: 
• Ground truthing  
• GIS map integration with field boundaries (through revenue map, or via Ground Truthing) 
• Weather data (standard weather services) 
• Rainfall data (moisture sensors, weather stations, or standard weather services) 
• Agriculture zone mapping 
• Terrain features/topography 
• Remote sensing data from satellites 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
294
Page | 43  
 
• Multi/Hyper spectral image and data capture using drones  
• Data from soil testing  
• Crop type (and variety) data 
• On ground sensors and weather stations  
• Crop information portals such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’ portal of Govt. of Haryana 
  
2. Model Creation - Creation & optimization using controls such as: 
· Multi/Hyper spectral image input – visible, near and far infrared, microwave data to 
support multilevel analysis of vegetation indices (such as moisture stress index, NVDI, 
LWCI etc.). Image data is captured via satellite remote sensing and drone -based 
sensors. 
· Image capture parameter management – Ensuring that drone image data is factored 
for parameters such as angle of capture, angle of sun on horizon, season, height of 
capture etc. 
· Establishment of appropriate data cleaning and ETL processes to ensure usable data 
is fed to the model. 
  
3. Prediction & accuracy analysis - Model to be tested against set acceptance levels (model 
accuracy levels to be finalized in phase 1) such that the model can: 
· Achieve predictions and classification/clustering accuracy that helps optimize 
agricultural operations 
· Can help increase efficiency (reduce turnaround time and effort) for government 
enabled farmer support in areas such as loss assessment, yield estimation etc. 
  
Phase 3 – Scale-Up across state   
Pilots that pass the acceptance criteria will be analyzed by the appointed NISG consulting team and 
Government agencies (Haryana and central agencies as applicable) to determine viability of scal ing 
the pilots across state. These scaled go-live projects may be based on a combination of different pilots 
(such that the scaling project can provide maximum benefit in terms of better and optimized 
operations) and may consist of multiple stages.  
These scaled go -live projects will then provide actionable data and recommendations to all 
stakeholders (including farmers, businesses working in agriculture space, Govt. of India, and Govt. of 
Haryana). Examples of such interactions and integrations are:  
• GoI PMFBY 
• GoI app – Crop cutting 
• Portal for agriculture businesses (such as seed sellers, private buyers, shelling mills etc.) 
• Interface for farmers 
• Interface for farming service providers (such as harvester owners, stubble management 
machine providers, transporters etc.) 
• Planning and futures businesses (commodities markets, Govt. planning departments etc.)

5.4. Use of blockchain as emerging technology in farm input tracing 
Farmers suffer from a lack of transparency around market demand/price which needs to be 
transformed, there is a need for trusted information/real -time market data using Blockchain 
framework which can help stakeholders to avoid multiple intermediaries. Blockchain technology has 
the potential to make the agriculture-supply chain more secure, transparent, and efficient. It promises 
end-to-end supply chain visibility and allows one to trace the origin of produce and trac k a 
product/produce during its journey in a supply chain. Blockchain solutions, if implemented, may lead 
to the elimination of intermediari es or middlemen, thereby leading to improved pricing, decreased 
transaction fees, thus eliminate issues of hoarding.  
As part of the As-Is Study, NISG has had interactions with officials of the Directorate of Agriculture at 
Krishi Bhavan, Panchkula, Distri ct officials of Department of Agriculture, Yamuna Nagar & District 
officials of Department of Agriculture, Karnal. Th e following observations were made of the current 
state of processes related to farm input management in the state of Haryana: 
5.4.1. As-Is Assessment 
- State Govt. Is the licensing authority for manufacturing/production/storage 
- Licensing authority for Wholesale (C &F), retail, distribution is the Deputy Director (District 
level) 
1. Seeds 
a. Licensing - Seed processing plant license is valid for 5 years. Manufacturing license 
given by Directorate and verification done by the district. Selling license is granted by 
district authority 
b. Production – Seeds are produced in seed production plants  
c. Testing - Seeds are tested for germination, moisture content, spuriousness (Refer 
Annex III for sample lab reports) 
d. Certifications: 
i. Certified Seeds (Authenticated by Seed Certification agencies) 
ii. TL (Truthful Level) -Truthful level Seeds (Self-Certified by manufacturers) 
e. Sampling - Random sampling of the seeds is carried out by QCI, subdivisional 
agricultural officers and field staff at retailer, distributer, and wholesaler levels. Which 
is then sent for testing at one of the state seed testing laboratories. 
f. Testing & Certification agency – Online process exists for seed testing at labs in 
Karnal, Sirsa, Kurukshetra, Rohtak & Panchkula. Certification is carried out by the state 
seed certification agency through its HQ & Regional offices  
g. Sample failure - If sample fails, then sale of entire lot is stopped, and a case is lodges 
against the company and the dealer 
h. Supply chain  – Manufacturing plant --> Wholesaler --> Distributer --> Retailer --> 
Farmer 
 
 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
302
Page | 51  
 
2. Pesticides 
a. Licensing – Lifelong validity of the license for the pesticide manufacturing plant. 
Manufacturing license given by Directorate and verification done by the district. 
Selling license is granted by district authority 
b. Production - Registration by C entral Insecticide Board (Faridabad) required for 
manufacturing.  
c. Sampling/Testing/Certification and failure management steps are like above 
d. Supply chain  – Manufacturing plant --> Wholesaler --> Distributer --> Retailer --> 
Farmer 
 
3. Fertiliser  
a. Licensing – Fertiliser manufacturing license is valid for 5 years. Manufacturing license 
given by Directorate and verification done by the district. Selling license is granted by 
district authority. 
b. Technology Portal - Mobile Fertiliser Monitoring System (M FMS) exists for end-to-
end monitoring of the fertiliser from production to sale  
c. Production - Manufacturer must enter the production value at the Portal 
d. Allocation - GoI allocates state wise allocation, State Gov allocates district wise, and 
District allocates Retailer wise. Rack allocation is done for fertilisers at the retail level 
e. Supply chain  – Manufacturing plant --> Wholesaler --> Distributer --> Retailer --> 
Farmer 
f. Last mile delivery  - Farmer pr ocures the fertiliser from the retailer through POS 
machines after thumbing (mandatory) and giving Aadhar Number. 
g. Subsidy - Bulk fertilisers are subsidised. If a farmer purchases more than 50 bags per 
month per single tran saction, then it gets highlighted in the MIS available at the 
district.  
h. DBT (Direct Beneﬁt Transfer) - Farmers can avail subsidy for the purchase of fertilisers 
via DBT through online purchase via Haryana  Agriculture website but as of now it is 
unverifiable. 
The present process of input monitoring is based on manual process except the online platforms used 
for testing in insecticides and MFMS in fertilisers. Lack of integration of all these platforms necessitates 
an over – arching system which can trace the produce from production to application by farmers and 
help in ascertaining the authenticity and efficiency of use of these inputs 
5.4.2. Recommended Process Improvement 
The following figure provides a schematic of various interacting parties and their activity data that is 
collected by using Point of Sale (POS)/Internet of Things (IoT) devices and t hen recorded on a 
Blockchain for offering transparency and traceability of the inputs/produce from farm to the 
processor.

Figure 20 Process flow of Agri-output from farm to processors – Transparency through traceability 
Pilot 1 - Pesticide, Seed, and Fertilizer Tracking: Tracking of seeds, pesticides, and fertilizers from the 
factory to the farmer, ensuring that counterfeit products are not introduced in the intermediary 
stages. As in the case of Haryana state, 60% of pesticides are  sourced from outside the state (from 
nearly 170 pesticide units) and the remaining percentage of pesticides is locally manufactured in 
approximately 150 manufacturing units. Affixing QR codes on the pesticide batches when they are 
manufactured can aid in capturing key details such as date of m anufacture, batch number, place of 
manufacture, quantity, factory accreditation and information about the chemical composition of the 
fertilizers. Once the QR code is scanned at the factory and the consignment leaves the site, the data 
is fed into the blockchain. This process is repeated at all subsequent stages till selling to the farmer in 
the supply chain to guarantee the chain of custody and thus precluding the entry of expired, non -
standard pesticides into the market.  
 
Figure 21 Pilot 1 - High level Pesticide, Seed and Fertilizer Tracking diagram 
Pilot 2 - Food Grain/Produce Tracking : The above -mentioned solution deploys a blockchain 
application designed to operate with a simple smartphone interface, either through the applicati on 
itself or by linking it with existing interfaces and systems for data capture. The following diagram 
describes one of the potential use cases of a blockchain-based supply chain:

Advantages 
1. Disintermediation of the multiple agents involved between harvest and selling the crop in the 
mandis. This will benefit the farmer helping them get better value for their produce 
2. Immutability and source tracking for the use of farm inputs. This will clearly establish the 
quality of the inputs used, hence help the buyer get access to better produce and help the 
farmer get value for it produce. 
5.4.3. Solution Requirements – Blockchain in Agriculture 
  
The purpose of this pilot is to use the immutability and disintermediation aspects of blockchain 
solutions for the benefit of farmers. This can be achieved by implementing the following solution in a 
phased manner (phases 1 and 2 are part of the current proposed pilot. If the current pilot is adjudged 
successful, then steps 3 through 5 will be tested through a subsequent pilot before state-wide rollout): 
1. Input traceability: Agricultural inputs will be tracked such that the nature and quality of the 
harvested crop can be controlled. This will include: 
a. Seed type – Source/type of seed will be recorded on blockchain through tie-ups with 
seed sellers. 
b. Fertilizer – Fertilizers that are used by the farmer in their crop will be recorded on 
blockchain and tied with the time of purchase. 
c. Pesticide – Pesticide sales in the pilot area will be tracked such that pesticides used in 
a farmer’s crop can accurately be identified. 
2. Harvest: Crop details and metrics will be recorded on blockchain (with immutable 
characteristics) 
a. Farmers will enter their harvesting date and their planned date of mandi arrival on a 
farmer app (app links to agricultural procurement blockchain).  
i. In subsequent pilot (pilot #2) this will allow for load planning at the Mandi, 
and features such as: if the Mandi does not have space on the planned day of 
farmer arrival, the app will ask farmer to arrive on a subsequent day (on FCFS 
basis). 
b. Samples of the harvest will be tested for parameters such as protein content, 
pesticide residue etc.  
3. Smart storage: Harvested (and tested) crop will be next tracked on blockchain from harvest 
to its arrival at Mandi. At the Mandi: 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
305
Page | 54  
 
a. The farmer’s harvest  will be transferred to storage silos. These silos will contain 
harvest of a single farmer, or multiple farmers where th e harvest has similar quality 
profile (similar nutrition profile, and similar fertilizer and pesticide use history).  
b. The silos will be published on the blockchain, and the crop (in silo lots) will be made 
available for procurement. 
4. Procurement: When the crop is published for procurement (where the time of availability for 
procurement and the quality of crop is immutably recorded on blockchain) it will be shown to 
procurement agencies. Here the procurement agencies can be state agencies, central 
agencies and private buyers who will bid for the crop on silo basis. This can be designed as: 
a. State agencies will publish their MSP rate based on crop quality 
b. Central agencies will publish their MSP rate based on crop quality 
c. Private buyers can bid at rates higher than the MSP rate to buy the crop. 
d. Farmer can set minimum acceptable bid price for private buyers with the option to 
negotiate the price. 
5. Payment: Once a bid is successful and procurement is finalized, the payment will be recorded 
on blockchain. Once payment  is transferred, the ownership of silo containing the farmer’s 
harvest will be transferred to the buyer. This will be done through a smart contract such that 
no other intermediaries are required. 
  
5.4.4. Solution Approach – Blockchain in Agriculture 
  
With the objective of creating a solution that is viable and can be scaled across the state, the project 
will proceed in four phases: 
Phase 1 – Definition of detailed project requirements and execution plan.  
A consulting team will work on the projects approved by t he Government and define detailed 
requirements such that the desired project outcome is measurable and scalable. This phase will le ad 
to creation of project reports (with acceptance criteria for pilot success/failure, and execution and 
scale-up plans) and RFPs for on-boarding of suitable SI/vendors. 
Phase 2 – Pilot-1 
Pilot will involve on -boarding of farmers and on -boarding of seed, fertilizer, and pesticide sellers in 
the pilot areas onto the common platform (with blockchain in the data layer). Once operat ions are 
stabilized and tested through two cropping seasons ( kharif & rabi ), the pilot will be assessed to 
understand the accuracy of data being generated to determine if a second pilot is to be attempted or 
not. Data accuracy will be adjudged by comparing  expected harvest parameters (such as expected 
nutrition content based on agricultural inputs: seeds, fertilizers etc.) against the actual/tested harvest 
parameters (parameters based on testing of crop samples). 
Phase 3 – Pilot-2 
If Pilot-2 is to be conduc ted, it will involve on -boarding of select Mandis and on -boarding of buyers 
onto the common platform (with blockchain in the data l ayer). Once operations are stabilized and 
tested through subsequent cropping seasons (kharif & rabi), the pilot will be assessed to understand 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
306
Page | 55  
 
the benefits realized, and a decision will be taken on whether the pilot should be scaled across the 
state of Haryana. This benefit will be judged on: 
1. Efficiency and Disintermediation – Did the pilot remove redundant actors from the 
procurement value chain? 
2. Farmer Impact – Did the pilot augment farmer income based on bidding procurement 
mechanism? And did the pilot p romote agricultural practices that lead to better crop sales 
(such as judicious and targeted use of pesticides and fertilizers)? 
Phase 4 – Scale-Up across state   
Pilots that pass the acceptance criteria will be analyzed by the appointed consulting team an d 
Government agencies (Central and state agencies as applicable) to determine viability of scaling the 
pilots across state. T hese scaled go -live projects may be based on a combination of different pilots 
(such that the scaling project can provide maximum b enefit in terms of better and optimized 
operations) and may consist of multiple stages.  
  
5.4.5. Pilot for Blockchain in Agriculture 
  
Indicative reference architecture for blockchain enabled procurement is shown in the figure below.  
Note that pilot-1 will focus on farmer services and farming inputs. If pilot -2 is to be executed, it will 
expand the work into Mandi and Procurement services. 
 
Figure 23 Indicative architecture for Blockchain based solutions 
  
As shown in the diagram above, the solution can include the following aspects:

1. Data Layer – These are databases and data warehouse structures that store and manage 
factors used for operationalizing the blockchain based solution. 
a. Farmer Data and Profiles (pilot-1) – Farmer and farming databases will carry data such 
as farmer location, farm size, crop type etc. either as a local database on pilot servers, 
or on Govt. of Haryana servers (in portals such as Meri Fasal Mera Byora). 
b. Farming Inputs Database (pilot -1) – Will be databas es that manage data of farming 
inputs such as pesticide/fertilizer and data of the sellers of these inputs. 
c. Mandi Data (pilot-2) – Carries data related to procurement and sales at fasal Mandis. 
This will include data such as Mandi capacity, Silo availability, Mandi labor availability, 
Registered bidders (for procurement), MSP data etc. 
d. Buyer Data (pilot -2) – Carries data of the buyers such  as buyer profiles, planned 
purchase volume, banking information etc. 
2. Blockchain – Blockchain will act as a data management and smart transaction processing layer 
such that applications and modules can be built on top of the facilities provided by this layer. 
This layer will contain features such as: 
a. Verified Crop History (pilot -1) – Crop history based on events such as pur chase of 
seed, purchase of fertilizer/pesticide will be maintained in its immutable nature so 
that: 
i. The data is validated by farmer and seller when the transaction is executed 
ii. The data is available to all permissioned actors on the blockchain platform. 
b. Sampling and test reports (pilot -1) – Data of the tested crop characteristics 
(nutritional content, pesticide residue etc.) will be saved and linked to a farmer’s crop 
data. These tests will be performed by state labs and entered onto the blockchain by 
the transacting parties i.e., the farmer and the testing lab. 
c. Compliance Certifications (pilot-1) – Compliance certifications (if any) received by the 
farmers will be saved on the blockchain by the transacting parties i.e., the farmer and 
the certifying agency. This will include certificates based on tie-ups with procurement 
agencies (private players included) as well as certifications provided by Govt. and 
Semi-Government agencies.  
d. Smart Contracts (pilot -2)– Smart contracts will be set up to provide transaction  
processing without human intervention such as: 
i. Automatic transfer of ownership of silos to buyer upon realization of 
payment. 
ii. Automatic classification of harvest based on crop parameters (such as 
nutritional content, pesticide residue etc.) and grouping o f silos with similar 
contents at the Mandi for auction purposes. 
e. User Account Management – Users will be set up on a permissioned blockc hain 
platform such as IBM’s Hyperledger (as envisioned by DITECH Haryana as per their 
blockchain CoE plans) or similar. Users will be added to pilot-1 and pilot-2 as required. 
f. Certificate Authority – Provides services (to users of blockchain) such as enrol lment 
services, transaction services and triggers, TLS certification etc. 
3. Application Modules – The following processing module groups will be developed 
a. User Onboarding – Users will be set up on the platform such that they can: 
i. Access data from platform 
ii. Buy/Sell/Provide services 
iii. Perform transactions 
iv. Save, process and receive/request verified and certified data 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
308
Page | 57  
 
b. Bid Managemen t and Transaction processing – This module will process all non -
blockchain transactions such as financial transactions, bids, mandi acce ss charges, 
labor payment schedules etc. 
c. Auxiliary Services – These services will integrate with modules required to 
operationalize the blockchain based procurement solution, and will include modules 
and linkages such as integration with government portal, integration with crop testing 
labs etc. 
4. Services Layer: A services layer will be built on top of the computational back end and will 
provide services such as: 
a. Farmer Services – Farmers will be able to access their farm data such as fertilization 
and pestic ide use schedules, crop profiles (nutritional analysis), a history of their 
implemented farm practices and payment management services. 
b. Farming Services – These are services for farming services providers such as seed, 
pesticide, and fertilizer sellers, and will include: 
i. Solution where service providers and farmers will be able to validate each 
other through a smartphone app based QR code. i.e., both farmer and seller 
can scan a QR code on the other party’s mobile app (mobile app to be created 
in the pilot) to identify the other party and authenticate transactions (sale or 
purchase). 
ii. Will include payment services such as linkage to UPI 
iii. Will provide for credit mechanism based on the arrangement between buyer 
and seller (using a smart contract-based payment transaction to be triggered 
on a later date). 
c. Mandi Services – Mandi services will include all serv ices related to workflow and 
capacity management such as silo management, farmer scheduling, 
loading/unloading service scheduling, labor service management etc. 
d. Procurement Services – These services will manage the sale/purchase processes for 
the crop, and  will include features such as bid management, tax management, 
transaction management, payment management etc. 
e. Integration Services – Integrations with platforms such as MFMB will be provided as 
part of integration services. 
  
5.4.6. Scaling-Up of Blockchain in Agriculture pilot   
  
Upon successful completion of pilot 1, the pilot will be evaluated by the consulting and state agencies 
on metrics such as adoption (of the solution) and accuracy (does the crop produce match the practices 
declared on blockchain). 
If pilot-1 is deemed successful, a decision is taken to proceed with pilot-2, pilot-2 will be run by adding 
crop buyers and mandis on the platform, and by proceedi ng with automation of the procurement 
process. Upon completion of pilot 2, it will be evaluated o n metrics such as disintermediation (have 
redundant processes and players been re-allocated) and farmer impact (impact on farmer income and 
farming practices). 
If pilot-2 is also deemed successful with a decision scale across state, the pilot will be made live across 
mandis in Haryana one -by-one (in a sequence and schedule to be decided by the pilot evaluation

5.5. Precision Farming 
Precision Agriculture can be defined as a farming management concept based on the science of 
improving crop yields and assisting decision making using advanced technology equipment/sensors 
and analysis tools. It can be realized through multiple technological interventions using AI/ML and 
drones for better farm management. 
As part of the As-Is Study, NISG has had interactions with officials of the Directorate of Agriculture at 
Krishi Bhavan, Panchkula, District officials of Department of Agriculture, Yamuna Nagar & District 
officials of Department of Agriculture, Karnal. The following observations were made of the curren t 
state of processes related to farm input management in the state of Haryana: 
5.5.1. As-Is Assessment  
1. No major precision farming techniques are followed presently except projects being run by 
state agencies such as MICADA with respect to micro irrigation 
2. Drip irrigation is currently sought on a limited scale for horticultural applications only. 
5.5.2. Recommendation for process improvement 
Usage of drone based localised insecticide spraying and fertiliser application technologies could 
significantly improve the efficie ncy of input usage and lead to higher returns to the farmer for the 
same or better yield. The efficacy of application and consequent yield improvement could also be 
tested during the pilot of major crops during the two seasons in Haryana.  
Pesticide spraying would be operationalised through autonomous drones which are programmed to 
identify the flight path based on the validated inputs from the ML model (developed using 
hyperspectral/multispectral images and consequent ground truthing from experts) 
Fertiliser spray would be operationalised by creating a plan for spray of foliar fertilisers (water soluble) 
and pellet-based fertilisers depending on the soil nutrient profile derived from on ground soil testing 
activities. 
 
Advantages 
1. Reduction in input usage per ha of the sown area 
2. Prevention of health hazards to the farmer due to manual application of insecticides and 
fertilisers 
3. Better soil and local fauna health due to reduced chemical usage 
4. Faster application with little manual interface 
5. Synchronisation with  Drone based  disease monitoring and pest control for effective area 
identification for pesticide application 
 
 
 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
312
Page | 61  
 
5.5.3. Solution Requirements – Precision Farming 
The objectives of this project are two-fold: 
1. To apply pesticide/weedicide/fungicide etc. for infestation/infection events that have been 
detected by ML model developed for pest, weed and disease surveillance (ref section on pilot 
for pest, weed and disease) 
a. Infestation/infection control solutions will be applied through automated interfaces 
such as drones. 
b. Cost benefit analysis will be done that compares cost and efficacy of manual 
application vs. automated application. This analysis will determine if this process 
should be scaled or not.  
2. To carry out initial soil quality testing with respect to t he crop type and  apply fertilizer 
accurately as per precision farming practices. 
a. Based on soil testing and crop type, a plan for fertilizer application will be created with 
type of fertilizer and application intervals. 
b. Required quantities of fertilizer will be appl ied through drone as per the drawn-up 
schedule (water soluble in liquid form and others in pellet form) 
c. Cost benefit analysis will be done based on  
i. crop yield,  
ii. and manual fertilization vs. automated fertilization costs. 
To implement these solutions: 
1. Fields in the pilot will be mapped to a GIS platform (with layers of soil type, crop type, 
topography, field boundaries etc. built into the map). 
2. Soil testing will be done prior to crop sowing such that a fertilization plan can be created (using 
support from Agriculture universities). 
a. Drones will be used for precision application of fertilizer according to the drawn-up 
schedule. 
3. Infestation events will be identified based on predictions from ML model (model from weed, 
pest, and disease pilot), or based on reporting from farmers. 
a. Drones will be used to precisely apply infestation/infection solutions (chemicals). The 
drones may cover areas in multiple fields (depending on the payload capacity) in a 
single flight.  
  
5.5.4. Solution Approach – Precision Farming 
With the objective of creating a solution that is viable and can be scaled across the state, the project 
will proceed in three phases:

Page | 62  
 
 
Figure 24 Phases of solution development and deployment - Precision Farming 
Phase 1 – Definition of detailed project requirements and execution plan.  
A consulting team will work on the projects approved by the government and define detailed 
requirements such that the desired project outcome is measurable and scalable. This phase will lead 
to creation of proje ct reports (with acceptance criteria for pilot success/failure, and execution and 
scale-up plans) and RFPs for on-boarding of suitable SI/vendors. 
Phase 2 – Pilot phase 
Key outcome of this phase will be development of precision farming practices that can o ptimize the 
target agricultural operations. These practices: 
1. Will utilize inputs such as: 
• Soil Quality data 
• GIS map integration with field boundaries (and other layers) 
• Weather data (standard weather services) 
• Infestation/infection data identified by ML algorithms, or by human observation. 
• Data from soil testing  
• Crop type (and variety) data 
• Crop information portals such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’ portal of Govt. of Haryana 
  
2. Will improve precision in application of fertilizer and pesticide/fungicide etc. by: 
· Use of GPS enabled drones to precisely apply the desired solution (fertilizer or 
pesticide/weedicide/fungicide etc.). 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
314
Page | 63  
 
· Automatic triggers for drone-based operation (reducing human dependence) 
· Allowing optimization of procurement (fertilizer or pesticide/ weedicide/fungicide 
etc.) by planning resource (and device/drone) utilization for the coverage area. 
  
3. Will be tested against set acceptance levels (to be finalized in phase 1) such that the solution 
passes cost benefit analysis that factors in: 
· Impact on yield (if any) 
· Cost factors 
· Time factors (time-based precision, and time saved in replacing manual practices) 
  
Phase 3 – Scale-Up across state   
Pilots that pass the acceptance criteria will be analyzed by the appointed NISG consulting team and 
Government agencies (Haryana and central agencies as applicable) to determine viability of scaling 
the pilots across state. These scaled go-live projects may be based on a combination of different pilots 
(such that the scaling project can provide maximum benefit in terms of better and optimized 
operations) and may consist of multiple stages.  
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
315
Page | 64  
 
5.5.5. Indicative solution architecture for pilot 
 Indicative reference architecture for precision farming pilot is shown in the figure below. 
 
Figure 25 Indicative solution for Precision Farming  
As shown in the diagram above, the solution can include the following aspects: 
1. Physical Layer – Physical Layer will rely on drone -based interfaces to collect imaging data as 
well as for precision agricultural operations such as spraying (fertilizer or infestation/infection 
control). 
a. Drones with multi/hyper spectral cameras will capture images of crops to determine 
infection/infestation events (using algorithm for infestation/infection created in pilot 
described in section 5.5.2).  
b. Drones with spraying (spray, stream, and misting) capabilities will be used to precisely 
apply fertilizer / pesticide / weedicide / fungicide etc. to affected crop areas. This will 
prevent overuse of chemicals and fertilizers. 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
316
Page | 65  
 
c. Soil Test Kits and IoT (Ground) Sensors – The data captured from soil test kits and on-
ground IoT sensors will help build and validate soil profile such that fertilization 
schedules and event can be setup. 
2. Middleware Layer – Development of the model will require a middleware layer that provides: 
a. Device Management: Devices including drones will be managed through this module. 
b. Energy Management: Devices will be tracked for energy dependence, energy 
efficiency and energy use. 
c. Data Capture: Data capture interfaces will allow consumption of data generated from 
field devices and will work through a network and encapsulation layer. 
3. Data Layer – These are databases and data warehouse structures that store and manage 
factors used for training the model. 
a. Soil data – Captures data about soil quality through lab testing of areas under pilot. 
b. GIS and pre -defined data – Data for factor s (including field boundaries, soil type, 
topography) that are expected to be static during the pilot are mapped to a GIS 
platform for use in training the algorithm. 
c. Crop database – Captures crop, field, and farmer data (which can be retrieved from 
Haryana Govt. initiatives such as ‘Meri Fasal Mera Byora’) and sets the data as a layer 
on GIS map. 
d. Fertilization Schedule – Captures fertilization needs of the crop based on soil testing 
data and the crop specific needs. This schedule will capture the time and quantity of 
specific fertilizers that need to be applied to the crop until harvesting.  
e. Image processing – Captures and saves image data (and metadata) for use in training 
appropriate algorithms and in verification of precision agriculture interventions.  
f. Infestation/Infection Event Database – Data of verified infestation/infection events is 
used to determine the precision agriculture intervent ion required (and to schedule 
the intervention). 
4. Processing Engines – The processing logic modules that will enable precision agriculture 
a. Schedule Management – Event engine that triggers appropriate modules based on 
input trigger or based on predefined events.  
b. Information Management – Information management system engine that enables all 
farmer, government, and agriculture services. 
c. Big Data Algorithms – Suitable Big data processing algorithm will need to be set up to 
manage and process all incoming data streams. 
d. Predictions Engine – Generates new events that require action from the precision 
agriculture system. The events are sent to schedule management module for 
processing. 
5. Services Layer: A services layer will be built during the pilot to enable precisi on agriculture 
operations. Once the pilot is completed and adjudged successful, the service layer will provide 
its users with features such as: 
a. Farmer Information management system – Farmers will be able to access their farm 
data such as fertilization and pesticide use schedules, soil health data and index, 
expected weather events data, recommended irrigation schedules etc. 
b. Data Management System – Usable and consumable data will be made available to 
farmers and other actors using the system. This data will allow for trend analysis and 
will act as a repository of past actions and schedules. 
c. Prediction and Alert Services – Theses alert services will keep stakeholders appraised 
of their events of interest. For instance, farmers will be notified of upcoming sur vey 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
317
Page | 66  
 
or spray operations, agriculture services providers will be appraised of the demand of 
specific agricultural consumables (such as fertilizer) in their areas of operation. 
  
5.5.6. Indicative solution architecture for Scaling pilot  
Upon successful completion of pilot, the pilot can be scaled across Haryana state by repli cating the 
model across different blocks in the state.  
1. Analysis of the pilot data will determine the applications where automation developed in this 
pilot project provide a net gain based on cost benefit analysis (for example certain operations 
may only be suitable to farms of a minimum/maximum size) 
2. Training and sensitization of farmers new to the system will include training on the platform, 
and presentation of the cost benefit analysis. 
3. New Farmers can be brought on board through a scale up and replication plan. 
4. The platform and code developed for pilot will be installed at a data center (secure data center 
with scaling capabilities). 
 
5.5.7. Functionality expected after rollout 
1. Farmer 
a. Monitoring efficiency of use of fertiliser and pesticide by applied quantity/kg/ha 
b. Guidelines & Recommendation for use of pesticides and fertilisers 
c. Alerts regarding crop status 
d. Recommended inputs for the sown crop 
e. Real time synchronisation 
2. State/District Department officials 
a. Interface for issuing advisories to farmers in local language 
b. Spot checking /surveying 
c. Access to drone survey report 
d. Mobile application for ground truthing

7. Unified Architecture Envisioned 
  
All pilots under this project are designed such that they can be combined under a unified 
architecture. This approach towards architectural design will ensure that different successful pilots 
can use common hardware, computing and services layers leading to: 
1. Lower costs: since the project elements do not need to be setup and maintained separately. 
2. Better user experience: since user can access features for all pilots on a common interface. 
An example of such an architecture (to be finalized in consulting phase of project) is given below. 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
320
Page | 69  
 
 
Figure 26 Unified architecture for agriculture pilots 
8. Project Implementation approach & timeline  
Four blocks would be selected for the conduct of the pilot. The selectio n of the same would be done 
in ag reement with the Department. The department would be responsible for the provision of 
administrative clearances for ex. clearances for flying drones, clearance for usage of cadastral maps 
from the revenue dept., clearances,  support, and orders required for  the conduct of pilot/CCEs on 
non-governmental farms etc.  
Two cropping cycles are intended to be covered in the pilot. Here Rice/Paddy crop would be assessed 
in the Kharif season (Jun -Oct) and wheat crop would be assessed in the Rabi cropping season (Nov -
Apr). The pilot will run for 15 months (01 Apr 2022 – 30 Jun 2023).

9. Rollout/scale-up plan 
Roll out plan to be finalised based on evaluation of the pilot in terms of accuracy and the cost benefit 
analysis with respect to existing methodologies. 
Annexures 
Annex I 
Random sampling method for field identification 
Random Stratified sampling to select the district and the plot for carrying out the Crop Cutting 
Experiment (CCE) 
Selection tables are provided to the district by the NSSO to select the field (Column A) and the plot 
(Column B) for carrying out CCE. 4 CCEs need to be carried out for every crop in every gram panchayat. 
Hence total no. of CCEs in the district become very large which is a huge logistical and operational 
exercise during the end of the season (both Kharif and Rabi). 
For a given district the column no. to be referred (from the random table) is provided in the selection 
table provided by NSSO. The first number in the column (let’s say 6310) is then noted down. To identify 
the field the patwaris Khasra book/Sijra/register is taken and Killa no. 6310 is identified. If no. of Killas 
is less than the noted number, then the noted number is divided by the total no. of Killas, and 
remainder is calculated. The Killa /filed no. corresponding to the remainder nu mber is then selected 
for the CCE. Normally the size of the field is taken as 80 x 72 footsteps (roughly 1 acre) 
750477/2022/O/o US(IT)
322
Page | 71  
 
 
Annex II 
Random Sampling method for plot identification (applicable to both SST and Randomly selected 
field) 
Starting from the SW corner of the field and depending on the size of the farm another table is referred 
(separate for single, double- & triple-digit farm lengths). Then based on the selection table B provided 
by NSSO column no. in the random table is iden tified. For locating the length w ise point, the first 
number in the list greater than 80 -7=73 (footsteps) is selected. Similarly, along the width a similar 
point is selected (no. in column greater than 72-7 = 65 footsteps). 7 footsteps are reduced from overall 
field length and width becau se it is roughly equal to 5m. Hence a pair is identified from the random 
table to identify coordinates for CCE plot. Form this coordinate e.g. (72 footsteps east and 21 footsteps 
north) a plot is marked extending 5 m in the  north and 5m in the east. This a rea is measured and 
cordoned off with sticks and tape.

analysis.overview: Digital Agriculture Mission aims to establish a comprehensive farmer-centric digital and space-tech ecosystem to enhance transparency and efficiency in the execution of government programs while providing crucial support for evidence-based policy making for farmers’ welfare. It seeks to create a robust and holistic farmer-centric digital ecosystem in the country, improve transparency and efficiency in the implementation of government initiatives, support the government in making informed policy decisions, and promote public and private innovation and partnerships, encouraging Agri techs in India’s digital economy. The mission includes key components such as Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) for Agriculture (Agristack, Krishi Decision Support System, Comprehensive Soil Fertility, and Profile Maps), Digital General Crop Estimation Survey (DGCES), support to Mahalanobis National Crop Forecasting Centre (MNCFC), and activities under the erstwhile NeGP-A scheme.
objectives: To create a robust and holistic farmer-centric digital ecosystem in the country
To improve transparency and efficiency in the implementation of government initiatives
To support the government in making informed policy decisions
To promote public and private innovation and partnerships, encouraging Agri techs in India’s digital economy
analysis.eligibility: States/UTs are eligible to submit proposals under the Digital Agriculture Mission, particularly for projects involving Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) for Agriculture, Digital General Crop Estimation Survey (DGCES), support to Mahalanobis National Crop Forecasting Centre (MNCFC), and IT initiatives for States, Academic & Research Institutions under the Mission. Proposals must include DPR, financial implications, clear timelines, and technology involved, and should preferably be submitted in the first quarter but not later than the second quarter of the Financial Year.
analysis.benefits: The mission provides support for evidence-based policy making, enhances transparency and efficiency in government program execution, promotes public-private partnerships in the agri-tech sector, enables real-time crop survey data collection through Digital Crop Survey (DCS), supports soil resource mapping at 1:10,000 scale, facilitates automated General Crop Estimation Survey (GCES) processes, and strengthens crop forecasting and yield estimation through integration with MNCFC. It also supports IT initiatives for states and academic institutions to develop scalable digital solutions using emerging technologies.
analysis.financial_support: Funds under the Digital Agriculture Mission are released to States/UTs in 4 equal tranches as per the latest guidelines of the Department of Expenditure (DoE), subject to submission of satisfactory status reports via PFMS. The mission provides financial assistance for Digital Crop Survey (DCS) pilots, manpower deployment for DCS/Help Desk/PMU, procurement of DCS-related infrastructure, and utilization of existing state funds for DCS activities. Academic and research institutes may receive 100% funds for building scalable digital solutions using advanced technologies aligned with Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) in Agriculture, subject to recommendation by a High-level Evaluation Committee.
analysis.application_process: 1. States/UTs should submit proposals under NeGP-A preferably in the first quarter but not later than the second quarter of the Financial Year. 2. The proposal must contain DPR, financial implication, clear timelines, and technology involved. 3. Proposals are examined based on use of latest technology, innovation, scalability, success, and viability of the proposed solution before proposing for pilot. 4. The States/UTs are advised to come up with proposals whose life-cycle from conception to implementation is preferably for one year with clear timelines of implementation. 5. Funds to the States/UTs are released in 4 equal tranches as per DoE guidelines, subject to submission of satisfactory status reports via PFMS. 6. Next installment is released after receipt of satisfactory status report from the States/UTs. 7. Pilot projects requiring basic infrastructure (hardware & manpower) have such costs borne by the concerned state. 8. The Centre has co-ownership of intellectual property created during the pilot, requiring States/UTs to share details and outcomes with the Centre. 9. States/UTs are required to share Cost-Benefit analysis with the Central Government as part of feedback project monitoring. 10. The sanctioned project must be completed within the stipulated time, extendable by up to one year at a time with prior approval, up to a maximum of two years; unspent amount must be surrendered if not completed in the extended year.
analysis.deadlines: Proposals should be submitted preferably in the first quarter but not later than the second quarter of the Financial Year.
analysis.implementing_agency: Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare (DA&FW), Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare
analysis.contact_details: Email: us-it@nic.in | Phone: 011-23382926 (Under Secretary, IT Division)
caveats: Pilot projects requiring basic infrastructure (hardware & manpower) have such costs borne by the concerned state.
The Centre has co-ownership of intellectual property created during the pilot; States/UTs must share details and outcomes with the Centre.
States/UTs must share Cost-Benefit analysis with the Central Government for feedback and project monitoring.
The project must be completed within the stipulated time; extension up to one year at a time is allowed with prior approval, up to a maximum of two years.
If the project is not completed in the extended year, the State/UT must surrender the unspent amount.
New proposals are considered only after confirmation from PAO on receipt of refund amount from previous unspent funds.
